content_sections.I Introduction: Large-momentum effective theory (LaMET) is an approach to calculate parton physics through large-momentum expansion of Euclidean observables, such as momentum distributions and static correlations calculable in lattice quantum chromodynamics (QCD)citebib.bib1, bib.bib2. It has a wide range of applications, including quark isovector distribution functionscitebib.bib3, bib.bib4, bib.bib5, bib.bib6, bib.bib7, bib.bib8, bib.bib9, bib.bib10, bib.bib11, bib.bib12, bib.bib13, bib.bib14, bib.bib15, bib.bib16, bib.bib17, bib.bib18, bib.bib19, bib.bib20, generalized parton distributionscitebib.bib21, bib.bib22, bib.bib23, bib.bib24, bib.bib25, bib.bib26, distribution amplitudes (DAs)citebib.bib27, bib.bib28, bib.bib29, bib.bib30, bib.bib31, and transverse-momentum-dependent distributionscitebib.bib32, bib.bib33, bib.bib34, bib.bib35. Recent reviews on LaMET can be found in Refs. citebib.bib36, bib.bib37. For collinear PDFs, the LaMET expansion starts from a quasi-PDF matrix element, which is motivated from the spatial correlator defining the momentum distribution in a many-body system: hlat‚Äč(z,a,Pz)=‚ü®Pz|Oőď‚Äč(z)|Pz‚ü©,superscript‚ĄélatūĚĎßūĚĎésubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßquantum-operator-productsubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßsubscriptūĚĎāőďūĚĎßsubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßdisplaystyletildehrm lat(z,a,Pz)= Pz|OGamma(z)|Pz, where |Pz‚ü©ketsubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß|Pz is the hadron state with a large momentum PzsubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßPz along zūĚĎßz direction. Oőď‚Äč(z)subscriptūĚĎāőďūĚĎßOGamma(z) is the quasi-PDF operator: Oőď‚Äč(z)=Ōą¬Į‚Äč(z)‚Äčőď‚ÄčU‚Äč(z,0)‚ÄčŌą‚Äč,subscriptūĚĎāőďūĚĎß¬ĮūĚúďūĚĎßőďūĚĎąūĚĎß0ūĚúď0displaystyle OGamma(z)=barpsi(z)Gamma U(z,0)psi, where ŌąūĚúďpsi, Ōą¬Į¬ĮūĚúďbarpsi denote the bare quark field, őďőďGamma is a Dirac structure, and U‚Äč(z,0)=P‚Äčexp‚Ā°(‚ąíi‚Äčg‚Äč‚ąę0zūĚĎĎz‚Ä≤‚ÄčAz‚Äč(z‚Ä≤))ūĚĎąūĚĎß0ūĚĎÉūĚĎĖūĚĎĒsuperscriptsubscript0ūĚĎßdifferential-dsuperscriptūĚĎß‚Ä≤subscriptūĚźīūĚĎßsuperscriptūĚĎß‚Ä≤U(z,0)=Pexp(-igint0zdzprimeAz(zprime)) is the Wilson gauge-link along the direction zūĚĎßz, where AőľsuperscriptūĚźīūĚúáAmu is the gluon gauge potential. Apart from the correlator above, there are other choices of Euclidean correlators that can be used to probe the same lightcone physics in the PDFs through large-momentum expansioncitebib.bib37. They form a universality class. In the large momentum limit, the quasi-PDF or longitudinal momentum distribution can be converted to the standard PDF through a perturbative matchingcitebib.bib37. Such a matching involves logarithms associated with the hadron momentum PzsubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßPz and the renormalization scale őľūĚúámu. Since PzsubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßPz and őľūĚúámu are independent scales, their ratio can be large, generating large logarithms that need to be resummed to obtain a more reliable result. It is the purpose of this work to study the effects of these potentially large logarithms and their resummation through standard renormalization group equation (renormalization group resummation or RGR). One may wonder whether resummation is necessary in present lattice calculations where Pz‚ąľőľ‚ąľ2similar-tosubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßūĚúásimilar-to2Pzsimmusim 2GeV. However, as we will argue, the physical scale associated with the quasi-PDF is actually the quark or gluon longitudinal momentum, pz=x‚ÄčPzsubscriptūĚĎĚūĚĎßūĚĎ•subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßpz=xPz with xūĚĎ•x being the momentum fraction. It can be much smaller than őľūĚúámu if xūĚĎ•x is small. After resummation, the intrinsic scale ‚ąľx‚ÄčPzsimilar-toabsentūĚĎ•subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßsim xPz appears in the running coupling constant őĪs(‚ąľx‚ÄčPz)annotatedsubscriptūĚõľūĚĎ†similar-toabsentūĚĎ•subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßalphas(sim xPz) and the perturbation series becomes a more physical expansion. But at small xūĚĎ•x, őĪs(‚ąľx‚ÄčPz)annotatedsubscriptūĚõľūĚĎ†similar-toabsentūĚĎ•subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßalphas(sim xPz) becomes very large and the perturbative matching breaks down. The physics is that if the quark/gluon longitudinal momentum pzsubscriptūĚĎĚūĚĎßpz is too small or comparable to the intrinsic transverse momentum, it is no longer collinear with the hadron momentum so the factorization to infinite-momentum-frame parton physics breaks down. Thus, we expect a large discrepancy between RGR and fixed-order matching at small xūĚĎ•x. Moreover, we can compare resummations up to next-to-leading order (NLO) or next-to-next-to-leading order (NNLO) to check the convergence of perturbative expansions. The above resummation has a correspondence in the coordinate space expansioncitebib.bib38. When the quasi-PDF correlator is expanded in terms of moments of PDFs at short distancescitebib.bib39, bib.bib40, bib.bib41, the natural scale is the correlator distance zūĚĎßz. This physical scale is recovered in the Wilson coefficients again through RGR. Operationally, ‚ąľ1/zsimilar-toabsent1ūĚĎßsim 1/z shows up as an intrinsic scale for the running coupling constant őĪs(‚ąľ1/z)annotatedsubscriptūĚõľūĚĎ†similar-toabsent1ūĚĎßalphas(sim 1/z). At large distances, őĪs(‚ąľ1/z)annotatedsubscriptūĚõľūĚĎ†similar-toabsent1ūĚĎßalphas(sim 1/z) is too large, and the perturbation series breaks down. Only the correlation data at z‚Č™1/őõQCDmuch-less-thanūĚĎß1subscriptőõQCDzll 1/Lambdarm QCD can be used to extract the PDF moments. Therefore, we expect a very different conclusion about the moments fitting from those done with fixed-order perturbation theorycitebib.bib42, bib.bib43. Through applying RGR to the LaMET matching, our goal is to improve the accuracy of the fixed-order matching at intermediate xūĚĎ•x, and at the same time, to exhibit the limitation of perturbation theory at small xūĚĎ•x. We demonstrate the approach by using the lattice QCD matrix elements for pion valence PDF at lattice spacing aūĚĎéa= 0.04 fm and aūĚĎéa= 0.06 fm and momentum PzsubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßPz= 0 ‚ąľsimilar-tosim 2.4 GeV calculated by BNL/ANL collaborationcitebib.bib43, bib.bib44, bib.bib19. Through a similar resummation in coordinate space, we also exhibit the large perturbative errors of Wilson coefficients at large distances, causing a large uncertainty in fitting ‚ü®x4‚ü©delimited-‚ü®‚ü©superscriptūĚĎ•4 x4 in the OPE approach. The rest of this paper is organized as follows. In Sec. , we present the formalism of renormalization group resummation in LaMET matching. In Sec. , we apply the RGR matching to pion valence PDF and compare it with the fixed-order result. In Sec. , we study RGR effects in fitting the moments of PDFs in the OPE approach using the same data. We summarize our results in Sec. .
content_sections.II Resumming quark‚Äôs longitudinal mome: In this section, we present the formalism for resumming quark‚Äôs longitudinal momentum logarithms through the renormalization group in LaMET matching. We start by renormalizing the lattice QCD matrix element hlat‚Äč(z,a,Pz)=‚ü®Pz|Ōą¬Į‚Äč(z)‚Äčő≥t‚ÄčU‚Äč(z,0)‚ÄčŌą‚Äč|Pz‚ü©superscript‚ĄélatūĚĎßūĚĎésubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßquantum-operator-productsubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß¬ĮūĚúďūĚĎßsuperscriptūĚõĺūĚĎ°ūĚĎąūĚĎß0ūĚúď0subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßtildehrm lat(z,a,Pz)= Pz|barpsi(z)gammatU(z,0)psi|Pz in the hybrid schemecitebib.bib45: h‚Äč(z,Pz)=hlat‚Äč(z,a,Pz)hlat‚Äč(z,a,0)‚Äčőł‚Äč(zs‚ąí|z|)‚ĄéūĚĎßsubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßsuperscript‚ĄélatūĚĎßūĚĎésubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßsuperscript‚ĄélatūĚĎßūĚĎé0ūĚúÉsubscriptūĚĎßūĚĎ†ūĚĎßdisplaystyletildeh(z,Pz)=fractildehrm lat(z,a,Pz)tildehrm lat(z,a,0)theta(zs-|z|) +hlat‚Äč(z,a,Pz)ZR‚Äč(z,a,őľ)‚ÄčhMS¬Į‚Äč(zs,őľ,0)‚Äčőł‚Äč(|z|‚ąízs),superscript‚ĄélatūĚĎßūĚĎésubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßsuperscriptūĚĎćūĚĎÖūĚĎßūĚĎéūĚúásuperscript‚Ąé¬ĮMSsubscriptūĚĎßūĚĎ†ūĚúá0ūĚúÉūĚĎßsubscriptūĚĎßūĚĎ†displaystyle+fractildehrm lat(z,a,Pz)ZR(z,a,mu)tildehoverlinerm MS(zs,mu,0)theta(|z|-zs), where zssubscriptūĚĎßūĚĎ†zs is the hybrid scheme parameter which satisfies a‚Č™zs‚Č™1/őõQCDmuch-less-thanūĚĎésubscriptūĚĎßūĚĎ†much-less-than1subscriptőõQCDall zsll 1/Lambdarm QCD to guarantee perturbation theory works when z<zsūĚĎßsubscriptūĚĎßūĚĎ†z<zs. ZR‚Äč(z,a,őľ)superscriptūĚĎćūĚĎÖūĚĎßūĚĎéūĚúáZR(z,a,mu) is the phenomenological renormalization factor to eliminate linear and logarithmic divergences based on the self-renormalization methodcitebib.bib46. It also contains the mass renormalization counterterm and scheme conversion factor to convert the lattice QCD matrix element to the MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinerm MS scheme. Therefore, we should have the following relation at short distances: hlat‚Äč(z,a,0)ZR‚Äč(z,a,őľ)=hMS¬Į‚Äč(z,őľ,0),superscript‚ĄélatūĚĎßūĚĎé0superscriptūĚĎćūĚĎÖūĚĎßūĚĎéūĚúásuperscript‚Ąé¬ĮMSūĚĎßūĚúá0fractildehrm lat(z,a,0)ZR(z,a,mu)=tildehoverlinerm MS(z,mu,0), where z‚Č™1/őõQCDmuch-less-thanūĚĎß1subscriptőõQCDzll 1/Lambdarm QCD. hMS¬Į‚Äč(z,őľ,0)superscript‚Ąé¬ĮMSūĚĎßūĚúá0tildehoverlinerm MS(z,mu,0) is the zero momentum matrix element in the MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinerm MS scheme, which is perturbatively calculated up to NNLO in the literaturecitebib.bib47, see Eq. . We introduce hMS¬Į‚Äč(zs,őľ,0)superscript‚Ąé¬ĮMSsubscriptūĚĎßūĚĎ†ūĚúá0tildehoverlinerm MS(zs,mu,0) in the denominator at large distances in Eq. to guarantee that h‚Äč(z,Pz)‚ĄéūĚĎßsubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßtildeh(z,Pz) is continuous, although the derivative needs not be. An important feature of the hybrid renormalization is that at short distances, the ratio of the lattice matrix elements has the proper normalization at z=0ūĚĎß0z=0 with some of the discretization effects being canceled. While at large distances, no undesired non-perturbative effects are introduced. Fourier transforming the hybrid renormalized matrix element: f‚Äč(x,Pz)=Pz‚Äč‚ąę‚ąí‚ąě+‚ąěd‚Äčz2‚ÄčŌÄ‚Äčei‚Äčx‚ÄčPz‚Äčz‚Äčh‚Äč(z,Pz),ūĚĎďūĚĎ•subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßsubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßsuperscriptsubscriptūĚĎĎūĚĎß2ūĚúčsuperscriptūĚĎíūĚĎĖūĚĎ•subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßūĚĎß‚ĄéūĚĎßsubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßdisplaystyletildef(x,Pz)=Pzint-infty+inftyfracdz2pieixPzztildeh(z,Pz), we obtain the quasi-PDF f‚Äč(x,Pz)ūĚĎďūĚĎ•subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßtildef(x,Pz) in the hybrid scheme, whose factorization in momentum space is: f‚Äč(x,Pz)ūĚĎďūĚĎ•subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßdisplaystyletildef(x,Pz) =‚ąę‚ąí11d‚Äčy|y|‚ÄčC‚Äč(xy,őľ|x|‚ÄčPz)‚Äčf‚Äč(y,őľ)absentsuperscriptsubscript11ūĚĎĎūĚĎ¶ūĚĎ¶ūĚź∂ūĚĎ•ūĚĎ¶ūĚúáūĚĎ•subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßūĚĎďūĚĎ¶ūĚúádisplaystyle=int-11fracdyleft|yright|Cleft(fracxy,fracmu|x|Pzright)f(y,mu) where f‚Äč(y,őľ)ūĚĎďūĚĎ¶ūĚúáf(y,mu) is the light-cone PDF and C‚Äč(xy,őľ|x|‚ÄčPz)ūĚź∂ūĚĎ•ūĚĎ¶ūĚúáūĚĎ•subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßCleft(fracxy,fracmu|x|Pzright) is the perturbative matching kernel, which has been calculated up to NNLO in the MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinerm MS scheme for the isovector combinationcitebib.bib47, bib.bib48. Based on their calculations, we present the NLO and NNLO hybrid scheme matching kernels in Eqs. and in Appendix. Note that we write one of the arguments as őľ|x|‚ÄčPzūĚúáūĚĎ•subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßfracmu|x|Pz instead of őľ|y|‚ÄčPzūĚúáūĚĎ¶subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßfracmu|y|Pz because we should have the momentum of the quasi parton |x|‚ÄčPzūĚĎ•subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß|x|Pz in the matching kernelcitebib.bib41. The standard QCD factorization formula for one of the unpolarized DIS structure functions F2subscriptūĚźĻ2F2 is as followscitebib.bib49: F2‚Äč(Q,xB)=subscriptūĚźĻ2ūĚĎĄsubscriptūĚĎ•ūĚźĶabsentdisplaystyle F2(Q,xB)= ‚ąęxB‚ąí1+dx‚ÄčC2‚Äč(Qőľ,xBx)‚Äčf‚Äč(x,őľ)superscriptsubscriptlimit-fromsubscriptūĚĎ•ūĚźĶlimit-from1differential-dūĚĎ•subscriptūĚź∂2ūĚĎĄūĚúásubscriptūĚĎ•ūĚźĶūĚĎ•ūĚĎďūĚĎ•ūĚúádisplaystyleintxB-1+dxhatC2left(fracQmu,fracxBxright)f(x,mu) where the summation over quark flavor and gluon has been omitted for simplicity, Q2=‚ąíq2superscriptūĚĎĄ2superscriptūĚĎě2Q2=-q2 is the virtuality of the photon and xB=Q22‚ÄčP‚čÖqsubscriptūĚĎ•ūĚźĶsuperscriptūĚĎĄ2‚čÖ2ūĚĎÉūĚĎěxB=fracQ22Pcdot q is the Bjorken variable. f‚Äč(x,őľ)ūĚĎďūĚĎ•ūĚúáf(x,mu) is the light-cone PDF and C2‚Äč(Q/őľ,xB/x)subscriptūĚź∂2ūĚĎĄūĚúásubscriptūĚĎ•ūĚźĶūĚĎ•hatC2left(Q/mu,xB/xright) is the coefficient function that matches the light cone PDF to the structure function, which was calculated up to NNLOcitebib.bib50, bib.bib51 and N3‚ÄčLOsuperscriptN3LOrm N3LOcitebib.bib52 in literature. There are logarithmic terms ‚ąľln‚Ā°(őľ2Q2)similar-toabsentsuperscriptūĚúá2superscriptūĚĎĄ2simln(fracmu2Q2) in the above formula and the higher-order coefficient functions. The scale őľūĚúámu dependence of these log terms in C2subscriptūĚź∂2hatC2 cancels the scale őľūĚúámu dependence of the light cone PDF and the QCD running coupling so that the structure function F2subscriptūĚźĻ2F2 is scale őľūĚúámu independent. For example, the leading log term őĪs‚ÄčCF2‚ÄčŌÄ‚Äčz‚Äč1+z21‚ąíz‚Äč(‚ąíln‚Ā°[őľ2Q2])subscriptūĚõľūĚĎ†subscriptūĚź∂ūĚźĻ2ūĚúčūĚĎß1superscriptūĚĎß21ūĚĎßsuperscriptūĚúá2superscriptūĚĎĄ2fracalphasCF2pizfrac1+z21-zleft(-lnleft[fracmu2Q2right]right) in the NLO result is correspondent to the leading order Dokshitzer‚ÄďGribov‚ÄďLipatov‚ÄďAltarelli‚ÄďParisi (DGLAP) evolution. These log terms are large if őľūĚúámu differs from QūĚĎĄQ by orders of magnitude. To resolve this issue, it is natural to choose őľ=QūĚúáūĚĎĄmu=Q in the quark coefficient function and extract PDFs at certain scales through DGLAP evolution. The factorization formula Eq. is Lorentz invariant and we consider a specific reference (Bjorken or Breit) frame for the purposes of understanding the physical scale. In this frame, the energy of virtual photon is zero and the momentum distribution of the partons is measured (see Fig. ): qőľ=(0,0,0,‚ąí2‚Äčx‚ÄčPz)superscriptūĚĎěūĚúá0002ūĚĎ•subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßdisplaystyle qmu=(0,0,0,-2xPz) pőľ=(x‚ÄčPz,0,0,x‚ÄčPz)superscriptūĚĎĚūĚúáūĚĎ•subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß00ūĚĎ•subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßdisplaystyle pmu=(xPz,0,0,xPz) (p+q)őľ=(x‚ÄčPz,0,0,‚ąíx‚ÄčPz). superscriptūĚĎĚūĚĎěūĚúáūĚĎ•subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß00ūĚĎ•subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßdisplaystyle(p+q)mu=(xPz,0,0,-xPz). Here we have rewritten the photon momentum in terms of the momentum of the struck parton. It is now clear that the virtuality of the photon is Q2=‚ąíq2=(2‚Äčx‚ÄčPz)2superscriptūĚĎĄ2superscriptūĚĎě2superscript2ūĚĎ•subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß2Q2=-q2=(2xPz)2. Although the above kinematics is for tree level scattering, Q2=(2‚ÄčxB‚ÄčPz)2superscriptūĚĎĄ2superscript2subscriptūĚĎ•ūĚźĶsubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß2Q2=(2xBPz)2 can be seen from the definition of the Bjorken variable xB=Q22‚ÄčP‚čÖqsubscriptūĚĎ•ūĚźĶsuperscriptūĚĎĄ2‚čÖ2ūĚĎÉūĚĎěxB=fracQ22Pcdot q in this frame where Pőľ=(Pz,0,0,Pz)superscriptūĚĎÉūĚúásubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß00subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßPmu=(Pz,0,0,Pz) and qőľ=(0,0,0,‚ąí2‚ÄčxB‚ÄčPz)superscriptūĚĎěūĚúá0002subscriptūĚĎ•ūĚźĶsubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßqmu=(0,0,0,-2xBPz). In Bjorken frame, it is natural to calculate the structure function in terms of the quasi-PDF, rather than the light-cone PDF. One can then recover the above standard DIS QCD factorization by matching quasi-PDF further to light-cone one as in Eq. . This new approach of using quasi-PDF may help us understand the origin of its physical scale, F2‚Äč(Q,xB)=C2‚Äč(Qőľ,xBx)‚äóf‚Äč(x,Pz)subscriptūĚźĻ2ūĚĎĄsubscriptūĚĎ•ūĚźĶtensor-productsubscriptūĚź∂2ūĚĎĄūĚúásubscriptūĚĎ•ūĚźĶūĚĎ•ūĚĎďūĚĎ•subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßdisplaystyle F2(Q,xB)=hattildeC2left(fracQmu,fracxBxright)otimestildef(x,Pz) where C2‚Äč(Qőľ,xBx)subscriptūĚź∂2ūĚĎĄūĚúásubscriptūĚĎ•ūĚźĶūĚĎ•hattildeC2left(fracQmu,fracxBxright) is the quasi DIS coefficient function that matches the quasi-PDF to the structure function. The DGLAP logs on both sides of this equation should match. We need to resum this type of large logarithms ‚ąľln‚Ā°(őľ24‚Äčx2‚ÄčPz2)similar-toabsentsuperscriptūĚúá24superscriptūĚĎ•2superscriptsubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß2simlnleft(fracmu24x2Pz2right) to all orders in perturbation theory to recover the small-xūĚĎ•x behavior in the LaMET calculations. The main result is that the natural scale for the perturbative expansion is 2‚Äčx‚ÄčPz2ūĚĎ•subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß2xPz, which appears in the coupling őĪs‚Äč(2‚Äčx‚ÄčPz)subscriptūĚõľūĚĎ†2ūĚĎ•subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßalphas(2xPz). In the factorization formula Eq. , part of the higher twist effects behave like ūĚí™‚Äč[őõQCD2x2‚ÄčPz2]ūĚí™delimited-superscriptsubscriptőõQCD2superscriptūĚĎ•2superscriptsubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß2cal Oleft[fracLambdarm QCD2x2Pz2right], which is also large for small xūĚĎ•x. The small-momentum large logarithms can be resummed using the standard renormalization group method. We can vary c‚Ä≤superscriptūĚĎź‚Ä≤cprime around 1 to test the stability of perturbative matching. The resummation scale is an order-of-magnitude estimate based on the physical scale and can vary without having meaningful physics impact, hence the parameter c‚Ä≤superscriptūĚĎź‚Ä≤cprime. Different choices of resummation scale (c‚Ä≤superscriptūĚĎź‚Ä≤cprime) correspond to higher order effects in őĪssubscriptūĚõľūĚĎ†alphas, which disappear when the perturbation series are computed to the infinite order. Then we just need to DGLAP evolve f‚Äč(x,Qeff)ūĚĎďūĚĎ•subscriptūĚĎĄefff(x,Qrm eff) to scale őľūĚúámu. content_sections.III Resummation Effect in Pion PDF Calcu: As an example, we study the effect of resumming the small-momentum large logarithms in the pion valence PDF calculation. The resummation improves the lattice QCD prediction in the 2‚Äčx‚ÄčPz2ūĚĎ•subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß2xPz range 0.7 GeV to 1.5 GeV, but generates a very large uncertainty in the smaller-xūĚĎ•x region.

Lattice QCD matrix elements for the valence parton distribution of a pion at lattice spacing aūĚĎéa= 0.04 fm and aūĚĎéa= 0.06 fm and momentum PzsubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßPz= 0 ‚ąľsimilar-tosim 2.4 GeV are calculated by the BNL/ANL collaboration. These data have been analyzed in recent BNL/ANL paperscitebib.bib43, bib.bib44, bib.bib19. A detailed explanation of their data can be found in Ref. citebib.bib43. The matrix elements are calculated on two different ensembles Lt√óL3subscriptūĚźŅūĚĎ°superscriptūĚźŅ3Lttimes L3: One ensemble has lattice spacing a=0.06ūĚĎé0.06a=0.06fm and lattice volume 48√ó64348superscript64348times 643, and the other one has lattice spacing a=0.04ūĚĎé0.04a=0.04fm and lattice volume 64√ó64364superscript64364times 643. In this section, we perform RGR matching based on the method in Appendix. The RGR matched PDFs for Pz=1.9subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß1.9Pz=1.9GeV are shown in Fig. , in comparison with fixed-order matched PDFs. The reason is that the physical scale 2‚Äčx‚ÄčPz2ūĚĎ•subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß2xPz for Pz=1.9subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß1.9Pz=1.9GeV at large xūĚĎ•x region is close to the PDF scale őľ=2ūĚúá2mu=2GeV we choose in the matching, where the resummed logs are not very large. However, if we have much larger momenta (e. g. Pz=10‚ÄčGeVsubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß10GeVPz=10rm GeV), we would expect to observe the resummation effects in the large xūĚĎ•x region. In the moderate 2‚Äčx‚ÄčPz2ūĚĎ•subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß2xPz region (e. g. 1.5‚ÄčGeV<2‚Äčx‚ÄčPz<2.7‚ÄčGeV1.5GeV2ūĚĎ•subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß2.7GeV1.5rm GeV<2xPz<2.7rm GeV), RGR matched and fixed-order matched PDFs are consistent with each other, which indicates that fixed-order perturbative matching works well. In the intermediate region (e. g. 0.7‚ÄčGeV<2‚Äčx‚ÄčPz<1.5‚ÄčGeV0.7GeV2ūĚĎ•subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß1.5GeV0.7rm GeV<2xPz<1.5rm GeV), resummation effects start to become important and improve the accuracy of the theoretical prediction. At small 2‚Äčx‚ÄčPz2ūĚĎ•subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß2xPz (e. g. 2‚Äčx‚ÄčPz<0.7‚ÄčGeV2ūĚĎ•subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß0.7GeV2xPz<0.7rm GeV), the effective coupling őĪs‚Äč(2‚Äčx‚ÄčPz)subscriptūĚõľūĚĎ†2ūĚĎ•subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßalphas(2xPz) becomes too large to be perturbative, and there are large discrepancies between RGR matched and fixed-order matched PDFs. There is also a large discrepancy between the NLO+RGR PDF and the NNLO+RGR PDF, indicating higher-order effects are not negligible. Therefore, the resummation of the large logarithms makes clear the breakdown of the perturbative matching at small 2‚Äčx‚ÄčPz2ūĚĎ•subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß2xPz (e. g. 2‚Äčx‚ÄčPz<0.7‚ÄčGeV2ūĚĎ•subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß0.7GeV2xPz<0.7rm GeV), where higher-twist effects are also unmanageable. So with the availability of larger PzsubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßPz we will be able to reliably extract light-cone PDFs at smaller xūĚĎ•x. content_sections.IV Resummed Coefficients in Operator Pro: The connection between the Euclidean correlator in Eq. and PDF has also been explored through coordinate-space operator product expansion (OPE)citebib.bib39, bib.bib38, bib.bib41. With a finite-range coordinate-space correlation calculated from lattice QCD, it has been proposed to extract moments of PDFs through fitting the truncated OPE expressioncitebib.bib56, bib.bib18, bib.bib43. To extract the moments reliably, one has to properly resum large logarithms of type őĪn‚Äč(őľ)‚Äčlnn‚Ā°(z‚Äčőľ)superscriptūĚõľūĚĎõūĚúásuperscriptūĚĎõūĚĎßūĚúáalphan(mu)lnn(zmu) in the Wilson coefficientcitebib.bib44. Once this is done, it is expected that OPE works only at perturbatively-small distances z‚Č™1/őõQCDmuch-less-thanūĚĎß1subscriptőõQCDzll 1/Lambdarm QCD up to 0.2‚ąľ0.3similar-to0.20.30.2sim 0.3fm. This is conjugate to the momentum-space expansion condition 2‚Äčx‚ÄčPz‚ČęőõQCDmuch-greater-than2ūĚĎ•superscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßsubscriptőõQCD2xPzggLambdarm QCD. We will show that with a Pz‚ąľ2‚ąí3similar-tosuperscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß23Pzsim 2-3 GeV, the pion correlator used in the previous section can determine the first moment ‚ü®x2‚ü©delimited-‚ü®‚ü©superscriptūĚĎ•2 x2 reliably, but ‚ü®x4‚ü©delimited-‚ü®‚ü©superscriptūĚĎ•4 x4 marginally. The BNL lattice group has already done extensive analysis to extract the pion PDF moments from lattice quasi-PDF correlatorscitebib.bib43, bib.bib44, bib.bib20. In Ref. citebib.bib43, the lattice correlator has been used to extract moments based on fixed-order Wilson coefficients, and they seem to get stable results for ‚ü®x2‚ü©delimited-‚ü®‚ü©superscriptūĚĎ•2 x2, ‚ü®x4‚ü©delimited-‚ü®‚ü©superscriptūĚĎ•4 x4 and ‚ü®x6‚ü©delimited-‚ü®‚ü©superscriptūĚĎ•6 x6 when using lattice data up to zūĚĎßz = 0.6 fm or 0.8 fm. In Ref. citebib.bib44, they fit the data with the Wilson coefficients with NLO renormalization group resummation and threshold resummation, and they get good results for ‚ü®x2‚ü©delimited-‚ü®‚ü©superscriptūĚĎ•2 x2 and ‚ü®x4‚ü©delimited-‚ü®‚ü©superscriptūĚĎ•4 x4 when using data up to zūĚĎßz = 0.48 fm. In Ref. citebib.bib20, they use data at physical pion mass and NNLO Wilson coefficients up to zūĚĎßz = 0.72 fm with fixed-order Wilson coefficients. They have studied whether RG-improved Wilson coefficients can describe lattice data in their Appendix B but they haven‚Äôt estimated the uncertainties for moment fit from the systematic errors of Wilson coefficients at large distance. Here, we repeat their analysis using RG resummed Wilson coefficients up to NNLO. We will illustrate the systematic errors of Wilson coefficients at large distance through varying parameter cūĚĎźc in the resummation scale c/zūĚĎźūĚĎßc/z. Our results show that the perturbative Wilson coefficients are well-defined only up to about 0.3 fm, and as a consequence, the resulting ‚ü®x4‚ü©delimited-‚ü®‚ü©superscriptūĚĎ•4 x4 has a significant error.

Thus, the natural moment scale for the perturbative expansion of the Wilson coefficient is also c/zūĚĎźūĚĎßc/z, which is small for a large zūĚĎßz. This indicates that Wilson coefficients have large perturbative errors at large distance. In Appendix B of Ref. citebib.bib20, they find that RGR Wilson coefficients can describe some lattice data very well when they choose the starting scale c/zūĚĎźūĚĎßc/z no less than two times 2‚Äče‚ąíő≥E/z2superscriptūĚĎísubscriptūĚõĺūĚźłūĚĎß2e-gammaE/z. To see the difference of the fixed-order and resummed Wilson coeffcients, we compare C0MS¬ĮsubscriptsuperscriptūĚź∂¬ĮMS0Coverlinerm MS0 and C0MS¬Į‚ÄčRGRsubscriptsuperscriptūĚź∂¬ĮMSRGR0Coverlinerm MS,rm RGR0 at NLO and NNLO at őľ=3.2ūĚúá3.2mu=3.2 GeV in Fig. . For z>0.4ūĚĎß0.4z>0.4 fm, the running coupling constant is close to the Landau pole where the perturbation series breaks down, and the OPE analysis is no longer useful.

The result beyond 0.3 fm suffers from large errors. The joint fit with Pz=0.86‚ąľ2.15subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß0.86similar-to2.15Pz=0.86sim 2.15 GeV and z=0.12‚ąľ0.3ūĚĎß0.12similar-to0.3z=0.12sim 0.3 fm at lattice spacing aūĚĎéa = 0.06 fm at őľ=3.2ūĚúá3.2mu=3.2 GeV is shown in Fig. . We vary c=(0.8‚ąľ1.2)‚ąó2‚Äče‚ąíő≥EūĚĎźsimilar-to0.81.22superscriptūĚĎísubscriptūĚõĺūĚźłc=(0.8sim 1.2)*2e-gammaE for NLO+RGR and NNLO+RGR. The mean value among choosing different nz0superscriptsubscriptūĚĎõūĚĎß0nz0, zminsubscriptūĚĎßminzrm min and zmaxsubscriptūĚĎßmaxzrm max is considered as the center value (each combination is equally weighted and we choose c=2‚Äče‚ąíő≥EūĚĎź2superscriptūĚĎísubscriptūĚõĺūĚźłc=2e-gammaE for center value). After continuum extrapolation, ‚ü®x2‚ü©delimited-‚ü®‚ü©superscriptūĚĎ•2 x2 are consistent within the error range among different Wilson coefficients. Both statistical and systematic errors are reasonable for ‚ü®x2‚ü©delimited-‚ü®‚ü©superscriptūĚĎ•2 x2. However, large systematic errors are observed in ‚ü®x4‚ü©delimited-‚ü®‚ü©superscriptūĚĎ•4 x4, especially for NLO+RGR case, which come mostly from varying cūĚĎźc. Our statistical errors for ‚ü®x4‚ü©delimited-‚ü®‚ü©superscriptūĚĎ•4 x4 are larger thancitebib.bib43, bib.bib44, bib.bib20 because we can only legitimately use zūĚĎßz up to 0.3 fm, whereas the previous works use zūĚĎßz up to 0.5‚ąľsimilar-tosim0.8 fm with fixed-order Wilson coefficients. Thus it appears that the determination of ‚ü®x4‚ü©delimited-‚ü®‚ü©superscriptūĚĎ•4 x4 through fitting the lattice correlation function has a large uncertainty.
content_sections.V Summary and Outlook: In this work, we study the effects of small-momentum large logarithm resummation in the perturbative matching of LaMET. The resummed matching improves the prediction accuracy at intermediate 2‚Äčx‚ÄčPz2ūĚĎ•subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß2xPz, and at the same time, clearly indicates that the perturbative matching is unreliable at small 2‚Äčx‚ÄčPz2ūĚĎ•subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß2xPz (e. g. 2‚Äčx‚ÄčPz0.3ūĚĎß0.3z>0.3fm). These observations are consistent with each other, as small xūĚĎ•x region in momentum space conjugates to large zūĚĎßz region in coordinate space. Thus, to calculate small-xūĚĎ•x parton physics, one has to go to larger hadron momentum than presently available. With increased computational power, it may be possible to reach Pz‚ąľsimilar-tosubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßabsentPzsim 5GeV, if so one can calculate on lattice partons at x‚ąľ0.1similar-toūĚĎ•0.1xsim 0.1. To calculate, x‚ąľ0.01similar-toūĚĎ•0.01xsim 0.01, one has to reach Pz‚ąľsimilar-tosubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßabsentPzsim 50GeV, which cannot be done without a breakthrough in lattice technology or reformulation of the LaMET formalism. A related question is about large-xūĚĎ•x PDFs. As x‚Üí1‚ÜíūĚĎ•1xto 1, a new soft scale appears, (1‚ąíx)‚ÄčPz1ūĚĎ•subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß(1-x)Pz, which is the momentum of the hadron remnant. Here again, large-logarithms appear due to incomplete cancellation of infrared physics. They can be re-summed to produce more reliable predictions in the large-xūĚĎ•x region. At the same time, however, it also exposes the breakdown of LaMET in the large xūĚĎ•x region, where the higher-twist corrections again become important. We hope to address this in a future publication.

sorted_papers.title:
Lattice QCD calculation of the pion distribution amplitude with domain
  wall fermions at physical pion mass
sorted_papers.abstract:
We present a direct lattice QCD calculation of the $x$-dependence of the pion
distribution amplitude (DA), which is performed using the quasi-DA in large
momentum effective theory on a domain-wall fermion ensemble at physical quark
masses and spacing $a\approx 0.084$ fm. The bare quais-DA matrix elements are
renormalized in the hybrid scheme and matched to $\overline{\rm MS}$ with a
subtraction of the leading renormalon in the Wilson-line mass. For the first
time, we include threshold resummation in the perturbative matching onto the
light-cone DA, which resums the large logarithms in the soft gluon limit at
next-to-next-to-leading log. The resummed results show controlled
scale-variation uncertainty within the range of momentum fraction
$x\in[0.25,0.75]$ at the largest pion momentum $P_z\approx 1.85$~GeV. In
addition, we apply the same analysis to quasi-DAs from a
highly-improved-staggered-quark ensemble at physical pion mass and $a=0.076$
fm. By comparison we find with $2\sigma$ confidence level that the DA obtained
from chiral fermions is flatter and lower near $x=0.5$.

sections.62: Lehner et¬†al., Grid python toolkit (gpt), https://github.com/lehner/gpt.

qualitative: Large Momentum Effective Theory (LaMET) enables the calculation of light-cone observables, such as the pion distribution amplitude (DA), by computing quasi-observables (quasi-DAs) on the lattice at large hadron momentum.

The quasi-DA is renormalized in the hybrid scheme and matched to the physical light-cone DA in the $\overline{\rm MS}$ scheme, with a subtraction of the leading renormalon in the Wilson-line mass to improve perturbative convergence.

Threshold resummation is implemented at next-to-next-to-leading logarithmic (NNLL) accuracy in the perturbative matching, which controls large logarithms arising in the soft gluon limit and improves the reliability of the matching procedure.

The inclusion of threshold resummation leads to reduced scale-variation uncertainty in the extracted DA, particularly in the central region $x \in [0.25, 0.75]$ at large pion momentum $P_z \approx 1.85$ GeV.
Domain-wall fermions are used to minimize chiral symmetry breaking effects, enabling a more accurate calculation at physical pion mass compared to other fermion formulations.

Comparison between domain-wall fermions and highly-improved staggered quarks (HISQ) at the same physical pion mass and lattice spacing reveals a statistically significant difference: the DA from chiral fermions (domain-wall) is flatter and lower near $x = 0.5$ at the $2\sigma$ confidence level.
quantitative: Lattice spacing: $a \approx 0.084$ fm (domain-wall fermion ensemble)
Lattice spacing: $a = 0.076$ fm (HISQ ensemble)
Pion momentum: $P_z \approx 1.85$ GeV (largest momentum used)
Region of controlled scale variation: $x \in [0.25, 0.75]$
Matching performed at NNLL accuracy with threshold resummation
Renormalization scheme: hybrid scheme with $\overline{\rm MS}$ matching
Renormalon subtraction: leading renormalon in the Wilson-line mass is subtracted
Statistical significance: $2\sigma$ confidence level for difference in DA between domain-wall and HISQ fermions near $x = 0.5$

content_sections.I Introduction: Understanding the structure of pions is of special importance as they are the lightest pseudo-Nambu-Goldstone bosons of chiral symmetry breaking in strong interactions. One particular quantity of interest is the pion distribution amplitude (DA) Ōē‚Äč(x)italic-ŌēūĚĎ•phi(x) which describes the probability amplitude of finding pion on the light-cone in a quark-antiquark pair Fock state, each carrying a momentum fraction of xūĚĎ•x and 1‚ąíx1ūĚĎ•1-x. The rich phenomenology of the pion DA originates from its universality as inputs to exclusive processes and form factors at large momentum transfer Q2‚ČęőõQCD2much-greater-thansuperscriptūĚĎĄ2superscriptsubscriptőõQCD2Q2ggLambdarm QCD2. For example, the scattering amplitudes of semileptonic BūĚźĶB-meson decaycitebib.bib1, bib.bib2 and deeply virtual meson production processescitebib.bib3, which have been used to probe physics beyond the Standard Model and to extract the generalized parton distributions, respectively, are proportional to convolutions involving the pion DA in the xūĚĎ•x-space. Therefore, knowing the xūĚĎ•x-dependence of DAs is key to making predictions for the hard exclusive processes. However, they are only weakly constrained by experiments so farcitebib.bib4, bib.bib5, bib.bib6, bib.bib7, which makes it highly desirable to calculate their xūĚĎ•x-dependence from first-principle methods like lattice QCD. The pion DA Ōē‚Äč(x,őľ)italic-ŌēūĚĎ•ūĚúáphi(x,mu) is defined from a light-cone correlation as i‚ÄčfŌÄ‚ÄčŌē‚Äč(x,őľ)=ūĚĎĖsubscriptūĚĎďūĚúčitalic-ŌēūĚĎ•ūĚúáabsentdisplaystyleifpiphi(x,mu)= ‚ąęd‚Äčő∑‚ąí2‚ÄčŌÄ‚Äčei‚Äčx‚ÄčP+‚Äčő∑‚ąíūĚĎĎsuperscriptūĚúā2ūĚúčsuperscriptūĚĎíūĚĎĖūĚĎ•superscriptūĚĎÉsuperscriptūĚúādisplaystyleintfracdeta-2pieixP+eta- √ó‚ü®0|Ōą¬Į‚Äč‚Äčő≥5‚Äčő≥+‚ÄčW‚Äč(0,ő∑‚ąí)‚ÄčŌą‚Äč(ő∑‚ąí)|ŌÄ‚Äč(P)‚ü©,absentquantum-operator-product0¬ĮūĚúď0subscriptūĚõĺ5subscriptūĚõĺūĚĎä0superscriptūĚúāūĚúďsuperscriptūĚúāūĚúčūĚĎÉdisplaystyletimes0|overlinepsigamma5gamma+W(0,eta-)psi(eta-)|pi(P), where fŌÄsubscriptūĚĎďūĚúčfpi is the pion decay constant, W‚Äč(0,ő∑‚ąí)=ūĚíę‚Äčexp‚Ā°[‚ąíi‚Äčg‚Äč‚ąę0ő∑‚ąíūĚĎĎs‚Äčnőľ‚ÄčAőľ‚Äč(n‚Äčs)]ūĚĎä0superscriptūĚúāūĚíęūĚĎĖūĚĎĒsubscriptsuperscriptsuperscriptūĚúā0differential-dūĚĎ†subscriptūĚĎõūĚúásuperscriptūĚźīūĚúáūĚĎõūĚĎ†W(0,eta-)=hatPexpleft[-iginteta-0!!ds,nmuAmu(ns)right] is the Wilson line between the two light-cone coordinates 00 and ő∑‚ąí=(ő∑0+ő∑3)/2superscriptūĚúāsuperscriptūĚúā0superscriptūĚúā32eta-=(eta0+eta3)/sqrt2 with ūĚíęūĚíęhatP denoting the path-ordering operator. Due to the real-time dependence of light-cone correlations, it is impossible to directly calculate the full pion DA on a Euclidean lattice. There are different approaches to extract information of the pion DA from lattice QCD, including the calculation of its moments ‚ü®(2‚Äčx‚ąí1)n‚ü©delimited-‚ü®‚ü©superscript2ūĚĎ•1ūĚĎõ(2x-1)ncitebib.bib8, bib.bib9, bib.bib10, bib.bib11, bib.bib12, bib.bib13, short distance factorization (SDF) of nonlocal correlationscitebib.bib14, bib.bib15, bib.bib16, bib.bib17, bib.bib18, bib.bib19, and the large momentum effective theory (LaMET)citebib.bib20, bib.bib21, bib.bib22, bib.bib23, bib.bib24, bib.bib25, bib.bib26, bib.bib27. The calculation of moments is based on the light-cone operator product expansion (OPE) of the pion-DA correlator, which encodes the DA moments in twist-two local operatorscitebib.bib8. The bottleneck of this method is the increasing noise in higher moments and power-divergent operator mixings for n>3ūĚĎõ3n>3. In the SDF approach, the spatial correlation function in the pion state can have an OPEcitebib.bib28 or be factorized into the convolution of the light-cone correlation and a perturbative matching kernel at a distance |z|‚Č™őõQCD‚ąí1much-less-thanūĚĎßsuperscriptsubscriptőõQCD1|z|llLambdarm QCD-1. Therefore, this method can provide information of the first few moments, which in principle can go beyond n=3ūĚĎõ3n=3, or the light-cone correlation within a limited range. However, it still requires a model assumption of the shape of DA to reconstruct its xūĚĎ•x-dependencecitebib.bib16, bib.bib19. The LaMET approach provides a direct calculation of the xūĚĎ•x-dependence through an effective theory expansion in the momentum space, up to power corrections suppressed by the parton momenta x‚ÄčPzūĚĎ•subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßxPz and (1‚ąíx)‚ÄčPz1ūĚĎ•subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß(1-x)Pz, which allows for a reliable calculation of the DA within a moderate range of xūĚĎ•x. Since the first LaMET calculation of the pion DAcitebib.bib23, various lattice artifacts and theoretical systematics have been studied. One of the key systematics is the lattice renormalization of the quasi-DA correlators, which suffer from the linear power divergence that must be subtracted at all distancescitebib.bib29, bib.bib30, bib.bib31, bib.bib32. The regularization-independent momentum subtraction (RI/MOM)citebib.bib33, bib.bib34, bib.bib35, bib.bib36 and ratiocitebib.bib37, bib.bib38 schemes were proposed to cancel the linear and other ultraviolet (UV) divergences, but both introduce uncontrolled non-perturbative effects at large distance for LaMET calculation. Then, the hybrid schemecitebib.bib39 was proposed to overcome this problem with a subtraction of Wilson line mass at long distancescitebib.bib40, bib.bib41, but there is still a remaining linear renormalon ambiguity of order őõQCDsubscriptőõQCDLambdarm QCD that contributes to a linear power correction in the LaMET expansion. Recently, this issue was resolved with the leading-renormalon resummation (LRR) methodcitebib.bib27, bib.bib42, which removes such an ambiguity in lattice renormalization and LaMET matching, thus improving the power accuracy to sub-leading order. Apart from lattice renormalization, there has also been significant progress in the perturbative matching, from the next-to-leading order (NLO) matching kernel for quasi-DAcitebib.bib23, bib.bib24, bib.bib43, bib.bib44, bib.bib45 to the renormalization group resummation (RGR)citebib.bib46, bib.bib47 and threshold resummationcitebib.bib46, bib.bib48, bib.bib49. On the numerical side, the first continuum extrapolation of the DA was carried out in Ref. citebib.bib25 with the RI/MOM scheme at unphysical pion masses, which observed a sensitive dependence on the pion mass. This calculation was succeeded by a continuum extrapolation at physical pion mass with NLO hybrid-scheme renormalization and matching, but without LRRcitebib.bib26. The LRR method was first implemented in Ref. citebib.bib27 with NLO matching and RGR. Meanwhile, the pion DA moments have also been calculated using the SDF approach at NLO accuracycitebib.bib16, bib.bib19. Nevertheless, no lattice calculation in the literature has included the threshold resummation yet. So far, all the lattice calculations of pion DA xūĚĎ•x-dependence have been performed with highly-improved-staggered-quark (HISQ) fermion actionscitebib.bib50, bib.bib51 or clover fermion actionscitebib.bib52, both of which explicitly break the chiral symmetry. Since the pions are the pseudo-Nambu-Goldstone bosons of chiral symmetry breaking in QCD, it is then very interesting to study how the chiral symmetry of lattice action affects their structure such as the DA. The overlap fermioncitebib.bib53, bib.bib54, bib.bib55, bib.bib56 and domain wall fermion (DWF) actionscitebib.bib57, bib.bib58, bib.bib59 are known to preserve chiral symmetry on the lattice, although these ensembles are more expensive to generate. Therefore, performing the same calculation on chiral fermion ensembles will provide us with knowledge about the effects of chiral symmetry breaking on the pion structure. In this work, we present the first direct calculation of the xūĚĎ•x-dependence of pion DA on a lattice ensemble with the DWF fermion action at physical pion mass and spacing a‚Čą0.084ūĚĎé0.084aapprox 0.084 fm. We renormalize the bare pion quasi-DA matrix elements with the hybrid scheme, and then match them to the continuum MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinerm MS scheme with LRR. After Fourier transforming to the xūĚĎ•x-space, we match the quasi-DA to the light-cone with next-to-next-to-leading logarithmic (NNLL) threshold resummation and NLO matching. For the first time, we reformulate the recently developed threshold resummation technique for the quasi-PDF casecitebib.bib48 to work for the quasi-DA, which improves the estimate of systematic uncertainties near the end-point regions x‚Üí0‚ÜíūĚĎ•0xto 0 and x‚Üí1‚ÜíūĚĎ•1xto 1. With the reformulated threshold factorization, we also find out that the complicated two-scale resummationcitebib.bib27 of the Efremov-Radyushkin-Brodsky-Lepage (ERBL) logarithmscitebib.bib60, bib.bib61, bib.bib62, bib.bib63 is now factorized into two separate pieces with each involving only a single physical scale, thus making it straightforward to understand and implement the RGR. Finally, we observe a notably flat DA in the region x‚ąą[0.25,0.75]ūĚĎ•0.250.75xin[0.25,0.75], while the results beyond this range become unreliable due to the breakdown of perturbation theory. Moreover, compared with the same analysis applied to the HISQ datacitebib.bib19 at physical pion mass and lattice spacing a=0.076ūĚĎé0.076a=0.076 fm, we observe that the pion DA from the DWF ensemble is slightly flatter near x=0.5ūĚĎ•0.5x=0.5 than that from the HISQ ensemble. Our results suggest that the pion structure is not sensitive to chiral symmetry on the lattice. This work is organized as follows. In Sec. , we present our lattice setup of the calculation, and show how the raw lattice data are processed to extract the matrix elements of pion DA. In Sec. , we analyze the bare matrix elements to extract the DA moments using the SDF or OPE approach, and discuss how the matrix elements are properly and consistently renormalized in the hybrid scheme with LRR to obtain xūĚĎ•x-dependent quasi-DA. In Sec. , we derive the formalism to resum both ERBL and threshold logarithms in the perturbative matching and implement the improved matching to extract the light-cone DA, and compare with the same analysis applied to data on a HISQ ensemblecitebib.bib19. Finally, we conclude in Sec. .
content_sections.II Lattice set up: In this calculation, we used a 2+1-flavor domain-wall gauge ensemble generated by RBC and UKQCD Collaborations of size Ns3√óNt√óN5=643√ó128√ó12superscriptsubscriptūĚĎĀūĚĎ†3subscriptūĚĎĀūĚĎ°subscriptūĚĎĀ5superscript64312812Ns3times Nttimes N5=643times 128times 12, denoted by 64Icitebib.bib64. The quark masses are at the physical point and the lattice spacing is a=0.0836ūĚĎé0.0836a=0.0836 fm. 55 gauge configurations were used in this calculation. The quark propagators are evaluated from Coulomb-gauge-fixed configurations using deflation based solver with 2000 eigen vectors. In addition, we used the boosted Gaussian momentum smearingcitebib.bib65 to improve the signal. The Gaussian radius was set to be rGsubscriptūĚĎüūĚźļrG = 0.58 fm. We chose the quark boost parameter jzsubscriptūĚĎóūĚĎßjz to be 0 and 6citebib.bib66, bib.bib67 which are optimal to hadron momentum Pz=2‚ÄčŌÄ‚Äčnz/(Ns‚Äča)subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß2ūĚúčsubscriptūĚĎõūĚĎßsubscriptūĚĎĀūĚĎ†ūĚĎéPz=2pi nz/(Nsa) with nzsubscriptūĚĎõūĚĎßnz = 0 and 8, so that the largest momentum in our calculation is Pz=1.85subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß1.85Pz=1.85 GeV. Since only two-point functions are involved in this calculationcitebib.bib19, measurements at other momenta (nz‚ąą[0,3]subscriptūĚĎõūĚĎß03nzin[0,3] for jz=0subscriptūĚĎóūĚĎß0jz=0 and nz‚ąą[4,8]subscriptūĚĎõūĚĎß48nzin[4,8] for jz=6subscriptūĚĎóūĚĎß6jz=6) were also computed through contractions using the same profiled quark propagator. To increase the statistics, we ultilzed the All Mode Averaging (AMA) techniquecitebib.bib68 with 2 exact and 128 sloppy sources for momenta nz‚ąą[4,8]subscriptūĚĎõūĚĎß48nzin[4,8], while 1 exact and 32 sloppy sources for nz=[0,3]subscriptūĚĎõūĚĎß03nz=[0,3]. The tolerance of the exact and sloppy sources are 10‚ąí8superscript10810-8 and 10‚ąí4superscript10410-4 respectively. To suppress the ultraviolet (UV) fluctuations and enhance the signal-to-noise ratio of the matrix elements with long Wilson links, we employed Wilson flowcitebib.bib69, with a flow time tF=1.0subscriptūĚĎ°ūĚźĻ1.0tF=1.0 (roughly corresponds to a smearing radius 8‚Äča28superscriptūĚĎé2sqrt8a2). At large Euclidean time, the effective masses decays to approach ground state plateaus, which are consistent with the dispersion relation E‚Äč(Pz)=Pz2+m2ūĚźłsubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßsuperscriptsubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß2superscriptūĚĎö2E(Pz)=sqrtPz2+m2 from zero momentum correlators, plotted as colored lines. Thus we two-state fit the energy spectrum at z=0ūĚĎß0z=0 using the dispersion relation as a prior for E0subscriptūĚźł0E0. The first-excited state of the pion using smeared correlators on the lattice has been studied in previous workscitebib.bib71, bib.bib66. In these works it was suggested that the first excited state is the ŌÄ‚ÄčūĚúč1300pi state. Therefore, the energy of the first excited pion state for different moment can be estimated using the dispersion relation and the mass of ŌÄ‚ÄčūĚúč1300pi state. We use this estimate as a prior for E1subscriptūĚźł1E1, with a width of 0.50.50.5GeV. The first-excited state energies from two-state fit are shown in Fig. as function of tm‚Äči‚ÄčnsubscriptūĚĎ°ūĚĎöūĚĎĖūĚĎõtmin, with tm‚Äči‚ÄčnsubscriptūĚĎ°ūĚĎöūĚĎĖūĚĎõtmin being the lower limit of the fit interval. By including the excited state contribution, we are able to utilize data at smaller Euclidean time, where the plateau of effective mass has not been reached, and still get good fit quality. Two examples of the fits at nz=8subscriptūĚĎõūĚĎß8nz=8 and z=0ūĚĎß0z=0 and z=3‚ÄčaūĚĎß3ūĚĎéz=3a with different fit ranges are shown in Fig. . To compensate the exponential decay in Euclidean time, we multiply the correlators with a factor of eE¬Į0‚ÄčtsuperscriptūĚĎísubscript¬ĮūĚźł0ūĚĎ°ebarE0t. There is a good consistency between data and our tmin=4‚ÄčasubscriptūĚĎ°min4ūĚĎétrm min=4a fit bands. The fitted ground state E0subscriptūĚźł0E0 and the first excited state E1subscriptūĚźł1E1 are shown in Fig. as a function of nzsubscriptūĚĎõūĚĎßnz. These results are consistent with the priors based on the dispersion relation, depicted as blue and orange bands. Once E0subscriptūĚźł0E0 and the ground state coefficients A0O0superscriptsubscriptūĚźī0subscriptūĚĎā0A0O0 and A0ŌÄsuperscriptsubscriptūĚźī0ūĚúčA0pi in Eq. are known, we can calculate bare fŌÄsubscriptūĚĎďūĚúčfpi, fŌÄbare=A0O0‚ÄčE0‚ÄčA0ŌÄ/2. subscriptsuperscriptūĚĎďbareūĚúčsuperscriptsubscriptūĚźī0subscriptūĚĎā00subscriptūĚźł0superscriptsubscriptūĚźī0ūĚúč2displaystyle frm barepi=fracA0O0sqrtE0A0pi/2. Although we have not calculated the renormalization constant ZAsuperscriptūĚĎćūĚźīZA and ZSsuperscriptūĚĎćūĚĎÜZS to obtain the renormalized fŌÄsubscriptūĚĎďūĚúčfpi, which makes it infeasible to directly compare with the physical value, the consistency of fŌÄbaresubscriptsuperscriptūĚĎďbareūĚúčfrm barepi from different fits can be used as a criteria of the fit quality besides the Ōá2/d. o. fformulae-sequencesuperscriptūĚúí2ūĚĎĎūĚĎúūĚĎďchi2/d. o. f and p-value. The fŌÄbaresubscriptsuperscriptūĚĎďbareūĚúčfrm barepi calculated from the coefficients of these different fits are shown in Fig. . The plot suggests that the fitted fŌÄsubscriptūĚĎďūĚúčfpi is consistent for different momenta with different choices of tminsubscriptūĚĎ°mintrm min, although the error increases significantly for large pion momenta. Taking energies from the above two-state fit of local correlators as inputs, we fit the non-local correlations |z|>0ūĚĎß0|z|>0 to extract the coefficients A0‚Äč(z)subscriptūĚźī0ūĚĎßA0(z) in Eq. . content_sections.III Extraction of pion quasi-DA III.2 Re: In the previous section, we have extracted the moments of pion DA through a renormalization-independent approach. To directly calculate the xūĚĎ•x-dependence Ōē‚Äč(x,őľ)italic-ŌēūĚĎ•ūĚúáphi(x,mu), however, we have to first properly renormalize the correlation functions. Among multiple renormalization schemes, the hybrid renormalization schemecitebib.bib39 is preferred in quasi-DA calculation because it allows a perturbative matching to MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinerm MS scheme at all zūĚĎßz, where the factorization theorem of quasi-DA is proven. In the hybrid scheme renormalization, Zhybrid(z,a)=ZR‚Äč(z,a)|z|‚Č§zsZR‚Äč(zs,a)‚Äče‚ąíőī‚Äčm‚Äč(a)‚Äč(|z|‚ąízs)|z|>zsdisplaystyle Zrm hybrid(z,a)=leftbeginmatrixZR(z,a)hfill|z|leq zs ZR(zs,a)e-delta m(a)(|z|-zs)|z|>zsendmatrixright. where őī‚Äčm‚Äč(a)‚ąľa‚ąí1similar-toūĚõŅūĚĎöūĚĎésuperscriptūĚĎé1delta m(a)sim a-1 is the mass renormalization parameter that removes the linear divergence, and the short-distance renormalization factor ZR‚Äč(z,a)superscriptūĚĎćūĚĎÖūĚĎßūĚĎéZR(z,a) can be some lattice matrix elements with the same divergence, for example, the same operator measured in other external states. A widely-used choice is the matrix element in the same hadron state at restcitebib.bib37, ZR(z,a)=HB(z,Pz=0,a). displaystyle ZR(z,a)=HB(z,Pz=0,a). At long range |z|>zsūĚĎßsubscriptūĚĎßūĚĎ†|z|>zs, the hybrid scheme requires the determination of the mass renormalization parameter őī‚Äčm‚Äč(a)ūĚõŅūĚĎöūĚĎédelta m(a). However, its determination contains an infrared ambiguity of ‚ąľūĚí™‚Äč(őõQCD)similar-toabsentūĚí™subscriptőõQCDsimO(Lambdarm QCD)citebib.bib39, which can be labelled as a regularization scheme dependence őī‚Äčm‚Äč(a,ŌĄ)ūĚõŅūĚĎöūĚĎéūĚúŹdelta m(a,tau). content_sections.III Extraction of pion quasi-DA III.3 xū: With the LaMET approach, we aim at calculating the local xūĚĎ•x-dependence directly in a certain range with controlled systematics. On the other hand, lattice calculations of the matrix elements are naturally performed in the coordinate space. Therefore, a Fourier transformation is needed to obtain the xūĚĎ•x-dependence of quasi-DA first. Due to the worsening signal-to-noise ratio as zūĚĎßz increases, lattice data are limited to a certain range of zūĚĎßz or z‚ÄčPzūĚĎßsubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßzPz, which makes an exact Fourier transform impossible. However, although the long tail of the correlation is unknown, it follows certain physical constraints that prevent it from going out of controlcitebib.bib39. By performing an extrapolation in z‚ÄčPzūĚĎßsuperscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßzPz using such constraints, one can reduce the uncertainties in the long-tail region, thus eliminating unphysical oscillatory behaviors in the xūĚĎ•x-space after the Fourier transform. One strong physical constraint on the long-tail distribution is the finite correlation length őĽ0‚ąĚPzproportional-tosubscriptūĚúÜ0subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßlambda0propto Pz in the coordinate space, which results in an exponential decay exp‚Ā°[‚ąíőĽ/őĽ0]ūĚúÜsubscriptūĚúÜ0exp[-lambda/lambda0] of the Euclidean correlation functions at large őĽ=z‚ÄčPzūĚúÜūĚĎßsubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßlambda=zPz. For example, we can model the long-tail region to the following formcitebib.bib39, H‚Äč(őĽ)‚ÜíőĽ‚Üí‚ąě(c1‚Äče‚ąíi‚ÄčőĽ/2(‚ąíi‚ÄčőĽ)d1+c2‚Äčei‚ÄčőĽ/2(i‚ÄčőĽ)d2)‚Äče‚ąíőĽ/őĽ0,‚ÜíūĚúÜ‚ÜíūĚźĽūĚúÜsubscriptūĚĎź1superscriptūĚĎíūĚĎĖūĚúÜ2superscriptūĚĎĖūĚúÜsubscriptūĚĎĎ1subscriptūĚĎź2superscriptūĚĎíūĚĎĖūĚúÜ2superscriptūĚĎĖūĚúÜsubscriptūĚĎĎ2superscriptūĚĎíūĚúÜsubscriptūĚúÜ0displaystyle H(lambda)xrightarrowlambdatoinftyleft(fracc1e-ilambda/2(-ilambda)d1+fracc2eilambda/2(ilambda)d2right)e-lambda/lambda0, where the parameterization inside the round brackets is motivated from the Regge behaviorcitebib.bib80 of the light-cone distribution near the endpoint regions. For symmetric pion DA, c1=c2subscriptūĚĎź1subscriptūĚĎź2c1=c2 and d1=d2subscriptūĚĎĎ1subscriptūĚĎĎ2d1=d2. To estimate the model dependence, we perform three different fits of the long tail, considering where the correlation functions start to be dominated by the exponentially decay, or more conservatively, assuming no exponential decay at all. These attempts include ‚ÄĘ fitting data from őĽ‚Čą8.5ūĚúÜ8.5lambdaapprox 8.5 to Eq. , labeled as ‚ÄúExponential‚ÄĚ; ‚ÄĘ fitting data from őĽ‚Čą8.5ūĚúÜ8.5lambdaapprox 8.5 to Eq. without the exponential decay e‚ąíőĽ/őĽ0superscriptūĚĎíūĚúÜsubscriptūĚúÜ0e-lambda/lambda0, labeled as ‚ÄúAlgebraic‚ÄĚ; ‚ÄĘ fitting data from őĽ‚Čą11ūĚúÜ11lambdaapprox 11 to Eq. , labeled as ‚ÄúLarge őĽūĚúÜlambda‚ÄĚ. The results are shown in Fig. . The long-tail extrapolation will eventually introduce about ¬Ī5%plus-or-minuspercent5pm 5% systematic uncertainties to the light-cone DA near x=0.5ūĚĎ•0.5x=0.5 in our case. To reduce this uncertainty, a more precise measurement of the long-range correlation of quasi-DA is necessary, especially for determining the oscillating behavior of the long tail.

Two important systematics have been discussed in Ref. citebib.bib27, including the leading power correction and the large small-momentum logarithmic contributions. The linear power correction has been demonstrated to be eliminated through LRRcitebib.bib42, so we apply the same correction on the matching kernel here. On the other hand, the small-momentum logarithmic contributions can be resummedcitebib.bib47 by solving the (ERBL) RG equation, but there is no well-established method due to the more complicated two-scale nature of the DA matching kernelcitebib.bib27. Note that the logarithm related to quark (antiquark) momentum fraction in Eq. has the form xy‚Äčln‚Ā°xūĚĎ•ūĚĎ¶ūĚĎ•fracxyln x or x¬Įy¬Į‚Äčln‚Ā°x¬Į¬ĮūĚĎ•¬ĮūĚĎ¶¬ĮūĚĎ•fracbarxbarylnbarx. The coefficients of the logarithm are thus suppressed when x‚Üí0,1‚ÜíūĚĎ•01xto0,1, whose contributions vanish when the quark (antiquark) momentum becomes very soft, except for the limit when x‚Üíy‚ÜíūĚĎ•ūĚĎ¶xto y. Therefore, the ERBL logarithm only needs to be resummed in the threshold limit x‚Üíy‚ÜíūĚĎ•ūĚĎ¶xto y. And as we will show below, the resummation of small-momentum logarithms will also become more straightforward in the threshold limit.
content_sections.IV Matching to the light-cone DA IV.1 Ph: It has been shown that there are two physical scales in the quasi-DA matching, corresponding to the quark momentum 2‚Äčx‚ÄčPz2ūĚĎ•subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß2xPz and antiquark momentum 2‚Äč(1‚ąíx)‚ÄčPz21ūĚĎ•subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß2(1-x)Pz, respectivelycitebib.bib27. These two scales becomes apparent when considering the threshold limit x‚Üíy‚ÜíūĚĎ•ūĚĎ¶xto y of ūĚíě‚Äč(x,y,őľ,Pz)ūĚíěūĚĎ•ūĚĎ¶ūĚúásubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßC(x,y,mu,Pz), where it can be factorized into a heavy-light Sudakov form factor H‚Äč(x‚ÄčPz,x¬Į‚ÄčPz,őľ)ūĚźĽūĚĎ•subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß¬ĮūĚĎ•subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßūĚúáH(xPz,barxPz,mu) and a jet function J‚Äč(|x‚ąíy|‚ÄčPz,őľ)ūĚźĹūĚĎ•ūĚĎ¶subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßūĚúáJ(|x-y|Pz,mu)citebib.bib81, bib.bib48, up to higher orders of (y‚ąíx)ūĚĎ¶ūĚĎ•(y-x) (note that the leading term is singular in (y‚ąíx)ūĚĎ¶ūĚĎ•(y-x), thus the corrections starts from the 00-th order), ūĚíě‚Äč(x,y,őľ,Pz)‚Üíx‚Üíy‚ÜíūĚĎ•ūĚĎ¶‚ÜíūĚíěūĚĎ•ūĚĎ¶ūĚúásubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßabsentdisplaystyleC(x,y,mu,Pz)xrightarrowxto y H‚Äč(x‚ÄčPz,x¬Į‚ÄčPz,őľ)‚äóJ‚Äč(|x‚ąíy|‚ÄčPz,őľ)tensor-productūĚźĽūĚĎ•subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß¬ĮūĚĎ•subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßūĚúáūĚźĹūĚĎ•ūĚĎ¶subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßūĚúádisplaystyle H(xPz,barxPz,mu)otimes J(|x-y|Pz,mu) +ūĚí™‚Äč((y‚ąíx)0). ūĚí™superscriptūĚĎ¶ūĚĎ•0displaystyle+O((y-x)0). In coordinate space, the factorization is multiplicative. Thus the order of the Sudakov factor HūĚźĽH and the jet function JūĚźĹJ in the momentum space factorization Eq. does not matter in the threshold limit. The heavy-light Sudakov factor is obtained by matching the heavy-light currents to soft-collinear effective theorycitebib.bib82, which in our case is the product of two components from two heavy-light currents in coordinate spacecitebib.bib82, bib.bib83, Hz‚Äč(x‚ÄčPz,x¬Į‚ÄčPz,őľ)=CŌē‚ąísign‚Äč(z‚Äčx)‚Äč(x‚ÄčPz,őľ)‚ÄčCŌēsign‚Äč(z‚Äčx¬Į)‚Äč(x¬Į‚ÄčPz,őľ),subscriptūĚźĽūĚĎßūĚĎ•subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß¬ĮūĚĎ•subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßūĚúásuperscriptsubscriptūĚź∂italic-ŌēsignūĚĎßūĚĎ•ūĚĎ•subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßūĚúásuperscriptsubscriptūĚź∂italic-ŌēsignūĚĎß¬ĮūĚĎ•¬ĮūĚĎ•subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßūĚúádisplaystyletildeHz(xPz,barxPz,mu)=Cphi-rm sign(zx)(xPz,mu)Cphirm sign(zbarx)(barxPz,mu), where the signs depend on whether the momentum of the quark (antiquark) is aligned with (opposed to) the wilson line direction. From now on we will focus on the physical region 0<x<10ūĚĎ•10<x<1 only, because the non-physical region is always far away from the threshold limit x‚Üíy‚ÜíūĚĎ•ūĚĎ¶xto y when 0<y<10ūĚĎ¶10<y<1. The heavy-light Sudakov factor in the quasi-DA differs from the quasi-PDF case in two aspects: the two heavy-light vertices have different momenta in quasi-DA; their phases are opposite in the quasi-DA. Although the Sudakov factor in Eq. explicitly depends on the momentum fraction xūĚĎ•x, its imaginary part has zūĚĎßz-dependence through sign(z)ūĚĎß(z) of the Wilson line direction. When Fourier transformed to the momentum space, it contributes to the matching kernel in the physical region as: ‚ĄĎ‚Ā°[Hz‚Äč(x‚ÄčPz,x¬Į‚ÄčPz,őľ)]=sign‚Äč(z)‚ÄčŌÄ‚ÄčőĪs‚ÄčCF4‚ÄčŌÄ‚Äčln‚Ā°x2x¬Į2,subscriptūĚźĽūĚĎßūĚĎ•subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß¬ĮūĚĎ•subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßūĚúásignūĚĎßūĚúčsubscriptūĚõľūĚĎ†subscriptūĚź∂ūĚźĻ4ūĚúčsuperscriptūĚĎ•2superscript¬ĮūĚĎ•2displaystyleIm[tildeHz(xPz,barxPz,mu)]=rm sign(z)pifracalphasCF4pilnfracx2barx2,, ‚ĄĪ‚Äč[i‚Äč‚ĄĎ‚Ā°[Hz‚Äč(x‚ÄčPz,x¬Į‚ÄčPz,őľ)]]y‚ąíx=őĪs‚ÄčCF4‚ÄčŌÄ‚Äč1y‚ąíx‚Äčln‚Ā°x2x¬Į2,‚ĄĪsubscriptdelimited-ūĚĎĖsubscriptūĚźĽūĚĎßūĚĎ•subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß¬ĮūĚĎ•subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßūĚúáūĚĎ¶ūĚĎ•subscriptūĚõľūĚĎ†subscriptūĚź∂ūĚźĻ4ūĚúč1ūĚĎ¶ūĚĎ•superscriptūĚĎ•2superscript¬ĮūĚĎ•2displaystyleF[iIm[tildeHz(xPz,barxPz,mu)]]y-x=fracalphasCF4pifrac1y-xlnfracx2barx2, where ‚ĄĪ‚Äč[i‚ÄčŌÄ‚Äčsign‚Äč(z)]y‚ąíx=(y‚ąíx)‚ąí1‚ĄĪsubscriptdelimited-ūĚĎĖūĚúčsignūĚĎßūĚĎ¶ūĚĎ•superscriptūĚĎ¶ūĚĎ•1F[ipirm sign(z)]y-x=(y-x)-1 has been used. The real part contribution is proportional to őī‚Äč(x‚ąíy)ūĚõŅūĚĎ•ūĚĎ¶delta(x-y) when convoluted with the jet function, or equivalently, acting as an multiplicative factor to the jet function. In the threshold factorization Eq. , all the external-state and Dirac-structure dependence has been absorbed into the Sudakov factor. So the jet function has the same form as the quasi-PDFcitebib.bib48, except that the variable is |y‚ąíx|ūĚĎ¶ūĚĎ•|y-x| instead of |1‚ąíx/y|1ūĚĎ•ūĚĎ¶|1-x/y| because the integral measure differs by a factor of 1/|y|1ūĚĎ¶1/|y| in the two cases, J‚Äč(|x‚ąíy|‚ÄčPz,őľ)=őĪs‚ÄčCF2‚ÄčŌÄ‚Äč[ln‚Ā°4‚ÄčPz2‚Äč|y‚ąíx|2őľ2|y‚ąíx|‚ąí1|y‚ąíx|]+[‚ąí1,1]ūĚźĹūĚĎ•ūĚĎ¶subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßūĚúásubscriptūĚõľūĚĎ†subscriptūĚź∂ūĚźĻ2ūĚúčsubscriptsuperscriptdelimited-4superscriptsubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß2superscriptūĚĎ¶ūĚĎ•2superscriptūĚúá2ūĚĎ¶ūĚĎ•1ūĚĎ¶ūĚĎ•11displaystyle J(|x-y|Pz,mu)=fracalphasCF2pileft[fraclnfrac4Pz2|y-x|2mu2|y-x|-frac1|y-x| right][-1,1]+ +őī‚Äč(x‚ąíy)‚Äč[1+őĪs‚ÄčCF2‚ÄčŌÄ‚Äč(2+ŌÄ24+12‚Äčln2‚Ā°4‚ÄčPz2őľ2‚ąíln‚Ā°4‚ÄčPz2őľ2)],ūĚõŅūĚĎ•ūĚĎ¶delimited-1subscriptūĚõľūĚĎ†subscriptūĚź∂ūĚźĻ2ūĚúč2superscriptūĚúč2412superscript24superscriptsubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß2superscriptūĚúá24superscriptsubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß2superscriptūĚúá2displaystyle+delta(x-y)left[1+fracalphasCF2pileft(2+fracpi24+frac12ln2frac4Pz2mu2-lnfrac4Pz2mu2right)right], and in coordinate space, Jz‚Äč(z,őľ)=1+őĪs‚ÄčCF2‚ÄčŌÄ‚Äč(12‚Äčlz2+lz+ŌÄ212+2),subscriptūĚźĹūĚĎßūĚĎßūĚúá1subscriptūĚõľūĚĎ†subscriptūĚź∂ūĚźĻ2ūĚúč12superscriptsubscriptūĚĎôūĚĎß2subscriptūĚĎôūĚĎßsuperscriptūĚúč2122displaystyletildeJz(z,mu)=1+fracalphasCF2pileft(frac12lz2+lz+fracpi212+2right), where lz=ln‚Ā°(e2‚Äčő≥E‚Äčőľ2‚Äčz2/4)subscriptūĚĎôūĚĎßsuperscriptūĚĎí2subscriptūĚõĺūĚźłsuperscriptūĚúá2superscriptūĚĎß24lz=ln(e2gammaEmu2z2/4). In the hybrid scheme, the threshold limit of the momentum-space formalism is modified bycitebib.bib84: őĒ‚ÄčūĚíěőĒūĚíědisplaystyleDeltaC |x‚Üíyhybridő≥i‚Äčő≥5=gammaigamma5rm hybrid|xto y= ‚ąíőī‚Äč(x‚ąíy)‚ÄčőĪs‚ÄčCF2‚ÄčŌÄ‚Äč(32‚Äčln‚Ā°zs2‚Äčőľ2‚Äče2‚Äčő≥E4+5+2‚Äčőīi‚Äč32). ūĚõŅūĚĎ•ūĚĎ¶subscriptūĚõľūĚĎ†subscriptūĚź∂ūĚźĻ2ūĚúč32superscriptsubscriptūĚĎßūĚĎ†2superscriptūĚúá2superscriptūĚĎí2subscriptūĚõĺūĚźł452subscriptūĚõŅūĚĎĖ32displaystyle-delta(x-y)fracalphasCF2pileft(frac32lnfraczs2mu2e2gammaE4+frac5+2deltai32right). We can verify that the combined contribution reproduces the ERBL logarithm as well as the finite terms of the NLO matching kernel Eq. in the threshold limit. The physical scales in the matching kernel become manifest after threshold factorization. The Sudakov factor incorporates the logarithms of the quark and anti-quark‚Äôs momenta, and the jet function depends on the soft gluon momentum. Moreover, the quark and antiquark momentum exist in two separate heavy-light Sudakov factors CŌē¬Ī‚Äč(pz,őľ)superscriptsubscriptūĚź∂italic-Ōēplus-or-minussubscriptūĚĎĚūĚĎßūĚúáCphipm(pz,mu), thus they can be resummed independently. So in the threshold limit, we are able to resum all the different logarithms in the matching kernel by solving the RG equations.
content_sections.IV Matching to the light-cone DA IV.2 Re: The three parts in the threshold limit are resummed separately. To obtain the threshold-resummed matching kernel, we perform a two-step convolution: we first remove the fixed-order singular threshold terms (i. e. , the Sudakov factor and jet functions in the form of őī‚Äč(y‚ąíx)ūĚõŅūĚĎ¶ūĚĎ•delta(y-x) and 1y‚ąíx1ūĚĎ¶ūĚĎ•frac1y-x) from the fixed-order matching kernel by convoluting an inverse threshold terms with the original fixed-order matching kernel, then add back the corresponding resummed terms by convoluting with the resummed singular threshold terms, CTR‚Äč(őľ)=J‚ÄčHTRsubscriptūĚź∂TRūĚúáūĚźĹsubscriptūĚźĽTRdisplaystyle Crm TR(mu)=JHrm TR (őľi,őľh1,őľh2,őľ)subscriptūĚúáūĚĎĖsubscriptūĚúásubscript‚Ąé1subscriptūĚúásubscript‚Ąé2ūĚúádisplaystyle(mui,muh1,muh2,mu) ‚äóJ‚ÄčHNLO‚ąí1‚Äč(őľ)‚äóCNLO‚Äč(őľ),tensor-productabsenttensor-productūĚźĹsubscriptsuperscriptūĚźĽ1NLOūĚúásubscriptūĚź∂NLOūĚúádisplaystyleotimes JH-1rm NLO(mu)otimes Crm NLO(mu), where J‚ÄčH=H‚äóJūĚźĹūĚźĽtensor-productūĚźĽūĚźĹJH=Hotimes J or J‚äóHtensor-productūĚźĹūĚźĽJotimes H, both reproducing the same singular threshold terms because the factorization is multiplicative in coordinate space. We average them and consider the difference between the two choices as a systematic error in our final results. Note that we have resummed the renormalon in our perturbative matching kernel CNLO‚ąí1‚Äč(őľ)subscriptsuperscriptūĚź∂1NLOūĚúáC-1rm NLO(mu) with LRR to remove the linear correction. The same linear renormalon from the linearly divergent Wilson line self energy now exist in the jet function. To resum the large logarithms in the LRR-improved matching kernel, we then need to resum the renormalon in the jet function in exactly the same way. To implement it to the resummed form Eq. , it is more straightforward to modify the coordinate space expression JzsubscriptūĚźĹūĚĎßtildeJz at scale őľ0=2‚Äčz‚ąí1‚Äče‚ąíő≥EsubscriptūĚúá02superscriptūĚĎß1superscriptūĚĎísubscriptūĚõĺūĚźłmu0=2z-1e-gammaE, then evolve to the scale őľūĚúámu, resulting in a coefficient (1+őĪs‚ÄčCF2‚ÄčŌÄ‚Äč(12‚Äčlz2+lz))1subscriptūĚõľūĚĎ†subscriptūĚź∂ūĚźĻ2ūĚúč12superscriptsubscriptūĚĎôūĚĎß2subscriptūĚĎôūĚĎß(1+fracalphasCF2pi(frac12lz2+lz)) of the renormalon resummed term, JūĚźĹdisplaystyletildeJ (z,őľ)zL‚ÄčR‚ÄčR=1+őĪs‚ÄčCF2‚ÄčŌÄ(12lz2+lz+ŌÄ212+2)+LRRz(z,mu)=1+fracalphasCF2pileft(frac12lz2+lz+fracpi212+2right)+ (1+őĪs‚ÄčCF2‚ÄčŌÄ(12lz2+lz))Nm[‚ąíőĪs(1+c1)+4‚ÄčŌÄő≤0‚ąę0,PV‚ąědudisplaystyleleft(1+fracalphasCF2pi(frac12lz2+lz)right)NmBig[-alphas(1+c1)+frac4pibeta0intrm 0,PVinftydu e‚ąí4‚ÄčŌÄ‚ÄčuőĪs‚Äč(őľ)‚Äčő≤01(1‚ąí2‚Äču)1+b(1+c1(1‚ąí2u)+‚Ä¶],displaystyle e-frac4pi ualphas(mu)beta0frac1(1-2u)1+bbig(1+c1(1-2u)+. . . Big], Then the regularized renormalons will cancel between the fixed-order jet function JNLO‚Äč(őľh)subscriptūĚźĹNLOsubscriptūĚúá‚ĄéJrm NLO(muh) and the resummed jet function JTR‚ąí1‚Äč(őľi,őľh)subscriptsuperscriptūĚźĹ1TRsubscriptūĚúáūĚĎĖsubscriptūĚúá‚ĄéJ-1rm TR(mui,muh). To turn off the resummation of either the Sudakov factor or the jet function, we can set the corresponding scale to the same as őľūĚúámu in Eq. . So we can examine the effect of the two resummations independently, as shown in Fig. . Here we truncate the resummed results for x‚ąą[0.2,0.8]ūĚĎ•0.20.8xin[0.2,0.8] to avoid őĪs‚Äč(2‚Äčx‚ÄčPz)subscriptūĚõľūĚĎ†2ūĚĎ•subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßalphas(2xPz) or őĪs‚Äč(2‚Äčx¬Į‚ÄčPz)subscriptūĚõľūĚĎ†2¬ĮūĚĎ•subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßalphas(2barxPz) becoming non-perturbative. As one can see, the resummations of the Sudakov factor and jet function have opposite effects on the final xūĚĎ•x-dependence of the light-cone DA. The higher-order large logarithms of soft quark (antiquark) momentum tend to enhance the endpoint regions of the DA (or equivalently, suppresses the endpoint contributions to physical observables as a convolution of hard kernel and the light-cone DA), while the higher-order large logarithms of soft gluon momentum tend to suppress the endpoint regions. In total, the DA is enhanced at endpoints after threshold resummation when compared to a fixed-order calculation. In order to check the convergence of perturbation theory after resummation, we vary the hard scale őľhsubscriptūĚúá‚Ąémuh and semi-hard scale őľisubscriptūĚúáūĚĎĖmui by a factor of c=1/2,2ūĚĎź122c=1/sqrt2,sqrt2. When the contribution from large logarithmic terms at higher orders of perturbation theory are significant, the resummed results will become sensitive to the choice of initial scales, indicating that the perturbation theory does not work or converge, so the matched DA is no longer reliable. We show the scale variation for the hard scale őľhsubscriptūĚúá‚Ąémuh and semi-hard scale őľisubscriptūĚúáūĚĎĖmui in Fig. . It is clear from the plot that the results are not very sensitive to the choice of both scales near x=0.5ūĚĎ•0.5x=0.5, but when approaching the endpoints, the scale dependence becomes very large, indicating that the perturbation theory breaks down. From our data, we have the largest pion momentum of Pz‚Čą1.85subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß1.85Pzapprox 1.85GeV. The corresponding reliable range of prediction is estimated to be x‚ąą[0.25,0.75]ūĚĎ•0.250.75xin[0.25,0.75]. To extend the range of the LaMET prediction, we have to go to higher hadron momentum. Since the range is determined by x‚ÄčPzūĚĎ•subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßxPz and x¬Į‚ÄčPz¬ĮūĚĎ•subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßbarxPz, with Pz=3.0subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß3.0Pz=3.0GeV data we could be able to reach xmin‚Čą0.15subscriptūĚĎ•min0.15xrm minapprox 0.15. To show that the reliable range does depend on the hadron momentum, we compare the results with Pz‚Čą1.6subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß1.6Pzapprox 1.6GeV in Fig. . With a smaller momentum, the scale variation grows faster, thus reduces the reliable range of data, suggesting a similar size of xmin‚ÄčPzsubscriptūĚĎ•minsubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßxrm minPz. We also notice that the results in x‚ąą[0.3,0.7]ūĚĎ•0.30.7xin[0.3,0.7] are consistent for the two momenta, suggesting that the power correction is well-controlled here. In our final result, we consider the systematic error from the choice of zs‚ąą[2‚Äča,3‚Äča]subscriptūĚĎßūĚĎ†2ūĚĎé3ūĚĎézsin[2a,3a] in the hybrid renormalization scheme, the choice of őĽtail=8.5,11subscriptūĚúÜtail8.511lambdarm tail=8.5,11 where we start extrapolating the long tail of the coordinate space correlation functions, whether we include an exponential decaying mode in the long-tail extrapolation, and what factor c‚ąą2‚ąí1,1,2ūĚĎźsuperscript2112cinsqrt2-1,1,sqrt2 to use in the hard scale and semi-hard scale őľi,h‚Üíc‚Äčőľi,h‚ÜísubscriptūĚúáūĚĎĖ‚ĄéūĚĎźsubscriptūĚúáūĚĎĖ‚Ąémui,hto cmui,h. For each of these choices, we obtain Ōēi‚Äč(x)subscriptitalic-ŌēūĚĎĖūĚĎ•phii(x) with a statistical error band. The systematic error band is obtained by requiring the total error band to cover all Ōēi‚Äč(x)subscriptitalic-ŌēūĚĎĖūĚĎ•phii(x) results. Figure shows the relative uncertainties as a function of xūĚĎ•x. We find that the statistical error x=0.5ūĚĎ•0.5x=0.5 is őīstat‚ÄčŌē‚Äč(0.5)‚Čą4%superscriptūĚõŅstatitalic-Ōē0.5percent4deltarm statphi(0.5)approx 4%, when including the scale variation, the error increases to őīstat+scale‚ÄčŌē‚Äč(0.5)‚Čą6%superscriptūĚõŅstatscaleitalic-Ōē0.5percent6deltarm stat+scalephi(0.5)approx 6%, and the total systematic error when further including the coordinate-space extrapolation and the difference choices of zssubscriptūĚĎßūĚĎ†zs is őīstat+sys‚ÄčŌē‚Äč(0.5)‚Čą8%superscriptūĚõŅstatsysitalic-Ōē0.5percent8deltarm stat+sysphi(0.5)approx 8%. When approaching the endpoints, the results are dominated by the scale variations, indicating that the perturbation theory becomes unreliable in this region. Noticing the rapid increase of statistical uncertainty when approaching the endpoint, we truncate the result when the systematic uncertainty increases to 10%percent1010%. This corresponding to a range of x‚ąą[0.25,0.75]ūĚĎ•0.250.75xin[0.25,0.75], within which LaMET is able to make reliable prediction with controllable systematics. The same procedure is applied to O3=ő≥z‚Äčő≥5subscriptūĚĎā3subscriptūĚõĺūĚĎßsubscriptūĚõĺ5O3=gammazgamma5 as well. We show the two results and their comparison in Fig. . Both results suggest a flat DA in the mid-xūĚĎ•x region. Comparing the results of two different operators, we find they are consistent within errors.
content_sections.IV Matching to the light-cone DA IV.3 Co: We compare our final results on DWF ensembles with a similar calculation based on HISQ ensembles at physical quark massescitebib.bib19, bib.bib91 in Fig. . The lattice spacing aHISQ=0.076subscriptūĚĎéHISQ0.076arm HISQ=0.076fm is similar to that in this work, and the momentum Pz=1.78subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß1.78Pz=1.78GeV is close to the largest one Pz=1.85subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß1.85Pz=1.85GeV in this calculation. The DWF results are slightly flatter when compared to the HISQ results, and the distribution at x=0.5ūĚĎ•0.5x=0.5 is ŌēDWF‚Äč(0.5)=1.07‚Äčsuperscriptitalic-ŌēDWF0.51.079phirm DWF(0.5)=1.07, lower than the HISQ result ŌēHISQ‚Äč(0.5)=1.19‚Äčsuperscriptitalic-ŌēHISQ0.51.198phirm HISQ(0.5)=1.19. We also take a ratio to the central value of the DWF DA to examine the relative deviation. The momentum-space DA on DWF ensembles is suggesting a slightly lower distribution than the HISQ ensemble within 2‚ÄčŌÉ2ūĚúé2sigma deviation. Thus, our calculation suggests that the explicit chiral symmetry breaking effect on the shape of pion DA is potential but not significant. To further distinguish different calculations, it is important to reduce the systematic uncertainty in mid-xūĚĎ•x region, which could be achieved by a more precise measurement of the long-range correlations to constrain the long-tail extrapolation. Meanwhile, this observation is based on the calculation from one lattice spacing, thus the discretization effects is not well-controlled. A more robust conclusion could be drawn after calculating on multiple lattice spacings and extrapolating the results to the continuum.
content_sections.V Conclusion: In this article, we present the first lattice QCD calculation of the xūĚĎ•x-dependence of pion DA with the chirally symmetric DWF fermion action at physical pion mass. For the first time, we reformulate the threshold resummation for the quasi-DA matching kernel, and resum both the ERBL and threshold logarithms with this technique in the perturbative matching to extract the light-cone DA. We analyze two different Dirac structures ő≥t‚Äčő≥5subscriptūĚõĺūĚĎ°subscriptūĚõĺ5gammatgamma5 and ő≥z‚Äčő≥5subscriptūĚõĺūĚĎßsubscriptūĚõĺ5gammazgamma5, both of which approach the light-cone limit ő≥+‚Äčő≥5subscriptūĚõĺsubscriptūĚõĺ5gamma+gamma5 when boosted to infinite momentum Pz‚Üí‚ąě‚ÜísubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßPztoinfty. With the largest pion momentum Pz‚Čą1.85subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß1.85Pzapprox 1.85GeV, we demonstrate that the region x‚ąą[0.25,0.75]ūĚĎ•0.250.75xin[0.25,0.75] is not sensitive to scale variations, where the systematics is under control. Beyond this region, the scale variation becomes much larger, indicating that the perturbative matching is no longer reliable. We also show that increasing the pion momentum could extend the reliable range of xūĚĎ•x. Our results are consistent between the two different Dirac structures. Applying the same analysis method to the quasi-DA from a HISQ ensemble at physical point, we notice that the pion DA from the DWF ensemble is flatter, with a smaller amplitude around x=0.5ūĚĎ•0.5x=0.5. The discrepancy is within 2‚ÄčŌÉ2ūĚúé2sigma, thus are not statistically significant enough to be conclusive. It should be pointed out that our study is limited to one lattice spacing, which cannot estimate the discretization effects until a continuum extrapolation is performed. Extending the future work to finer lattice spacings to examine the results in the continuum limit will give us a more convincing and comprehensive understanding of the chiral symmetry breaking effects on pion structure.

sorted_papers.title:
Hybrid Renormalization for Quasi Distribution Amplitudes of A Light
  Baryon
sorted_papers.abstract:
We develop a hybrid scheme to renormalize quasi distribution amplitudes of a
light baryon on the lattice, which combines the self-renormalization and ratio
scheme. By employing self-renormalization, the UV divergences and linear
divergence at large spatial separations in quasi distribution amplitudes are
removed without introducing extra nonperturbative effects, while making a ratio
with respect to the zero-momentum matrix element can properly remove the UV
divergences in small spatial separations. As a specific application,
distribution amplitudes of the $\Lambda$ baryon made of $uds$ are investigated,
and the requisite equal-time correlators, which define quasi distribution
amplitudes in coordinate space, are perturbatively calculated up to the
next-to-leading order in strong coupling constant $\alpha_s$. These
perturbative equal-time correlators are used to convert lattice QCD matrix
elements to the continuum space during the renormalization process.
Subsequently, quasi distribution amplitudes are matched onto lightcone
distribution amplitudes by integrating out hard modes and the corresponding
hard kernels are derived up to next-to-leading order in $\alpha_s$ including
the hybrid counterterms. These results are valuable in the lattice-based
investigation of the lightcone distribution amplitudes of a light baryon from
the first principles of QCD.

qualitative: The hybrid renormalization scheme combines self-renormalization and the ratio scheme to address UV divergences and linear divergences in quasi distribution amplitudes of light baryons on the lattice.
Self-renormalization removes UV divergences and linear divergences at large spatial separations without introducing nonperturbative effects.
The ratio scheme with respect to the zero-momentum matrix element effectively removes UV divergences at small spatial separations.

The hybrid approach enables a consistent matching of quasi distribution amplitudes to lightcone distribution amplitudes via the integration out of hard modes in the context of Large Momentum Effective Theory (LaMET).
Perturbative calculations of equal-time correlators up to next-to-leading order in őĪ_s are used to connect lattice QCD matrix elements to the continuum, facilitating the renormalization process.
The matching procedure includes hybrid counterterms derived up to next-to-leading order in őĪ_s, ensuring accuracy in the transition from quasi-observables to lightcone observables.
quantitative: Perturbative equal-time correlators are calculated up to next-to-leading order (NLO) in the strong coupling constant őĪ_s.
Hard kernels for the matching of quasi distribution amplitudes to lightcone distribution amplitudes are derived up to NLO in őĪ_s, including contributions from hybrid counterterms.
The hybrid renormalization scheme incorporates both self-renormalization and ratio scheme counterterms, with the latter specifically designed to cancel UV divergences at small spatial separations.
The renormalization process involves converting lattice matrix elements to the continuum using perturbatively computed equal-time correlators at NLO in őĪ_s.
The matching procedure in LaMET framework integrates out hard modes, with the resulting hard kernels including NLO corrections and hybrid counterterm contributions.
