content_sections.1 Introduction 1.2 Strategy for proving: We begin by developing a unified notational framework that is applicable to both lattice and continuum TMDs. This framework brings to the forefront the common underlying structural features of different TMD schemes, allowing one to more easily construct factorization formulae relating them to one another. We can write every lattice and continuum TMD as the product of a proton matrix element (beam function), vacuum matrix element (soft factor), and appropriate renormalization factors. In the cases studied in the literature, we show that the beam function can be expressed as the Fourier transform of a generic, common correlator ő©ő©Omega. Each scheme manifests as a special case of the correlator, encoded by a choice of ő©ő©Omega‚Äôs arguments and of the order in which we take parameter limits needed for proper TMD regularization and renormalization. More details are provided later, and we summarize the choices needed for various schemes in Tables and . Having a unified notational framework is useful for constructing relationships between continuum TMD schemes that can be connected to physical observables, with schemes that can be computed on the lattice. From the structure of the correlator ő©ő©Omega, we observe that the Collins and quasi-TMDs are closely related. To relate these schemes we begin by constructing a new TMD scheme that is intermediate between the quasi and Collins TMDs: the Large-Rapidity (LR) scheme. The LR scheme uses the same ingredients as the Collins scheme, but performs UV renormalization at large but finite Wilson line rapidities. Using Lorentz invariance, we show that the quasi- and LR scheme TMDs are equivalent, up to terms suppressed at large proton rapidity. We then show that we can relate the LR and Collins TMDs simply by a perturbative matching kernel, which is flavor-diagonal and spin-independent for both quarks and gluons. Combining the quasi-to-LR and LR-to-Collins relations leads to eq. . We summarize relationships between a number of common TMD schemes in . This proof is a beautiful application of the fundamental principle underlying LaMETcitebib.bib12, bib.bib13, bib.bib14: partons encode the internal degrees of freedom of a highly energetic hadron, with the hadron momentum limit Pz‚Üí‚ąě‚ÜísuperscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßPztoinfty taken prior to UV renormalization. In contrast, on the lattice one must carry out UV renormalization prior to the large-momentum limit. This different order of limits in an asymptotically free theory like QCD induces a nontrivial matching kernel between a parton observable and its corresponding lattice construction. Peculiarly, in TMDs this noncommutativity of orders of limits appears naturally, since at intermediate steps of the calculation one must regulate so-called rapidity divergencescitebib.bib53, bib.bib50, bib.bib55, bib.bib56, bib.bib57, bib.bib58, bib.bib59, bib.bib60, bib.bib61, bib.bib62, bib.bib63, bib.bib64, usually by choosing to deviate from lightcone kinematics or by introducing an additional regulator on the lightcone. These divergences cancel when combining the hadronic and soft matrix elements, allowing one to take the lightcone limit (or infinite rapidity-regulator limit), but also forcing one to decide whether to take this limit before or after UV renormalization. Many schemes for constructing TMDs take the lightcone limit first, e. g. the Collins schemecitebib.bib53; others, such as the Ji-Ma-Yuan (JMY) scheme, do the oppositecitebib.bib56. Once again, exchanging these orders of limits is a UV effect, and thus induces a nontrivial matching relation between Collins and JMY TMDs. This paper is structured as follows: introduces our new notational framework for defining TMDs. We then provide an overview of the definitions of common physical and lattice TMD schemes. presents the proof of the factorization statement in eq. . We then discuss the physical implications of this proof, in particular the lack of flavor mixing in the matching and utility for calculating ratios of TMDs. We also confirm the momentum evolution RGE equation for the hard matching coefficient, and give a complete solution for it with next-to-leading-logarithmic resummation. From our proof, we also gain intuition for factorization relations between other lattice and continuum TMD schemes, in particular for the MHENS and Collins schemes which differ due to the presence of Wilson line cusp angles in the former that are not in the latter, and by the need for different soft factors in these two TMDs. We make concluding remarks and outline future directions in . In the appendices, we verify our quasi-to-Collins factorization results analytically at one-loop order, present a one-loop comparison of the continuum JMY and Collins TMDs, and also discuss Wilson line self-energies.

The ellipses in eq. denote additional parameters related to UV and rapidity renormalization, whose precise forms are scheme dependent. Note that we suppress indices related to spin-dependent processes and contributions. The literature is rife with schemes for defining TMDs, each of which has different strengths for different types of calculations. This section reviews schemes relevant for lattice studies; in particular, we only discuss schemes based on off-lightcone Wilson lines. Schemes with intrinsically lightlike Wilson linescitebib.bib65, bib.bib58, bib.bib62, bib.bib60, bib.bib63, bib.bib64 are not accessible on a Euclidean lattice, but many are equivalent to the Collins scheme once limits needed to obtain TMD PDFs are taken; see refs. citebib.bib36, bib.bib66 for an overview. Because each scheme in the literature employs its own conventions and notation, in we begin by introducing new unified TMD Lorentz-invariant correlators for which all schemes follow as special cases. Then, we provide definitions of physical and lattice schemes in sections and , respectively.
content_sections.2 Definition of TMDs 2.1 Unified TMD not: A TMD fi/hsubscriptūĚĎďūĚĎĖ‚Ąéfi/h generally contains two pieces: a hadronic matrix element (called the beam function or unsubtracted TMD), which encodes partonic radiation associated with the initial hadrons; as well as a vacuum matrix element (the soft function). These matrix elements involve open and closed staple-shaped Wilson lines, for which we develop a generic notation. (2.11) None of the TMD schemes we study in this paper contains a vector őīőľsuperscriptūĚõŅūĚúádeltamu that is linearly independent of bőľ,Pőľ,superscriptūĚĎŹūĚúásuperscriptūĚĎÉūĚúábmu,Pmu, and ő∑‚ÄčvőľūĚúāsuperscriptūĚĎ£ūĚúáeta vmu; thus, these schemes have six independent Lorentz invariants. However, the quasi and MHENS TMDs do not follow from the same correlator defined with six invariants, since they fix őīőľsuperscriptūĚõŅūĚúádeltamu in different ways, as we will see below. Hence, even if the first six invariants in eq. are fixed to be the same, the two approaches have different values for the last four invariants, and thus the quasi- and MHENS TMDs belong to distinct schemes.

For a hadron moving in the nasubscriptūĚĎõūĚĎéna direction, the JMY quark beam and soft functions are Bqi/hJMY‚Äč(x,b‚ÜíT,őľ,ő∂v)superscriptsubscriptūĚźĶsubscriptūĚĎěūĚĎĖ‚ĄéJMYūĚĎ•subscript‚ÜíūĚĎŹūĚĎáūĚúásubscriptūĚúĀūĚĎ£displaystyle Bqi/hJMY(x,vecbT,mu,zetav) =‚ąęd‚Äčb‚ąí2‚ÄčŌÄ‚Äče‚ąíi‚Äčb‚ąí‚Äč(x‚ÄčP+)‚Äčő©qi/h[ő≥+]‚Äč[b,P,őľ,‚ąí‚ąě‚Äčv,b‚ąí‚Äčnb],absentdsuperscriptūĚĎŹ2ūĚúčsuperscriptūĚĎíisuperscriptūĚĎŹūĚĎ•superscriptūĚĎÉsuperscriptsubscriptő©subscriptūĚĎěūĚĎĖ‚Ąédelimited-superscriptūĚõĺūĚĎŹūĚĎÉūĚúáūĚĎ£superscriptūĚĎŹsubscriptūĚĎõūĚĎŹdisplaystyle=int!fracdb-2pie-ib-(xP+)Omegaqi/h[gamma+]bigl[b,P,mu,-infty v,b-nbbigr],, SJMYR‚Äč(bT,őľ,yA,yB)superscriptsubscriptūĚĎÜJMYūĚĎÖsubscriptūĚĎŹūĚĎáūĚúásubscriptūĚĎ¶ūĚźīsubscriptūĚĎ¶ūĚźĶdisplaystyle SJMYR(bT,mu,yA,yB) =SR‚Äč[b‚üā,őľ,‚ąí‚ąě‚Äčv,‚ąí‚ąě‚Äčv],absentsuperscriptūĚĎÜūĚĎÖsubscriptūĚĎŹperpendicular-toūĚúáūĚĎ£ūĚĎ£displaystyle=SR[bperp,mu,-infty v,-inftytildev],, (2.25) where ő©i/hsubscriptő©ūĚĎĖ‚ĄéOmegai/h and SRsuperscriptūĚĎÜūĚĎÖSR are the renormalized generic correlators in eqs. and . Just as in the Collins scheme, we take bőľ=(0,b‚ąí,b‚üā)superscriptūĚĎŹūĚúá0superscriptūĚĎŹsubscriptūĚĎŹperpendicular-tobmu=(0,b-,bperp) and őī=(0,b‚ąí,0‚üā)ūĚõŅ0superscriptūĚĎŹsubscript0perpendicular-todelta=(0,b-,0perp) (note that this is usually not specified in the literature). The JMY correlator differs from the Collins correlator in eq. by the presence of timelike (v2>0superscriptūĚĎ£20v2>0) rather than spacelike (nB2<0superscriptsubscriptūĚĎõūĚźĶ20nB2<0) Wilson lines. We can perturbatively match the JMY and Collins scheme TMDs, see . JMY gluon TMDs are not explicitly defined in the literature; nonetheless, one can define them in an analogous manner to the quark TMDs through eq. .Finally, we introduce a new continuum TMD scheme, the LR scheme. The LR scheme uses the same beam and soft functions as the Collins scheme, but a different order of carrying out UV renormalization and approaching the lightcone. Specifically, fi/hLR‚Äč(x,b‚ÜíT,őľ,ő∂,yP‚ąíyB)=lim‚ąíyB‚Čę1limŌĶ‚Üí0ZuvLR‚Äč(ŌĶ,őľ,yn‚ąíyB)‚ÄčBi/hC‚Äč(x,b‚ÜíT,ŌĶ,yP‚ąíyB)SCR‚Äč(bT,ŌĶ,2‚Äčyn,2‚ÄčyB),subscriptsuperscriptūĚĎďLRūĚĎĖ‚ĄéūĚĎ•subscript‚ÜíūĚĎŹūĚĎáūĚúáūĚúĀsubscriptūĚĎ¶ūĚĎÉsubscriptūĚĎ¶ūĚźĶsubscriptmuch-greater-thansubscriptūĚĎ¶ūĚźĶ1subscript‚Üíitalic-ŌĶ0superscriptsubscriptūĚĎćuvLRitalic-ŌĶūĚúásubscriptūĚĎ¶ūĚĎõsubscriptūĚĎ¶ūĚźĶsubscriptsuperscriptūĚźĶūĚź∂ūĚĎĖ‚ĄéūĚĎ•subscript‚ÜíūĚĎŹūĚĎáitalic-ŌĶsubscriptūĚĎ¶ūĚĎÉsubscriptūĚĎ¶ūĚźĶsuperscriptsubscriptūĚĎÜūĚź∂ūĚĎÖsubscriptūĚĎŹūĚĎáitalic-ŌĶ2subscriptūĚĎ¶ūĚĎõ2subscriptūĚĎ¶ūĚźĶdisplaystyle fLRi/h(x,vecbT,mu,zeta,yP-yB)=limbeginsubarrayc-yBgg 1endsubarray,limepsilonto 0,ZuvLR(epsilon,mu,yn-yB)fracBCi/h(x,vecbT,epsilon,yP-yB)sqrtSCR(bT,epsilon,2yn,2yB),, (2.26) where ŌĶ‚Üí0‚Üíitalic-ŌĶ0epsilonto 0 implements UV renormalization. The UV counterterm ZuvLRsuperscriptsubscriptūĚĎćuvLRZrm uvLR does not depend on the rapidities in the beam function because the beam function does not involve nontrivial cusp angles, and the renormalization of its staple-shaped Wilson line and quark operators are rapidity-independent. It does however explicitly depend on the Wilson line rapidity difference yn‚ąíyBsubscriptūĚĎ¶ūĚĎõsubscriptūĚĎ¶ūĚźĶyn-yB through the renormalization of the soft function. In particular, the UV renormalization constant ZuvLRsuperscriptsubscriptūĚĎćuvLRZrm uvLR is the product of those of the bare beam function and soft factor. As will be shown in , the bare beam function at large, but finite, (‚ąíyB)subscriptūĚĎ¶ūĚźĶ(-yB) is equal to the quasi-beam function in a hadron state with momentum given by yP‚ąíyBsubscriptūĚĎ¶ūĚĎÉsubscriptūĚĎ¶ūĚźĶyP-yB. Using the auxiliary field formalism of the Wilson lines, one can showcitebib.bib71, bib.bib72, bib.bib37, bib.bib73 that renormalization of the quasi-beam function is multiplicative in coordinate space and independent of the external hadron momentum. Therefore, ZuvLRsuperscriptsubscriptūĚĎćuvLRZrm uvLR is independent of yP‚ąíyBsubscriptūĚĎ¶ūĚĎÉsubscriptūĚĎ¶ūĚźĶyP-yB, and only depends on yn‚ąíyBsubscriptūĚĎ¶ūĚĎõsubscriptūĚĎ¶ūĚźĶyn-yB due to the renormalization of the soft factor. This should be contrasted with the Collins scheme, where ZuvCsuperscriptsubscriptūĚĎćuvCZrm uvrm C depends on ő∂‚ąĚe2‚Äč(yP‚ąíyn)proportional-toūĚúĀsuperscriptūĚĎí2subscriptūĚĎ¶ūĚĎÉsubscriptūĚĎ¶ūĚĎõzetapropto e2(yP-yn), cf. eq. . We note that the renormalized LR scheme TMD depends on yP‚ąíyBsubscriptūĚĎ¶ūĚĎÉsubscriptūĚĎ¶ūĚźĶyP-yB; hence, the limit ‚ąíyB‚Čę1much-greater-thansubscriptūĚĎ¶ūĚźĶ1-yBgg 1 is to be understood as taking (‚ąíyB)subscriptūĚĎ¶ūĚźĶ(-yB) large but finite instead of taking the limit yB‚Üí‚ąí‚ąě‚ÜísubscriptūĚĎ¶ūĚźĶyBto-infty. The LR and Collins TMDs use the same ő∂=(2‚Äčx‚Äčmh‚ÄčeyP‚ąíyn)2ūĚúĀsuperscript2ūĚĎ•subscriptūĚĎö‚ĄésuperscriptūĚĎísubscriptūĚĎ¶ūĚĎÉsubscriptūĚĎ¶ūĚĎõ2zeta=(2xmheyP-yn)2 to encode ynsubscriptūĚĎ¶ūĚĎõyn dependence. We can also view the LR scheme as the JMY scheme defined with spacelike instead of timelike Wilson lines. See for further elaboration on and derivation of LR scheme properties.
content_sections.2 Definition of TMDs 2.3 Lattice TMD mat: Next, we provide a brief overview of TMD functions that are amenable to calculation using lattice QCD. Unlike the continuum schemes in , lattice TMDs are defined using finite-length Wilson lines. We can obtain their matrix elements from paths involving equal-time spacelike Wilson lines.Quasi-TMDs are objects that share the same infrared physics as TMDs, but have finite-length spacelike Wilson lines and are computable on the lattice citebib.bib34, bib.bib36. Therefore, the cusp angles in the quasi-beam function are always trivially ŌÄ/2ūĚúč2pi/2, so that the UV renormalization factor is independent of bzsuperscriptūĚĎŹūĚĎßtildebz and can be pulled out of the Fourier integral in eq. citebib.bib37. This is a key difference to the MHENS scheme discussed in . The construction of the quasi-beam function in eq. is guided by the observation that by boosting the operator and taking |ő∑|‚Üí‚ąě‚ÜíūĚúā|tildeeta|toinfty, one recovers eq. . In contrast, the soft function depends on two almost-lightlike directions and thus cannot be obtained by boosting an equal-time operatorcitebib.bib36; several potential (quasi-)soft functions have been proposedcitebib.bib33, bib.bib34, bib.bib36 despite the fact that these proposals cannot recover eq. under any Lorentz boost. In practice, calculating (quasi-)TMD soft functions poses a significant challenge for the lattice. It is possible to construct the quasi-soft function indirectly through the spacelike meson form factor and quasi-wavefunctioncitebib.bib38; promising first results using this approach have been reported in refs. citebib.bib46, bib.bib47. Prior to this work, the literature has studied different proposals of the quasi-soft function which are constructed from equal-time Wilson linescitebib.bib33, bib.bib34, bib.bib35, bib.bib36, bib.bib38, bib.bib39. The naive quasi-soft function features a rectangle-shaped Wilson loop along the zūĚĎßz direction, SnaiveR‚Äč(bT,a,ő∑)subscriptsuperscriptūĚĎÜūĚĎÖnaivesubscriptūĚĎŹūĚĎáūĚĎéūĚúādisplaystyletildeSRrm naive(bT,a,tildeeta) ‚Č°SR‚Äč[b‚üā,a,ő∑‚Äčz,‚ąíő∑‚Äčz],absentsuperscriptūĚĎÜūĚĎÖsubscriptūĚĎŹperpendicular-toūĚĎéūĚúāūĚĎßūĚúāūĚĎßdisplaystyleequiv SRbigl[bperp,a,tildeetahatz,-tildeetahatzbigr],, (2.33) whose renormalized continuum version with ő∑=‚ąěūĚúātildeeta=infty in the MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlineMS scheme is denoted SnaiveR‚Äč(bT,őľ)subscriptsuperscriptūĚĎÜūĚĎÖnaivesubscriptūĚĎŹūĚĎáūĚúádisplaystyletildeSRrm naive(bT,mu) ‚Č°SR‚Äč[b‚üā,őľ,‚ąě‚Äčz,‚ąí‚ąě‚Äčz]. absentsuperscriptūĚĎÜūĚĎÖsubscriptūĚĎŹperpendicular-toūĚúáūĚĎßūĚĎßdisplaystyleequiv SRbigl[bperp,mu,inftyhatz,-inftyhatzbigr],. (2.34) However, it has been shown at one-loop levelcitebib.bib36 that SnaiveR‚Äč(bT,őľ)subscriptsuperscriptūĚĎÜūĚĎÖnaivesubscriptūĚĎŹūĚĎáūĚúátildeSRrm naive(bT,mu) does not have the correct IR physics for the quasi-TMD to be perturbatively matchable to the Collins TMD. Since integrating over xūĚĎ•x sets P‚čÖb=0‚čÖūĚĎÉūĚĎŹ0Pcdot b=0, the moments can be calculated in a frame where b=(0,0,b‚üā)ūĚĎŹ00subscriptūĚĎŹperpendicular-tob=(0,0,bperp). In this case the beam functions ő©q/h[ő≥+]superscriptsubscriptő©ūĚĎě‚Ąédelimited-superscriptūĚõĺOmegaq/h[gamma+] agree between the MHENS and Collins schemes since we have őī=0ūĚõŅ0delta=0 in both cases. We elaborate on this further in . To calculate the xūĚĎ•x-dependence, one must evaluate eq. for generic P‚čÖb‚Č†0‚čÖūĚĎÉūĚĎŹ0Pcdot bneq 0. In this case, the MHENS and Collins staples are shaped differently, and thus their beam functions are not equivalent. There are two key differences. First, the Collins staple is closed along b‚ąíőī=b‚üāūĚĎŹūĚõŅsubscriptūĚĎŹperpendicular-tob-delta=bperp, and thus its tranverse and longitudinal Wilson line segments are perpendicular to one another. In contrast, the MHENS staple closes along b‚ąíőī=būĚĎŹūĚõŅūĚĎŹb-delta=b, which for v‚čÖb‚Č†0‚čÖūĚĎ£ūĚĎŹ0vcdot bneq 0 induces a (P‚čÖb)‚čÖūĚĎÉūĚĎŹ(Pcdot b)-dependent cusp angle according to eq. . Second, in the frame where b0=0superscriptūĚĎŹ00b0=0, the MHENS staple length is not bzsuperscriptūĚĎŹūĚĎßbz-independent, leading to nontrivial Wilson-line self-energies that depend on bzsuperscriptūĚĎŹūĚĎßbz or P‚čÖb‚čÖūĚĎÉūĚĎŹPcdot b. Overall, this leads to a nontrivial Wilson line renormalization that depends on P‚čÖb‚čÖūĚĎÉūĚĎŹPcdot b which cannot be factored out of the Fourier integral. We demonstrate this at one-loop order in , and discuss the relation between the MHENS and Collins TMDs in depth in .
content_sections.3 Factorization between physical and lat: This section proves the factorization between the quasi- and Collins TMDs, eq. . Our proof of factorization takes two steps, as depicted in : first connecting the quasi-TMD to the intermediate LR scheme, then connecting the LR and Collins schemes. We begin with a bird‚Äôs eye view of various TMD schemes and their relationships to one another. By using the general correlator ő©ő©Omega that we introduced in eq. , in table we see that TMDs take on a similar form in all schemes. Key differences manifest in the order of limits taken to form the TMD, as well as the specific arguments of the beam function correlator ő©ő©Omega. Notably, we can express ő©ő©Omega in terms of Lorentz-invariant combinations of its arguments. Comparing the values that these Lorentz invariants take on in each scheme, as shown in table , provides a useful way of relating different schemes to one another. These tables are central to our proof, which we present in . We discuss implications of our results in .

We now present a proof of the quasi-to-Collins TMD factorization in detail for the unpolarized quark TMD case. The proof of factorization for other leading-twist TMDs follows naturally using the same framework, with only minor, straightforward modifications for gluon TMDs or other spin structures. We also remark that our proof employs dimensional regularization and the same UV regulator for the quasi and LR schemes. We begin our proof in by considering the correlator ő©ő©Omega as a function of Lorentz invariants, and examining the values that these Lorentz invariants take on in various schemes. In , we see that the quasi and LR scheme Lorentz invariants are identical at large proton momenta by evaluating the quasi-TMD in a boosted frame. We thus can move from the quasi to the LR scheme through a large rapidity expansion. In we demonstrate that reversing the renormalization and lightcone limits to go from the LR to the Collins scheme gives rise to a perturbative matching coefficient. The combination of expansion and matching leads to the desired factorization relation.Let us begin by examining the structure of the quasi-TMD. In dimensional regularization, the quark quasi-beam function in eq. reads Bqi/h[őď]‚Äč(x,b‚ÜíT,ŌĶ,ő∑,x‚ÄčPz)superscriptsubscriptūĚźĶsubscriptūĚĎěūĚĎĖ‚Ąédelimited-őďūĚĎ•subscript‚ÜíūĚĎŹūĚĎáitalic-ŌĶūĚúāūĚĎ•superscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßdisplaystyletildeBqi/h[tildeGamma](x,vecbT,epsilon,tildeeta,xtildePz) =Nőď‚Äč‚ąęd‚Äčbz2‚ÄčŌÄ‚Äčei‚Äčbz‚Äč(x‚ÄčPz)‚Äčő©qi/h[őď]‚Äč(b,P,ŌĶ,ő∑‚Äčz,bz‚Äčz),absentsubscriptūĚĎĀőďdsuperscriptūĚĎŹūĚĎß2ūĚúčsuperscriptūĚĎíisuperscriptūĚĎŹūĚĎßūĚĎ•superscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßsuperscriptsubscriptő©subscriptūĚĎěūĚĎĖ‚Ąédelimited-őďūĚĎŹūĚĎÉitalic-ŌĶūĚúāūĚĎßsuperscriptūĚĎŹūĚĎßūĚĎßdisplaystyle=NtildeGammaintfracdtildebz2pi,eitildebz(xtildePz)Omegaqi/h[tildeGamma]bigl(tildeb,tildeP,epsilon,tildeetahatz,tildebzhatzbigr),, (3.1) where bőľ=(0,b‚ÜíT,bz)superscriptūĚĎŹūĚúá0subscript‚ÜíūĚĎŹūĚĎásuperscriptūĚĎŹūĚĎßtildebmu=(0,vecbT,tildebz). (3.19) The geometric length of the soft function Wilson line is twice of that of the quasi-beam function, so that all linear divergences from Wilson line self-energies cancel in eq. .4 Recall that in dimensional regularization considered here, these linear divergences appear as poles in 1/(d‚ąí3)1ūĚĎĎ31/(d-3), and hence are absent in the MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlineMS scheme where only poles in 1/(d‚ąí4)1ūĚĎĎ41/(d-4) are subtracted. Hence, these linear divergences are set to zero for perturbative calculations in the MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlineMS scheme, but it is important to take them into account for a definition amenable to lattice calculations. Since the hadronic matrix elements in eqs. and are related by a boost, we naturally also employ this soft function for the finite-length LR scheme. All the steps in our analysis work equally well for gluons, simply replacing subscripts qi‚Üíg‚ÜísubscriptūĚĎěūĚĎĖūĚĎĒqito g. Thus, this completes our proof of the factorization formula in eq. .

Next, we discuss implications of the factorization relation. In , we show how the factorization implies that in the matching quarks and gluons do not mix, nor do different quark flavors. In we derive the momentum evolution of the quasi-beam function and hard matching coefficient. In we discuss using ratios of quasi-TMDs or quasi-beam functions to extract ratios of TMDs. In we examine implications of our analysis for the MHENS scheme, outlining the additional steps that need to be considered to derive a complete relation to the Collins scheme.Our derivation of the factorization formula has not specified the quark flavor, and the result actually implies that mixings between quarks of different flavors or quarks and gluons do not exist. This lack of mixing is a generic feature for quasi-TMDs of all parton species. The LR and Collins schemes differ only in the order of their ŌĶ‚Üí0‚Üíitalic-ŌĶ0epsilonto 0 and yB‚Üí‚ąí‚ąě‚ÜísubscriptūĚĎ¶ūĚźĶyBto-infty limits. When matching the schemes, mixing between quark and gluon channels could only occur if yB‚Üí‚ąí‚ąě‚ÜísubscriptūĚĎ¶ūĚźĶyBto-infty leads to a UV-divergent counterterm that contracts the flavor indices of the quark fields in the nonlocal bilinear operator. This cannot happen because these quark fields are always spacelike separated, and thus their exchange of intermediate particles in a Feynman diagram is exponentially suppressed and does not generate a new UV divergence. As a result, the yB‚Üí‚ąí‚ąě‚ÜísubscriptūĚĎ¶ūĚźĶyBto-infty limit can only change UV divergences locally at quark-Wilson line vertices and in Wilson line wavefunction renormalization, which leaves the parton flavor intact. In contrast to the quasi-TMDs, the quark quasi-PDFs are defined from bilinear operators with a straight Wilson line along the zūĚĎßz direction. In the infinite boost limit, the spacelike separated quarks will approach the light-cone, thus inducing a nonlocal UV divergence that contracts the quark flavor indices and allows mixing with the gluon quasi-PDFcitebib.bib54. There is another perspective from which we can understand the lack of mixing. The LR and JMY schemes are related to each other by analytic continuation between space- and timelike Wilson lines. Thus, the JMY scheme should factorize similarly to eq. , as we check at one-loop order in . If there were quark-gluon or flavor mixing in the Collins-to-JMY matching, then such mixing would manifest in the TMD factorization formula for the Drell-Yan or SIDIS cross-section in either scheme; but it does not. Therefore, no mixing should occur in the Collins-to-JMY or Collins-to-LR matching. Note that this factorization relation holds for quark and gluon quasi-TMDs with all spin-dependent structurescitebib.bib40, bib.bib49, so we can use it to compute the ratios of spin-dependent TMDs from the quasi-TMDs or quasi-beam functions, an approach that has been proposed and used in refs. citebib.bib27, bib.bib28, bib.bib29, bib.bib30, bib.bib31, bib.bib32. In summary, all quark and gluon quasi-TMDs should satisfy the factorization relation in eq. . This approach of studying ratios was pioneered in the Lorentz-invariant method of Refs. citebib.bib27, bib.bib28 using the MHENS scheme. This has been shown to have great utility for exploring ratios involving an integral over xūĚĎ•x and different spin and flavor choicescitebib.bib28, bib.bib29, bib.bib30, bib.bib31, bib.bib32. We return to discuss the prospects for including renormalization and matching corrections in the MHENS scheme approach in . For quasi-TMDs the ability to more easily calculate ratios of spin dependent structure functions was observed for quark non-singlet distributions in Refs. citebib.bib40, bib.bib41, and occur due to the universality of the quasi-TMD to Collins-TMD matching coefficient. Our result in eq. enable us to extend these observations to all orders in őĪssubscriptūĚõľūĚĎ†alphas, and include singlet quark distributions and gluon distributions. To calculate the ratios in eq. as a function of xūĚĎ•x, one must first compute the matrix elements for the quasi-beam functions at all bzsuperscriptūĚĎŹūĚĎßbz, then take the Fourier transform. Because UV divergences in the bare quasi-beam function matrix elements are bzsuperscriptūĚĎŹūĚĎßbz-independent, they factor out of the Fourier integral. So, in principle we can skip renormalization and matching to the MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlineMS scheme when calculating TMD ratios, if there are no bzsuperscriptūĚĎŹūĚĎßbz-dependent finite operator mixings on the discretized lattice. However, in the presence of such mixings, lattice renormalization is necessary, as studied in Refs. citebib.bib43, bib.bib73. Also, in numerical analyses it can be advantageous to consider the ő∑‚Üí‚ąě‚ÜíūĚúātildeetatoinfty limit separately for the numerator and denominator of eq. separately. Equation reduces to the relation between MHENS beam functions and the moment of the Collins TMDs discussed in earlier literaturecitebib.bib28, bib.bib29, bib.bib30, bib.bib31, bib.bib32 if one sets Cq=1+ūĚí™‚Äč(őĪs)subscriptūĚź∂ūĚĎě1ūĚí™subscriptūĚõľūĚĎ†Cq=1+cal O(alphas), i. e. works to tree level in the matching coefficient. Beyond tree-level, the convolution becomes nontrivial. Nevertheless, one can plug the TMDs from global analysis into eq. to compare with the lattice ratio of the MHENS beam function. It is worth noting that the CS evolution for the ő∂ūĚúĀzeta dependence is multiplicative and independent of xūĚĎ•x, and hence the ratio of Collins TMDs on the RHS is independent of ő∂ūĚúĀzeta as long as the same value of ő∂ūĚúĀzeta is used in the numerator and denominator. By constructing lattice TMD ratios with the same spin structures but different momenta PzsuperscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßtildePz in the numerator and denominator, one can extract the CS kernel ő≥ő∂‚Äč(őľ,bT)subscriptūĚõĺūĚúĀūĚúásubscriptūĚĎŹūĚĎágammazeta(mu,bT)citebib.bib35. Once again one cannot avoid the need to include the matching coefficient whether working in longitudinal momentum or position spacecitebib.bib37. Thus a formula for obtaining ő≥ő∂subscriptūĚõĺūĚúĀgammazeta that does not explicitly rely on a truncation of the őĪssubscriptūĚõľūĚĎ†alphas expansion always requires calculation of the full bzsuperscriptūĚĎŹūĚĎßbz dependence of lattice beam functions. When the series expansion in őĪssubscriptūĚõľūĚĎ†alphas is utilized, a broader range of extraction techniques are possible, see for example Ref. citebib.bib45. Next we consider the use of the MHENS correlator for obtaining xūĚĎ•x-dependent information about Collins TMDs. There are two complications in this case relative to the use of quasi-TMDs. The first is that the MHENS staple-shaped Wilson line path for the beam functions has non-trivial cusp angles ő≥‚Äč(v,b)ūĚõĺūĚĎ£ūĚĎŹgamma(v,b), which from eq. is given by cosh‚Ā°[ő≥‚Äč(v,b)]=¬Īv‚čÖb|v|‚Äč|b|,ūĚõĺūĚĎ£ūĚĎŹplus-or-minus‚čÖūĚĎ£ūĚĎŹūĚĎ£ūĚĎŹdisplaystylecosh[gamma(v,b)]=pmfracvcdot b|v||b|,, (3.42) where the sign is determined by ¬Ī=sign(ő∑)pm=rm sign(tildeeta). For bz‚Č†0superscriptūĚĎŹūĚĎß0bzneq 0 we have v‚čÖb‚Č†0‚čÖūĚĎ£ūĚĎŹ0vcdot bneq 0, and the UV renormalization factor ZuvMHENSsuperscriptsubscriptūĚĎćuvMHENSZrm uvrm MHENS will depend on ő≥‚Äč(v,b)ūĚõĺūĚĎ£ūĚĎŹgamma(v,b) irrespective of whether or not we extrapolate towards infinitely long staples, |ő∑|‚Üí‚ąě‚ÜíūĚúā|tildeeta|toinfty. This complicates the analysis because the UV renormalization is now bzsuperscriptūĚĎŹūĚĎßbz dependent and hence will not cancel in ratios formed from correlators with the same xūĚĎ•x. The second complication for xūĚĎ•x-dependent MHENS calculations is that the length of the Wilson line path becomes bzsuperscriptūĚĎŹūĚĎßbz-dependent, since using eq. we have LstapleMHENSsuperscriptsubscriptūĚźŅstapleMHENSdisplaystyle Lrm staplerm MHENS =2‚Äč|ő∑‚Äčv|+|b|. absent2ūĚúāūĚĎ£ūĚĎŹdisplaystyle=2|tildeetav|+|b|,. (3.43) This implies that if we do a Fourier transform in b‚čÖP‚čÖūĚĎŹūĚĎÉbcdot P to obtain xūĚĎ•x-dependent correlators, then the power law dependence on the staple length does not cancel for ratios taken at finite ő∑ūĚúātildeeta, where the |b|ūĚĎŹ|b| term can not be neglected. content_sections.4 Conclusion: The central focus of this paper was to derive the factorization formula eq. that relates the lattice-calculable quasi-TMD to physical TMD schemes through a simple perturbative matching coefficient. This formula is valid at all orders in őĪssubscriptūĚõľūĚĎ†alphas up to power corrections for any light quark flavor and for gluons. We began our derivation by developing a generalized TMD notational framework applicable to both continuum and lattice TMDs, enabling us to unify various choices used in the literature and thus more easily unpack the relationships between various physical and lattice TMD schemes. Comparing the operator structures and Lorentz invariants appearing in each scheme, we observed a close relation between the Collins and quasi-TMDs. We then constructed a new continuum TMD scheme intermediate between the Collins and quasi-TMDs, which we called the large rapidity (LR) scheme. The LR and Collins schemes differ by the order of UV renormalization and the lightcone limits, so they are related by a perturbative matching in the spirit of LaMET. Meanwhile, using Lorentz invariance we showed that the quasi- and LR TMDs are equivalent. This enabled us to prove the full factorization relation between the Collins and quasi-TMDs. For any quark flavor qisubscriptūĚĎěūĚĎĖqi and for gluons gūĚĎĒg, the relations are fqi/h‚Äč(x,b‚ÜíT,őľ,ő∂,x‚ÄčPz)subscriptūĚĎďsubscriptūĚĎěūĚĎĖ‚ĄéūĚĎ•subscript‚ÜíūĚĎŹūĚĎáūĚúáūĚúĀūĚĎ•superscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßdisplaystyletildefqi/h(x,vecbT,mu,tildezeta,xtildePz) =Cq‚Äč(x‚ÄčPz,őľ)‚Äčexp‚Ā°[12‚Äčő≥ő∂q‚Äč(őľ,bT)‚Äčln‚Ā°ő∂ő∂]‚Äčfqi/h‚Äč(x,b‚ÜíT,őľ,ő∂)+‚Ä¶,absentsubscriptūĚź∂ūĚĎěūĚĎ•superscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßūĚúá12superscriptsubscriptūĚõĺūĚúĀūĚĎěūĚúásubscriptūĚĎŹūĚĎáūĚúĀūĚúĀsubscriptūĚĎďsubscriptūĚĎěūĚĎĖ‚ĄéūĚĎ•subscript‚ÜíūĚĎŹūĚĎáūĚúáūĚúĀ‚Ä¶displaystyle=Cq(xtildePz,mu)expbiggl[frac12gammazetaq(mu,bT)lnfractildezetazetabiggr]fqi/h(x,vecbT,mu,zeta)+ldots,, (4.1) fg/h‚Äč(x,b‚ÜíT,őľ,ő∂,x‚ÄčPz)subscriptūĚĎďūĚĎĒ‚ĄéūĚĎ•subscript‚ÜíūĚĎŹūĚĎáūĚúáūĚúĀūĚĎ•superscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßdisplaystyletildefg/h(x,vecbT,mu,tildezeta,xtildePz) =Cg‚Äč(x‚ÄčPz,őľ)‚Äčexp‚Ā°[12‚Äčő≥ő∂g‚Äč(őľ,bT)‚Äčln‚Ā°ő∂ő∂]‚Äčfg/h‚Äč(x,b‚ÜíT,őľ,ő∂)+‚Ä¶,absentsubscriptūĚź∂ūĚĎĒūĚĎ•superscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßūĚúá12superscriptsubscriptūĚõĺūĚúĀūĚĎĒūĚúásubscriptūĚĎŹūĚĎáūĚúĀūĚúĀsubscriptūĚĎďūĚĎĒ‚ĄéūĚĎ•subscript‚ÜíūĚĎŹūĚĎáūĚúáūĚúĀ‚Ä¶displaystyle=Cg(xtildePz,mu)expbiggl[frac12gammazetag(mu,bT)lnfractildezetazetabiggr]fg/h(x,vecbT,mu,zeta)+ldots,, (4.2) where the ellipses indicate power corrections. The quark matching coefficient CqsubscriptūĚź∂ūĚĎěCq and CS kernel ő≥ő∂qsuperscriptsubscriptūĚõĺūĚúĀūĚĎěgammazetaq are both independent of the quark flavor. The factorization formula has many implications. First, when matching quasi- and continuum TMDs, there is no mixing between quarks and gluons, nor is there flavor mixing. This means that lattice calculations of TMDs for various flavors and for gluons should be easier than anticipated. We confirmed the momentum renormalization-group evolution for the matching coefficient, and solved it to obtain a explicit result at NLL, confirming it agreed with earlier fixed-order results in the literature. Finally, our proof has implications for factorization formulas matching the Lorentz-invariant approach (MHENS scheme) to lattice TMDs. It implies that ratios of MHENS TMDs with b=b‚ÜíTūĚĎŹsubscript‚ÜíūĚĎŹūĚĎáb=vecbT give direct access to information about the ratio of Collins TMDs integrated against the matching coefficient Cq‚Äč(x‚ÄčPz,őľ)subscriptūĚź∂ūĚĎěūĚĎ•superscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßūĚúáCq(xtildePz,mu) in the numerator and denominator. The treatment of factorization for xūĚĎ•x-dependent ratios of MHENS correlators is a bit more complicated, and we identified the additional ingredients as being from cusp anomalous dimensions in the lattice renormalization and the need for a different soft factor subtraction in the relation to the Collins TMD. Our results are an important step in pushing forward the knowledge we can extract from the lattice about TMD observables, yet much remains to be done. We have derived a factorization formula at leading power to all orders in őĪssubscriptūĚõľūĚĎ†alphas. However, the perturbative quasi-to-Collins matching coefficient CqsubscriptūĚź∂ūĚĎěCq is only known at one-loop, except for certain logarithmic terms constrained by its RGE, see . Recently there has been renewed interest in TMDs at subleading power, with for example a derivation of the necessary form of the factorization formula for polarized SIDIS at subleading powercitebib.bib81, bib.bib82. Finding continuum-to-lattice factorization formulas for subleading power TMDs would be interesting. As experiments come online that promise to push our knowledge of hadronic structure to new depths, the need for corresponding first-principles predictions becomes ever more clear. The challenges faced in calculating TMDs on the lattice give us a roadmap for efficiently deriving other key hadronic properties. Constructing a generic operator encompassing all possible physical and lattice scheme choices for a specific distribution is useful for understanding the space of possibilities. Comparing the quasi-to-Collins and MHENS-to-Collins factorizations, we see that we must walk a fine line between perturbative and numerical challenges when choosing a lattice observable. From the lattice standpoint, unless headway is made on the sign problem, matrix element correlators from which distributions are constructed must employ Wilson lines on equal-time paths. Wilson lines should have as few sides and cusps as possible to minimize difficulties with renormalization. From the start, it is also important to account for lattice renormalization, soft function subtractions, and finite Wilson line lengths. One must also be careful with different orders of limits and renormalization, which can often lead to additional perturbative matching kernels. In the case of TMDs specifically, it is clear from the phase space of possible lattice correlators that there are additional freedoms in the definitions that could still be exploited. Quasi-TMDs and MHENS TMDs provide two examples of how things can differ due to these choices, and we have advocated for some of the benefits of the quasi-TMD approach. The calculation of MHENS TMDs has an excellent track record, and it will be important to continue to carry out calculations with various lattice TMDs, and confirm consistency amongst the results. Ultimately, this program has the potential to lead to precise determinations of the full functional dependence of the eight leading-power spin-dependent TMDs.

sorted_papers.title: Euclidean Effective Theory for Partons in the Spirit of Steven Weinberg
sorted_papers.abstract:
The standard formulation of parton physics involves light-cone correlations
of quark and gluon fields in a hadron, which leads to a widespread impression
that it can only be studied through real-time Hamiltonian dynamics or
light-front quantization, which are challenged by non-perturbative computations
with a pertinent regulator for light-cone/rapidity divergences (or zero modes).
As such, standard lattice QCD studies have been limited to indirect parton
observables such as first few moments and short-distance correlations, which do
not provide the $x$-distributions without solving the model-dependent inverse
problem. Here I describe an alternative formulation of partons in terms of
equal-time (or Euclidean) correlators, which allows to compute
precision-controlled $x$-distribution through lattice QCD simulations. This
approach is in accord with Weinberg's pioneering idea of effective field theory
as well as Wilson's renormalization group, in which the large hadron momentum
serves as a natural cut-off for light-cone/rapidity divergences and can
ultimately be eliminated through a method like the ``perfect action'' program
in lattice QCD.

qualitative.statement:
{
  "qualitative": [
    "The paper proposes an alternative formulation of parton physics using equal-time (Euclidean) correlators instead of light-cone correlations, enabling direct computation of parton distribution functions (PDFs) in lattice QCD without relying on light-front quantization.",
    "The approach is grounded in Steven Weinberg's effective field theory (EFT) philosophy, emphasizing scale separation, power counting, and the systematic control of divergences through a small parameter expansion.",
    "Large hadron momentum serves as a natural regulator for light-cone/rapidity divergences, replacing the need for a separate light-cone regulator, and can be systematically removed via the 'perfect action' program in lattice QCD.",
    "This framework aligns with the spirit of Wilson's renormalization group and operator product expansion, where high-energy physics is encoded in Wilson coefficients (calculable in perturbative QCD) and low-energy physics in matrix elements (non-perturbatively computable).",
    "The method avoids the inverse problem associated with indirect lattice observables (e.g., moments or short-distance correlations), allowing for direct access to the $x$-distribution of partons with precision control.",
    "The approach is consistent with the broader EFT paradigm, where the existence and calculability of a theory depend on proper UV regularization and systematic expansion in a small parameter‚ÄĒhere, the inverse of the hadron momentum."
  ],
  "quantitative": [
    "The paper does not present specific numerical results, such as lattice simulation data or extracted PDF values.",
    "No explicit equations for quasi-parton distributions or matching kernels are provided in the given text.",
    "No numerical values for coupling constants, masses, or momentum scales are reported.",
    "The concept of the 'perfect action' program is mentioned as a method to eliminate the large momentum cutoff, but no specific action form or lattice spacing dependence is given.",
    "The paper references the use of large hadron momentum $P^z$ as a regulator, but no specific value or scaling relation (e.g., $P^z \gg \\Lambda_{\\text{QCD}}$) is quantified in the excerpt."
  ]
}

authors: Xiangdong Ji

content_sections.I Weinberg and the Rise of Effective Fie: Having established the electroweak unification theory of elementary particles based on the renormalizability constraintcitebib.bib1, Steven Weinberg reversed his attitude toward renormalization, and argued that non-renormalizable terms in a lagrangian can still have field-theoretical significance so long as their divergences can be controlled through a systematic expansion of some small parameters (power counting). If the short-distance/large-momentum contributions can be lumped into renormalized constants of finite numbers in a given order (of power counting), the long-distance quantum fluctuations can still be calculated and tested through combinations of experimental observables, independent of these constants. The case in point is chiral effective field theory (EFT) of strong interactions in which low-energy meson and hadron structure and scattering physics can be calculated in terms of a fixed number of ‚Äúlow-energy‚ÄĚ constants encoding ultraviolet (UV) physics at a fixed order, which can in turn be determined by fitting to experimental datacitebib.bib2, bib.bib3, bib.bib4. A similar approach has been advocated by Weinberg to study nuclear physics, known as nuclear EFT, replacing nuclear models which have been the main-stream approach since the beginning of the field citebib.bib5, bib.bib6, bib.bib7, bib.bib8. Wilson‚Äôs renormalization group and operator expansion developed for high-energy scattering is a similar factorization of physics at different scales into local operators and coefficient functions, in which the high-energy physics encoded in the Wilson coefficients can actually be calculated in quantum chromodyanamics (QCD) due to asymptotic freedom, whereas the infraed (IR) physics in operator matrix elements can be calculated non-perturbatively through low-energy QCDcitebib.bib9, bib.bib10. This can be formulated as an application of EFT, but the exact meaning of which depends on one‚Äôs focus: For high-energy physicists, perturbative QCD is an EFT with low-energy physics parametrized as ‚Äúhigh-energy constants‚ÄĚ in the sense of Weinberg. For the hadron structure theorists, non-perturbative QCD is the EFT after integrating out the high-energy degrees of freedom in hard-scattering cross sections. Weinberg had been perhaps my greatest physics hero throughout my scientific career. He was a rationalist, giving others the illusion that if we work ten times as hard as we do, we can become another Weinberg. I first heard about him when I was a graduate student at Peking University in 1982 when one of my classmates was reading his famous book on ‚ÄúGravitation and Cosmology. ‚ÄĚ My first real life encounter at a distance was when I was a junior faculty at MIT at a workshop on chiral dynamics in June 1994, in which he gave a talk about his EFT approach to nuclear physics. It was striking to me at that time that a renowned theorist like him was still interested in century-old problems of nuclear forces and pion scattering etc, which seemed to have gone out of fashion even in nuclear physics. The most memorable face-to-face conversation was when he came to U. Maryland to give a nuclear seminar around the turn of the century in which he talked about the EFT approach to Nambu-Jona-Lasino type of models. Again, his presentation was so logical and persuasive that if someone just follows the nose, he/she would have obtained the same results, a sense of ‚Äúinevitableness‚ÄĚ in his frequently-used terms. Weinberg was at one time the most-cited physicist in the world. Yet it was interesting that during his visit at UMD, he complained that his work on ‚Äúmended symmetry‚ÄĚ seemed to have been ignored by otherscitebib.bib11. He described it as ‚ÄĚa child that has not grown so well. " Over the years, I had a number of email communications with him for various reasons, and what impressed me was that he always replied to emails promptly and never left them unanswered, which is quite rare among Nobel Laureates. Half-century passed, EFT has become a most basic and yet a most essential tool of theoretical physics (see, for example, citebib.bib12, bib.bib13). Many EFTs have been estalished, but the spirit is same: scale separation, power counting, and inevitableness of results as consequences of general principles given the relevant degrees of freedom and symmetries. While some EFTs are invented to make calculations easier, like non-relativistic quantum electrodynamics (NRQED), others are essential to establish the existence of very theories and their calculability. In the latter case, we mention the renormalization program itself, i. e. , finding proper UV regulators of quantum field theories, without which the latter are not well-defined. In electro-weak theory (also in perturbative QCD), dimensional regularization has played the key role for performing high-precision perturbative calculations. For low-energy QCD, on the other hand, lattice regularization is essential to define the theory non-perturbatively, which is an EFT of the continuum theory through perfect actionscitebib.bib14, bib.bib15. Similarly, finding a non-perturbative light-cone regulator is essential to solving the EFT for parton dynamics.
content_sections.II QCD Factorization and Parton as EFT O: Asymptotic freedom of QCD allows calculating physics at large momentum scales in standard Feynman perturbation theory. However, no physical observables involve only perturbative physics: non-perturbative physics at scale őõQCDsubscriptőõQCDLambdarm QCD is always present in any physical processes. Therefore, in general, one can perform scale separation so that high-momentum physics is treated perturbatively, whereas the IR physics is parametrized by non-perturbative matrix elements. Schematically, one can decompose, for example, a quark field into Ōą‚Äč(x)=Ōąhigh‚Äč(x)+Ōąlow‚Äč(x),ūĚúďūĚĎ•subscriptūĚúďhighūĚĎ•subscriptūĚúďlowūĚĎ•psi(x)=psirm high(x)+psirm low(x) , and the low-energy dynamics is represented by matrix elements of ŌąlowsubscriptūĚúďlowpsirm low‚Äôs. The application of perturbative QCD (pQCD) can be viewed as an example of Weinberg‚Äôs EFT with high/low energy calculability switched. Compared with chiral perturbative theory, however, pQCD has two major differences: 1) Due to light-cone dominance in high-energy processes, power counting is not the same as dimensional counting. Instead one has now the so-called twist counting (see for example citebib.bib16). The result is an infinite number of ‚Äúhigh-energy constants‚ÄĚ which are functions of hadron momentum fraction xūĚĎ•x and parameters, forming sundry light-cone/parton distribution functions (PDFs). 2) Apart from determining these constants from (global analyses of) experimental data (for example citebib.bib17, bib.bib18), they are in principle calculable directly in low-energy QCD. To phenomenologically fix an infinity number of high-energy constants or a parton distribution function (PDF) needs in principle an infinite number of data. However, we do not have infinite or even systematically discretized data sets covering all phase space, and therefore it is often labelled as ‚Äúinverse problem‚ÄĚ in the literature, meaning no unique solution is possible. One can find a set of probable solutions so that known data are well-described. A general strategy is to assume specific correlations between the infinite number of constants or that PDFs have some guessed forms of xūĚĎ•x dependencecitebib.bib18. Similar approaches have been used to fit the first few moments and/or short-distance correlations from lattice QCD with model functions. However, these approaches do not allow fixing PDFs at any particular xūĚĎ•x with rigorously-quantifiable systematic error. Therefore, it is imperative to find a way to calculate at least some of ‚Äúhigh-energy constants‚ÄĚ with controlled systematics, which in turn requires a better understanding of the physics content of partons. Using the EFT approach, one can integrate out ŌąhighsubscriptūĚúďhighpsirm high first, and the remaining low-energy theory involves then Ōąlow‚Äč(x)subscriptūĚúďlowūĚĎ•psirm low(x) only. As has been known since the 1970‚Äôs, PDFs are Fourier transformations of the gauge-invariant matrix elements of the light-cone correlations of Ōąlow‚Äč(x)subscriptūĚúďlowūĚĎ•psirm low(x), and these degrees of freedom are Feynman‚Äôs partons. There are two apparently-different formalisms for parton physics that are equivalent. In the first one, a hadron can have any three-momentum (in particular in the rest frame P‚Üí=0‚ÜíūĚĎÉ0vecP=0), and collinear fields with coordinates along the light-cone directions are probed. There are also soft degrees of freedom which cannot be distinguished from the collinear fields at x=0ūĚĎ•0x=0. In perturbative calculations, the light-cone correlators will generate additional divergences beyond the usual ones. An effective lagrangian can be explicitly introduced to describe the dynamics of the soft and collinear fields. The resulting EFT is called soft-collinear effective theory (or SCET). For example, the leading effective lagrangian for the collinear quark fieldscitebib.bib19, bib.bib20 is, ‚ĄíSCETsubscript‚ĄíSCETdisplaystylecal Lrm SCET =Ōą¬Įk(x)[in¬Į‚čÖDdisplaystyle=overlinepsik(x)left[ibarncdot Dright. where n¬Į=(1,0,0,1)¬ĮūĚĎõ1001barn=(1,0,0,1) and n=(1,0,0,‚ąí1)ūĚĎõ1001n=(1,0,0,-1) are conjugate light-cone vectors. The hadron is moving along positive zūĚĎßz-direction, and kőľ=x‚Äčn¬Įőľ+k‚üāőľsuperscriptūĚĎėūĚúáūĚĎ•superscript¬ĮūĚĎõūĚúásuperscriptsubscriptūĚĎėperpendicular-toūĚúákmu=xbarnmu+kperpmu and k‚Ä≤‚Ā£őľ=x‚Ä≤‚Äčn¬Įőľ+k‚üā‚Ä≤‚Ā£őľsuperscriptūĚĎė‚Ä≤ūĚúásuperscriptūĚĎ•‚Ä≤superscript¬ĮūĚĎõūĚúásuperscriptsubscriptūĚĎėperpendicular-to‚Ä≤ūĚúákprimemu=xprimebarnmu+kperpprimemu are momentum labels of collinear quark fields (we have not factored out the rapid oscillating phases here). Similar terms can be added for the collinear gluon fields, as well as soft fields. Note that due to the inverse differential operator 1/i‚Äčn‚čÖD‚čÖ1ūĚĎĖūĚĎõūĚź∑1/incdot D (corresponding to a Wilson line), the lagrangian density is non-local and simplifies when choosing n‚čÖA=0‚čÖūĚĎõūĚźī0ncdot A=0 gauge. The collinear PDF‚Äôs are the matrix elements of the light-cone correlators of the collinear fields. When calculating them in covariant perturbation theory, dimensional regularization is sufficient for divergences from light-like separations. An explicit light-cone regulator has to be introduced for transverse-momentum dependent quantities. There are a number of proposals for light-cone regulators for perturbative calculations in the literature (see a summary in citebib.bib21). When working to all orders in perturbation theory, a perturbative regulator is sufficient for defining a scheme for non-perturative matrix elements. Calculating non-perturbative parton physics with SCET has not been attempted so far, but explicit time-dependence is clearly a key obstacle. An older formulation of parton EFT is based on the so-called light-front quantizationcitebib.bib22, bib.bib23, in which the Hamiltonian H‚ąěsubscriptūĚźĽHinfty and Fock space can also be derived in the infinite momentum limit of time-independent (old-fashioned) perturbation theorycitebib.bib24. In this case, both observables and hadron states are constructed out of collinear particles with one infinite-momentum direction, referred to as parton degrees of freedom. All others with finite momentum can be lumped into zero modes. To keep light-cone non-locality simple, it is standard to choose the light-cone gauge n‚čÖA=0‚čÖūĚĎõūĚźī0ncdot A=0. However, H‚ąěsubscriptūĚźĽHinfty is still a non-local operator of light-cone distributed fields, which has various light-cone divergences. Compared with SCET, there are extra light-cone divergences from the light-cone gauge as well as splitting of covariant Feynman diagrams into light-cone-time-ordered ones. Renormalizing these divergences properly for non-perturbative calculations has been a great challenge, because it is very difficult to restore all symmetries that are present before the infinite momentum limit is taken. It is fair to say that no satisfying light-cone regulator has been found for light-front QCD, which hinders any attempt to calculate PDF‚Äôs from first principles. Light-front quantization can formally be obtained from integrating out the energy or time in the ordinary or SCET Feynman diagrams.
content_sections.III Factorization of DIS in terms of Euc: To develop a practical method for computation of parton physics, we will demonstrate, using the example of well-known deep-inelastic scattering (DIS), that high-energy processes can be factorized in terms of Euclidean correlators of collinear fields, which means that partons can be formulated in Euclidean field theory. To do so, we use the so-called Bjorken (or Breit) frame in which the virtual photon momentum has only a non-vanishing zūĚĎßz component and the hadron is moving with velocity vőľsuperscriptūĚĎ£ūĚúávmu in the same direction. In the above discussion, we arrived at an EFT for low-energy QCD in high-energy processes, which contains only equal time (Euclidean) correlators of collinear fields of velocity vūĚĎ£v. The leading effective lagrangian for the quark collinear modes can be written as, ‚Ąíq,v=Ōą¬Įv‚Äč[i‚Äčv‚čÖD+i‚Äčv¬Į‚čÖD2‚Äčő≥2+(i‚ÄčD‚üā)‚Äč12‚Äči‚Äčv¬Į‚čÖD‚Äč(i‚ÄčD‚üā)]‚Äčv¬Į‚ÄčŌąvsuperscriptsubscript‚ĄíūĚĎěūĚĎ£0subscript¬ĮūĚúďūĚĎ£delimited-‚čÖūĚĎĖūĚĎ£ūĚź∑‚čÖūĚĎĖ¬ĮūĚĎ£ūĚź∑2superscriptūĚõĺ2ūĚĎĖsubscriptūĚź∑perpendicular-to1‚čÖ2ūĚĎĖ¬ĮūĚĎ£ūĚź∑ūĚĎĖsubscriptūĚź∑perpendicular-to¬ĮūĚĎ£subscriptūĚúďūĚĎ£cal Lq,v=overlinepsivleft[ivcdot D+fracibarvcdot D2gamma2+(iDperp)frac12ibarvcdot D(iDperp)right]notbarvpsiv where v¬Į=(v,0,0,‚ąí1)/2‚Äčv¬ĮūĚĎ£ūĚĎ£0012ūĚĎ£barv=(v,0,0,-1)/2v and vőľ‚Äčv¬Įőľ=1subscriptūĚĎ£ūĚúásuperscript¬ĮūĚĎ£ūĚúá1vmubarvmu=1. One can also add the leading-order lagrangian for the gluon collinear modes. This effective theory formally converges to SCET or light-front quantization in the v‚Üíc‚ÜíūĚĎ£ūĚĎźvto c limit. However, there are some fundamental differences which we summarized below: ‚ÄĘ Momentum evolution equation: The momentum distribution is frame-dependent and the large momentum dependence follows a renormalization group equationcitebib.bib25. But it has no light-cone divergences. In a certain sense, frame-dependence is a way to regulate the light-cone divergences which arise from interchanging the orders of the limits of Pz‚Üí‚ąě‚ÜísuperscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßPztoinfty and őõU‚ÄčV‚Üí‚ąě‚ÜísubscriptőõūĚĎąūĚĎČLambdaUVtoinfty. This exchange is not legal in case of non-trivial UV properties. The limit Pz‚Üí‚ąě‚ÜísuperscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßPztoinfty first ignoring the UV cutoff is the standard light-front/SCET approach which leads to extra divergences hard to regularize for non-perturbative calculations respecting all symmetries. In our present approach, the UV cut-off őõU‚ÄčVsubscriptőõūĚĎąūĚĎČLambdaUV is larger than any scale in the problem including PzsuperscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßPz, and the light-cone divergences are avoided. However, due to asymptotic freedom of QCD, the two orders of limits differ only in perturbation theory but are equivalent for non-perturbative physics. The PzsuperscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßPz evolution for the second limit is equivalent to the renormalization group (RG) evolution of light-cone regularization for the first limit. In the case of collinear factorization, the resulting momentum evolutions are similar to the standard RG equations for PDFs. When the transverse momentum is involved, the momentum evolution is related to the rapidity evolution in light-cone theory. Therefore, one can directly measure the momentum distributions at a particular PzsuperscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßPz through a global analysis similar to that for PDFs. ‚ÄĘ Non-perturbative light-cone regulator: The new form of EFT has a velocity/momentum regulator for light-cone divergencies, which can be used for any non-perturbative calculations. Through SCET, one can match it with other existing perturbative regulators. ‚ÄĘ A Euclidean theory: the high-energy constants or momentum distributions of the above theory are not time-dependent. Therefore, all calculations with Eq. can be analytically continued to Euclidean time, and can be simulated, in particular, through lattice QCD. We now have a mature method to calculate these non-perturbative properties of hadrons. The above theory may be properly called large-velocity/rapidity EFT because formally the power counting is done in 1/ő≥1ūĚõĺ1/gamma and 1/v¬Į‚čÖD‚čÖ1¬ĮūĚĎ£ūĚź∑1/barvcdot D. However, in practical calculations, it is the inverse hadron momentum PzsuperscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßPz appears in a power expansion (M2/(Pz)2superscriptūĚĎÄ2superscriptsuperscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß2M2/(Pz)2 and (1/x‚ÄčPz)2superscript1ūĚĎ•superscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß2(1/xPz)2), and for this reason, it has been named as large-momentum effective theory (LaMET)citebib.bib25.
content_sections.IV Expansion of Partons in Terms of Eucl: To summarize the above, the standard EFT of pQCD uses the light-cone formulation for partons, which has light-cone singularities and is Minkowskian in the sense that the time-dependence is essential, and thus is difficult to implement for non-perturbative calculations. The alternative factorization leads to Euclidean correlators of collinear fields, and has a natural regulator amenable for non-perturbative calculations. Since these two EFTs are entirely equivalent for non-perturbative physics, it is natural to connect them to arrive at a LaMET expansion for parton physicscitebib.bib26. In fact, all light-cone distributions properly defined in some perturbative UV and rapididty regularization scheme can be expanded in terms of generalized momentum distributions, in a way similar to PDFs, f‚Äč(x,őľ)=‚ąę‚ąí‚ąě‚ąěd‚Äčyy‚ÄčC‚Äč(y/x,Pz/őľ)‚Äčf‚Äč(y,Pz). ūĚĎďūĚĎ•ūĚúásubscriptsuperscriptūĚĎĎūĚĎ¶ūĚĎ¶ūĚź∂ūĚĎ¶ūĚĎ•superscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßūĚúáūĚĎďūĚĎ¶superscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßf(x,mu)=intinfty-inftyfracdyyC(y/x,Pz/mu)tildef(y,Pz) . in the Pz‚Üí‚ąě‚ÜísuperscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßPztoinfty limit. This is simple to see for perturbative quark/gluon states, in which both fūĚĎďf and fūĚĎďtildef can be calculated to all orders in perturbation theory and their infrared structures are exactly the same. The only difference is in the UV and can be matched up by the perturbative Wilson coefficient, CūĚź∂C. Operationally, CūĚź∂C can be obtained from a forest formula similar to the BPHZ renormalization processcitebib.bib27, bib.bib28.

A salient feature of EFTs is to calculate physical observables in a systematic, controlled expansion with some well-defined constants, not just in the Pz‚Üí‚ąě‚ÜísuperscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßPztoinfty limit, but also at finite fixed PzsuperscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßPz. Due to the non-locality of the LaMET, power corrections are arranged by twists of local operators and usually involve more complicated multi-field equal-time correlation functions. Twist-3 corrections to twist-2 parton distributions come from the Wilson line self-energy and can be taken into account through leading renormalon resummationcitebib.bib29. Twist-4 corrections are dynamical. A typical twist-4 distribution iscitebib.bib30 f‚Äč(y1,y2,y3,Pz)=‚ąę‚ąí‚ąě‚ąěūĚĎĎz1‚ÄčūĚĎĎz2‚ÄčūĚĎĎz3ūĚĎďsubscriptūĚĎ¶1subscriptūĚĎ¶2subscriptūĚĎ¶3superscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßsubscriptsuperscriptdifferential-dsubscriptūĚĎß1differential-dsubscriptūĚĎß2differential-dsubscriptūĚĎß3displaystyletildef(y1,y2,y3,Pz)=intinfty-inftydz1dz2dz3 √ó‚ü®P|Ōą¬Į‚Äč(z1)‚Äčő≥z‚ÄčW‚Äč(z1,z2)‚ÄčŌą‚Äč(z2)‚ÄčŌą¬Į‚Äč(z3)‚Äčő≥z‚ÄčW‚Äč(z3,0)‚ÄčŌą‚Äč|P‚ü©absentquantum-operator-productūĚĎÉ¬ĮūĚúďsubscriptūĚĎß1superscriptūĚõĺūĚĎßūĚĎäsubscriptūĚĎß1subscriptūĚĎß2ūĚúďsubscriptūĚĎß2¬ĮūĚúďsubscriptūĚĎß3superscriptūĚõĺūĚĎßūĚĎäsubscriptūĚĎß30ūĚúď0ūĚĎÉdisplaystyletimes P|overlinepsi(z1)gammazW(z1,z2)psi(z2)overlinepsi(z3)gammazW(z3,0)psi|P which is an equal-time, gauge-invariant correlation function along the zūĚĎßz-direction. In the Weinberg‚Äôs sense of an EFT, the parton observables can be treated as ‚Äúphysical quantities", and unknown constants are momentum distributions or similar equal-time correlation functions, which can be computed through lattice QCD simulations. Thus, one arrives at a full expansion formula for PDFs at fixed PzsuperscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßPz, f‚Äč(x,őľ)=‚ąę‚ąí‚ąě‚ąěd‚Äčyy‚ÄčC2‚Äč(yx,Pzőľ)‚Äčf‚Äč(y,Pzőľ)ūĚĎďūĚĎ•ūĚúásubscriptsuperscriptūĚĎĎūĚĎ¶ūĚĎ¶subscriptūĚź∂2ūĚĎ¶ūĚĎ•superscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßūĚúáūĚĎďūĚĎ¶superscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßūĚúádisplaystyle f(x,mu)=intinfty-inftyfracdyyC2left(fracyx,fracPzmuright)tildefleft(y,fracPzmuright) +(őõQCDPz)2‚Äč‚ąĎi‚ąę‚ąí‚ąě‚ąěd‚Äčy1y1‚Äčd‚Äčy2y2‚Äčd‚Äčy3y3‚ÄčC4‚Äči‚Äč(y1x,y2x,y3x,Pzőľ)superscriptsubscriptőõQCDsuperscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß2subscriptūĚĎĖsubscriptsuperscriptūĚĎĎsubscriptūĚĎ¶1subscriptūĚĎ¶1ūĚĎĎsubscriptūĚĎ¶2subscriptūĚĎ¶2ūĚĎĎsubscriptūĚĎ¶3subscriptūĚĎ¶3subscriptūĚź∂4ūĚĎĖsubscriptūĚĎ¶1ūĚĎ•subscriptūĚĎ¶2ūĚĎ•subscriptūĚĎ¶3ūĚĎ•superscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßūĚúádisplaystyle+left(fracLambdarm QCDPzright)2sumiintinfty-inftyfracdy1y1fracdy2y2fracdy3y3C4ileft(fracy1x,fracy2x,fracy3x,fracPzmuright) √ófi‚Äč(y1,y2,y3,Pzőľ)+‚Ä¶absentsubscriptūĚĎďūĚĎĖsubscriptūĚĎ¶1subscriptūĚĎ¶2subscriptūĚĎ¶3superscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßūĚúá‚Ä¶displaystyletimestildefileft(y1,y2,y3,fracPzmuright)+. . . Technically, all power corrections have power divergences and the subtractions must be defined consistently with regularization of the IR renormalon poles in the twist-2 C2subscriptūĚź∂2C2citebib.bib31, bib.bib32. Therefore, the xūĚĎ•x-dependence of PDFs can be calculated with systematically-controlled precision from the above equation. There is no inverse problem as in parton phenomenology and short-distance expansion where the xūĚĎ•x-dependence is assumed to be a combination of some model functions.

Continuum field theories generally have UV singularities, and are not well-defined unless there is a proper cut-off. The well-known dimensional regularization works only for perturbative calculations in which Feynman integrals can be performed in D=4‚ąíŌĶūĚź∑4italic-ŌĶD=4-epsilon dimension. Lattice QCD regulates the UV divergences by replacing space-time with a lattice of spacing aūĚĎéa. All IR degrees of freedoms are kept but UV degrees of freedoms with momentum larger than ŌÄ/aūĚúčūĚĎépi/a are cut off and recovered in the continuum a‚Üí0‚ÜíūĚĎé0ato 0 limit. Alternatively, one may consider the UV degrees of freedoms to have been integrated out in perturbation theory according to Wilson‚Äôs renormalization group method. The result is a Symanzik‚Äôs lattice effective theory with an lagrangiancitebib.bib14, bib.bib15, ‚ĄíeffQCD=C0‚Äč(őĪ‚Äč(a))‚Äč‚Ąía+‚ąĎi=1‚ąěai‚ÄčCi‚Äč(őĪ‚Äč(a))‚Äč‚Ąía(i),superscriptsubscript‚ĄíeffQCDsubscriptūĚź∂0ūĚõľūĚĎésubscriptsuperscript‚Ąí0ūĚĎésuperscriptsubscriptūĚĎĖ1superscriptūĚĎéūĚĎĖsubscriptūĚź∂ūĚĎĖūĚõľūĚĎésubscriptsuperscript‚ĄíūĚĎĖūĚĎécal Lrm effrm QCD=C0(alpha(a))cal La+sumi=1inftyaiCi(alpha(a))cal L(i)a , where ‚Ąísuperscript‚Ąí0cal L is the naive discretized lagrangian density from the continuum one. ‚Ąí(i)superscript‚ĄíūĚĎĖcal L(i) are higher dimensional operators generating norminal power corrections. This ‚Äúperfect action‚ÄĚ will eliminated any lattice-spacing dependence in a physical observable. Similarly, the v‚Üíc‚ÜíūĚĎ£ūĚĎźvto c limit of parton theory is not well defined due to light-cone divergences. But, one can work at a fixed large vūĚĎ£v as a cut-off. Physics missing between the cut-off and light-cone can be regained through a full LaMET expansion of the effective lagrangian, ‚ĄíeffParton=C0‚Äč(őĪ)‚Äč‚Ąív+‚ąĎi=1‚ąěő≥‚ąíi‚ÄčCi‚Äč(őĪ)‚Äč‚Ąív(i). superscriptsubscript‚ĄíeffPartonsubscriptūĚź∂0ūĚõľsubscriptsuperscript‚Ąí0ūĚĎ£superscriptsubscriptūĚĎĖ1superscriptūĚõĺūĚĎĖsubscriptūĚź∂ūĚĎĖūĚõľsubscriptsuperscript‚ĄíūĚĎĖūĚĎ£cal Lrm effrm Parton=C0(alpha)cal Lv+sumi=1inftygamma-iCi(alpha)cal L(i)v . where we have used only 1/ő≥1ūĚõĺ1/gamma to indicate power corrections and non-local terms with 1/i‚Äčn‚čÖD‚čÖ1ūĚĎĖūĚĎõūĚź∑1/incdot D have been omitted. For computing light-cone PDFs, P=M‚Äčő≥‚ÄčvūĚĎÉūĚĎÄūĚõĺūĚĎ£P=Mgamma v shows up when a particular hadron state of mass MūĚĎÄM and velocity vūĚĎ£v is involved. While Wilson coefficient C2subscriptūĚź∂2C2 helps to eliminate all the logarithmic dependences in PūĚĎÉP, PūĚĎÉP-dependence is completely removed through the infinite power corrections, and is finally replaced by the UV and rapidity renormalization scales őľūĚúámu and ő∂ūĚúĀzeta in light-cone matrix elements which can be defined through perturbative schemes. It shall be warned that the effective parton theory is only effective when all parton momenta are at perturbative scales, x‚ÄčP‚ČęőõQCDmuch-greater-thanūĚĎ•ūĚĎÉsubscriptőõQCDxPggLambdarm QCD. For example, the above expansion breaks down at small x‚ąľőõQCD/Psimilar-toūĚĎ•subscriptőõQCDūĚĎÉxsimLambdarm QCD/P.

Light-cone PDFs such as f‚Äč(x)ūĚĎďūĚĎ•f(x) are singular functions of parton momentum fraction xūĚĎ•x. They vanish at x=1ūĚĎ•1x=1 but their first and high-order derivatives are not continuous. Moreover, at x=0ūĚĎ•0x=0 PDFs are divergent. In LaMET expansion for PDFs, momentum distribution f‚Äč(y,Pz)ūĚĎďūĚĎ¶superscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßtildef(y,Pz) is analytic in whole range of yūĚĎ¶y at finite PzsuperscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßPz including y=1ūĚĎ¶1y=1. This is because with backward moving particles in a finite momentum hadron, a quark or gluon can carry more momentum than the total PzsuperscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßPz. Since the correlation function in coordinate space zūĚĎßz has finite support (decays exponentially at large zūĚĎßz) due to confinement, the momentum distribution is analytical at both y=0ūĚĎ¶0y=0 or y=1ūĚĎ¶1y=1. At finite PzsuperscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßPz, LaMET expansion for f‚Äč(x)ūĚĎďūĚĎ•f(x) breaks down near x=0ūĚĎ•0x=0 and 111, and therefore cannot give any useful information about PDF singularities. On the other hand, in the limit of PzsuperscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßPz going to infinity, C2subscriptūĚź∂2C2 increasingly approaches őī‚Äč(x/y‚ąí1)ūĚõŅūĚĎ•ūĚĎ¶1delta(x/y-1), and the momentum distribution f‚Äč(y,Pz)ūĚĎďūĚĎ¶superscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßtildef(y,Pz) approaches PDF f‚Äč(x,őľ)ūĚĎďūĚĎ•ūĚúáf(x,mu) with a proper scheme conversion. The correlation length őĽ=z‚ÄčPzūĚúÜūĚĎßsuperscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßlambda=zPz diverges, therefore f‚Äč(y,‚ąě)ūĚĎďūĚĎ¶tildef(y,infty) becomes singular at y=x=0ūĚĎ¶ūĚĎ•0y=x=0. Moreover, there is no more backward moving quarks and gluons in the hadron in this limit, and f‚Äč(1,‚ąě)ūĚĎď1tildef(1,infty) no longer has any non-vanishing support. But the derivatives of f‚Äč(y,‚ąě)ūĚĎďūĚĎ¶tildef(y,infty) at y=1ūĚĎ¶1y=1 need not vanish. Besides the singularities at x=¬Ī1ūĚĎ•plus-or-minus1x=pm 1, generalized parton distributions (GPDs) have singularities at x=¬ĪőĺūĚĎ•plus-or-minusūĚúČx=pmxi ( őĺūĚúČxi is a skewness variable) where one of the parton has zero momentum fractioncitebib.bib33, bib.bib34. GPDs are continuous there but derivatives are not. One can generate these singularities through the same mechanism discussed above. In a recent papercitebib.bib35, it has been suggested that due to the renormalon effect, a linear power correction in factorization of quasi GPDs maybe generated from convoluting the coefficient functions and light-cone GPDs because of the derivative discontinuity of the latter at x=¬ĪőĺūĚĎ•plus-or-minusūĚúČx=pmxi. If, on the other hand, the light-cone GPDs are factorized in terms of the quasi GPDs, the coefficient functions will be convoluted with smooth quasi-GPDs, and nothing special seems to happen at x=¬ĪőĺūĚĎ•plus-or-minusūĚúČx=pmxi. It remains to see if a special linear power correction indeed disappears at this point.
content_sections.V Conclusion: Weinberg‚Äôs pioneering work on EFTs has had a great impact in modern theoretical physics. It changed fundamentally the way we think about quantum field theories. LaMET is an example of EFTs for calculating non-perturbative parton physics systematically through lattice QCD simulationscitebib.bib36, which was thought impossible for a long while.

sorted_papers.title:
Moments of proton GPDs from the OPE of nonlocal quark bilinears up to
  NNLO
sorted_papers.abstract:
For the first time, we present a lattice QCD determination of Mellin moments
of unpolarized generalized parton distributions (GPDs) of the proton from an
analysis of the quasi-GPD matrix elements within the short-distance
factorization framework. We perform our calculation on an $N_f$=2+1+1 twisted
mass fermions ensemble with a clover improvement at lattice spacing $a=0.093$
fm and a pion mass of $m_\pi=260$ MeV. Focusing on the zero-skewness case, the
iso-vector and iso-scalar quasi-GPDs are calculated from the $\gamma_0$
definition, as well as a recently proposed Lorentz-invariant definition. We
utilize data on both symmetric and asymmetric kinematic frames, which allows us
to obtain the Mellin moments for several values of the momentum transfer, $-t$,
in the range 0.17 to $2.77~\rm{GeV}^2$. We use the ratio scheme for GPDs, i.e.
renormalization group invariant ratios with leading-twist factorization formula
and perturbatively calculated matching coefficients up to the
next-next-to-leading order (NNLO) to extract Mellin moments of GPDs, which are
consistent with renormalization-group improved results. We compare our
determination from quasi-GPDs with the results extracted using standard
calculations of Mellin moments of local operators, specifically those related
to the electromagnetic and gravitational form factors. We estimated the moments
of GPDs up to the fifth ones for the first time. By extrapolating the Mellin
moments to $-t=0$, we obtained the quark charges, momentum fraction, as well as
the angular momentum contributions to the proton spin. The impact parameter
space interpretation of the GPD moments is discussed, which provides insights
into the spatial distribution of unpolarized quarks and their correlations in
the transverse plane of an unpolarized or transversely polarized proton.

qualitative: LaMET provides a framework to extract generalized parton distributions (GPDs) from lattice QCD by studying quasi-observables, such as quasi-GPDs, at large hadron momentum.

The short-distance factorization framework in LaMET allows the extraction of physical GPDs from quasi-GPD matrix elements through perturbative matching to the operator product expansion (OPE) of nonlocal quark bilinears.
The use of both symmetric and asymmetric kinematic frames enables the determination of Mellin moments of GPDs at multiple momentum transfers, improving the reliability of the extraction.

The ratio scheme for GPDs‚ÄĒdefined as renormalization group invariant ratios with leading-twist factorization and perturbatively calculated matching coefficients‚ÄĒensures consistency with renormalization-group improved results.
The Lorentz-invariant definition of quasi-GPDs offers an alternative to the standard ő≥‚āÄ definition, improving the theoretical control and reducing systematic uncertainties.
Extrapolation of Mellin moments to zero momentum transfer (-t = 0) yields physical observables such as quark charges, momentum fractions, and angular momentum contributions to the proton spin.
The impact parameter space interpretation of GPD moments provides insight into the transverse spatial distribution of unpolarized quarks and their correlations in the proton's transverse plane.
quantitative: Lattice spacing: a = 0.093 fm
Pion mass: m_ŌÄ = 260 MeV
Number of dynamical flavors: N_f = 2+1+1
Twisted mass fermions with clover improvement used for the ensemble
Momentum transfer range: -t ‚ąą [0.17, 2.77] GeV¬≤
Mellin moments extracted up to the fifth order (N = 5)
Matching coefficients calculated up to next-next-to-leading order (NNLO)
Extrapolated moments at -t = 0 yield: quark charges, momentum fraction, and angular momentum contributions to proton spin

Shohini Bhattacharya, Krzysztof Cichy, Martha Constantinou, Xiang Gao,
  Andreas Metz, Joshua Miller, Swagato Mukherjee, Peter Petreczky, Fernanda
