content_sections.I Introduction: The introduction of generalized parton distributions (GPDs)citebib.bib1, bib.bib2, bib.bib3 has opened up a new perspective into the three-dimensional imaging of the nucleon. These pioneering quantities reveal information on hadron structure far beyond the traditional one-dimensional parton distribution functions (PDFs), typically investigated in deep-inelastic scattering (DIS) experiments, and the transverse structure encoded in the various form factors. In particular, GPDs provide a more comprehensive and nuanced view of the internal structure of the nucleon, offering insights into the spatial distributions of quarks and gluonscitebib.bib4, bib.bib5, bib.bib6, bib.bib7. What is more, the moments of GPDs are related to the matrix elements of the energy-momentum tensor (EMT), from which we can gain valuable insights into the distribution of the hadron‚Äôs internal energy, momentum, and pressurecitebib.bib8, bib.bib9, bib.bib10, as well as the coupling of hadrons to gravity. Extracting the moments at zero momentum transfer allows the momentum fraction, spin, and angular momentum carried by the quarks and gluons inside the hadron to be determined. These information have the potential to improve our understanding of fundamental physics and could lead to significant advancements in various fields within nuclear and particle physics. There have been efforts to parameterize the GPDs and fit them from global experimentscitebib.bib8, bib.bib11, bib.bib12, bib.bib13, bib.bib14, bib.bib15, bib.bib16, bib.bib17, bib.bib18, bib.bib19, bib.bib20, but this field is still in its infancy, as it remains a challenge to extract the xūĚĎ•x-dependence of GPDs from processes such as deeply-virtual Compton scattering (DVCS)citebib.bib21, bib.bib22, bib.bib23, bib.bib24. Other exclusive reactions, which could in principle alleviate this problem, include double DVCScitebib.bib25, bib.bib26 and further processes where two particles (with one or both of them being photons) are detected in addition to the final-state nucleoncitebib.bib27, bib.bib28, bib.bib29, bib.bib30, bib.bib31. However, those are typically very difficult to measure. Given the challenges in extracting GPDs from experiments, it is highly desirable to have lattice QCD results that provide complementary knowledge and potential guidance to experiments. In this work, we focus on the GPDs with unpolarized quarks, HūĚźĽH and EūĚźłE, which are obtained from the Fourier transform of light-cone correlators Főľ‚Äč(z,P,őĒ)superscriptūĚźĻūĚúáūĚĎßūĚĎÉőĒdisplaystyle Fmu(z,P,Delta) =‚ü®pf|q¬Į‚Äč(‚ąíz2)‚Äčő≥őľ‚ÄčūĚí≤‚Äč(‚ąíz2,z2)‚Äčq‚Äč(z2)|pi‚ü©,absentquantum-operator-productsubscriptūĚĎĚūĚĎď¬ĮūĚĎěūĚĎß2superscriptūĚõĺūĚúáūĚí≤ūĚĎß2ūĚĎß2ūĚĎěūĚĎß2subscriptūĚĎĚūĚĎĖdisplaystyle= pf|barq(-tfracz2)gammamu,cal W(-tfracz2,tfracz2)q(tfracz2)|pi,, with ő≥őľ=ő≥+superscriptūĚõĺūĚúásuperscriptūĚõĺgammamu=gamma+. The quark and anti-quark are separated along the light-cone direction z=l‚Äčn‚ąíūĚĎßūĚĎôsubscriptūĚĎõz=ln- and are connected by the Wilson-line ūĚí≤‚Äč(‚ąíz2,z2)=ūĚíę‚Äčexp‚Ā°(i‚Äč‚ąę‚ąíl‚Äčn‚ąí/2l‚Äčn‚ąí/2ūĚĎĎl‚Ä≤‚ÄčA+)ūĚí≤ūĚĎß2ūĚĎß2ūĚíęūĚĎĖsuperscriptsubscriptūĚĎôsubscriptūĚĎõ2ūĚĎôsubscriptūĚĎõ2differential-dsuperscriptūĚĎô‚Ä≤superscriptūĚźīW(-fracz2,fracz2)=Pexp(iint-ln-/2ln-/2dlprimeA+). Because of Lorentz invariance, the GPDs are functions of xūĚĎ•x and two Lorentz-invariant products of the vectors pf,pisubscriptūĚĎĚūĚĎďsubscriptūĚĎĚūĚĎĖpf,,pi1Alternatively, P=(pf+pi)/2ūĚĎÉsubscriptūĚĎĚūĚĎďsubscriptūĚĎĚūĚĎĖ2P=(pf+pi)/2, őĒ=pf‚ąípiőĒsubscriptūĚĎĚūĚĎďsubscriptūĚĎĚūĚĎĖDelta=pf-pi. and n‚ąísubscriptūĚĎõn-, which is conventionally chosen as őĺ=‚ąí(őĒ‚Äčn‚ąí)/(2‚ÄčP‚Äčn‚ąí)ūĚúČőĒsubscriptūĚĎõ2ūĚĎÉsubscriptūĚĎõxi=-(Delta n-)/(2Pn-) and t=őĒ2ūĚĎ°superscriptőĒ2t=Delta2. Though lattice QCD can compute non-perturbative matrix elements, these are time-dependent quantities and cannot be computed for a lattice defined in Euclidean space. It is instead the first few Mellin moments of GPDs that are traditionally extracted from the latticecitebib.bib32, bib.bib33, bib.bib34, bib.bib35, bib.bib36, bib.bib37, bib.bib38, bib.bib39, bib.bib40, bib.bib41, bib.bib42, bib.bib43, bib.bib44, bib.bib45, bib.bib46, bib.bib47, bib.bib48, bib.bib49, bib.bib50, bib.bib51, as matrix elements of local operators. However, due to signal decay and power-divergent mixing under renormalization, there are no moments beyond the third that exist. Breakthrough was made about a decade ago when the quasi-PDF method was proposedcitebib.bib52, bib.bib53 that utilizes operators holding the same form as Eq. but with equal-time quark fields that are separated along a spatial direction. Without loss of generality, the direction is chosen to be z=l‚Äčn3ūĚĎßūĚĎôsubscriptūĚĎõ3z=ln3. This approach paves a way to relate the Euclidean matrix elements to the light-cone PDFs through factorization by an expansion in powers of 1/P31subscriptūĚĎÉ31/P3, with P3subscriptūĚĎÉ3P3 the hadron momentum, and makes it possible to compute xūĚĎ•x-dependent PDFs from lattice QCD. The quasi-PDF approach, often referred to as large momentum effective theory (LaMET)citebib.bib54, was then extended to the GPDs and other light-cone quantities. Starting from the same quasi-PDF operator, the so-called Ioffe-time pseudo-distributions or pseudo-PDFs (pPDFs)citebib.bib55, bib.bib56 were proposed to extract either the Mellin moments or xūĚĎ•x-dependent PDFs by expanding in z2superscriptūĚĎß2z2. Several other approachescitebib.bib57, bib.bib58, bib.bib59, bib.bib60, bib.bib61, bib.bib62, bib.bib63 also became available in the past few years. Soon after the theoretical breakthrough, significant progress has been made for the calculation of PDFscitebib.bib64, bib.bib65, bib.bib66, bib.bib67, bib.bib68, bib.bib69, bib.bib56, bib.bib70, bib.bib71, bib.bib72, bib.bib73, bib.bib74, bib.bib75, bib.bib76, bib.bib77, bib.bib78, bib.bib79, bib.bib80, bib.bib81, bib.bib82, bib.bib83, bib.bib84, bib.bib85, bib.bib86, bib.bib87, bib.bib88, bib.bib89, bib.bib90, bib.bib91, bib.bib92, bib.bib93, bib.bib94, bib.bib95, bib.bib96, bib.bib97, including higher-twist distributionscitebib.bib98, bib.bib99, bib.bib100, bib.bib101, parton distribution amplitudescitebib.bib102, bib.bib103, bib.bib104, bib.bib105, bib.bib106, bib.bib107, bib.bib108, bib.bib109, GPDscitebib.bib110, bib.bib111, bib.bib112, bib.bib113, bib.bib114, bib.bib115, bib.bib116, bib.bib117, bib.bib118, bib.bib119 as well as transverse-momentum dependent parton distributions citebib.bib120, bib.bib121, bib.bib122, bib.bib123, bib.bib124, bib.bib125. More information can be found in several recent reviewscitebib.bib126, bib.bib54, bib.bib127, bib.bib128, bib.bib129. For GPDs in particular, the Dirac structure ő≥őľ=ő≥0superscriptūĚõĺūĚúásuperscriptūĚõĺ0gammamu=gamma0 was usually taken for the quasi-GPD matrix elements as proposed for quasi-PDFs and inspired by the ő≥+superscriptūĚõĺgamma+ definition in the light cone. This definition is, however, frame-dependent for GPDs, requiring that calculations are defined in the symmetric kinematic frame: the momentum transfer is symmetrically distributed as p‚Üíf=P‚Üí+őĒ‚Üí/2subscript‚ÜíūĚĎĚūĚĎď‚ÜíūĚĎÉ‚ÜíőĒ2vecpf=vecP+vecDelta/2 and p‚Üíi=P‚Üí‚ąíőĒ‚Üí/2subscript‚ÜíūĚĎĚūĚĎĖ‚ÜíūĚĎÉ‚ÜíőĒ2vecpi=vecP-vecDelta/2. Encouraging results were reported, with, however, a heavy computational cost for every value of the momentum transfercitebib.bib110, bib.bib111, bib.bib112, bib.bib113, bib.bib114, bib.bib115, bib.bib116. It was recently proposed and numerically proven that one can equivalently relate the matrix elements in any kinematic frame (e. g. , p‚Üíf=P‚Üísubscript‚ÜíūĚĎĚūĚĎď‚ÜíūĚĎÉvecpf=vecP and p‚Üíi=P‚Üí‚ąíőĒ‚Üísubscript‚ÜíūĚĎĚūĚĎĖ‚ÜíūĚĎÉ‚ÜíőĒvecpi=vecP-vecDelta) to the symmetric one, through Lorentz-invariant amplitudes based on the Lorentz-covariant parameterization of the matrix elementscitebib.bib117, bib.bib118, bib.bib119, bib.bib130. This finding established a basis for faster and more efficient computation of GPDs using lattice QCD in asymmetric frames, allowing for flexibility in the distribution of transferred momentum between the initial and final states. Additionally, a novel Lorentz-invariant definition of quasi-GPDs matrix elements was proposed in the same work, which may lead to smaller power corrections in matching to the light-cone GPDs. Based on that, exploratory results using xūĚĎ•x-space matching and RI-MOM renormalization were established. In this work, we instead will apply the leading-twist short-distance factorization in coordinate space with a ratio-scheme renormalization to extract the first moments of GPDs at a broad range of values for the momentum transfer. By comparing our results with traditional moment calculations, we will be able to assess the efficacy of different definitions for quasi-GPDs, as well as access higher-order moments that are difficult or even impossible to calculate through traditional methods. The plan of the paper is as follows. In Sec. , we review the definition of quasi HūĚźĽH and EūĚźłE GPDs. In Sec. , we present our bare quasi-GPD matrix elements and the ratio scheme renormalization. In Sec. , we discuss the determination of Mellin moments of the proton GPDs using short-distance factorization with perturbative matching. In Sec. , we discuss the tūĚĎ°t-dependence of GPD moments to determine the quark charges, momentum fraction, and total spin contribution to the proton. Additionally, we provide an interpretation of the results in the impact parameter space. Finally, Sec. contains our conclusions. Some supplementary material is presented in the Appendix.
content_sections.II Quasi HūĚźĽH and EūĚźłE GPD matrix el: As discussed in Ref. citebib.bib117, the quasi-GPD matrix elements can be parametrized in terms of Lorentz-invariant amplitudes ūĚíúisubscriptūĚíúūĚĎĖcal Ai, with certain kinematic factors. Therefore, quasi-GPD matrix elements in different frames can be related to each other, which largely reduces the computational cost for the study of GPDs at various values for the momentum transfer. This can be achieved via a calculation in a convenient asymmetric frame, in which the initial or final states do not carry any momentum transfer. Here, we choose p‚Üíf=P‚Üísubscript‚ÜíūĚĎĚūĚĎď‚ÜíūĚĎÉvecpf=vecP and p‚Üíi=P‚Üí‚ąíőĒ‚Üísubscript‚ÜíūĚĎĚūĚĎĖ‚ÜíūĚĎÉ‚ÜíőĒvecpi=vecP-vecDelta. In this section, we will review some of the key aspects of Ref. citebib.bib117, on which this work relies. It is evident that when the hadron momentum P‚Üí=(0,0,P3)‚ÜíūĚĎÉ00subscriptūĚĎÉ3vecP=(0,0,P3) is finite, the definition of ő≥0superscriptūĚõĺ0gamma0 varies across different frames, though such discrepancies vanish in the infinite momentum limit. What follows is the question of which definition could be an appropriate choice that can provide good convergence to the light-cone quantities, and can be described by the perturbative matching. To explore this possibility, inspired by Eq. and Eq. , it is natural to define the quasi-GPD matrix elements also in a Lorentz-invariant form as ‚ĄčLIs/a‚Äč(z‚čÖP,z‚čÖőĒ,(őĒ)2,z2)subscriptsuperscript‚ĄčūĚĎ†ūĚĎéLI‚čÖūĚĎßūĚĎÉ‚čÖūĚĎßőĒsuperscriptőĒ2superscriptūĚĎß2displaystylecal Hs/arm LI(zcdot P,zcdotDelta,(Delta)2,z2) =ūĚíú1+őĒ‚čÖzP‚čÖz‚ÄčūĚíú3,absentsubscriptūĚíú1‚čÖőĒūĚĎß‚čÖūĚĎÉūĚĎßsubscriptūĚíú3displaystyle=cal A1+dfracDeltacdot zPcdot zcal A3,, ‚ĄįLIs/a‚Äč(z‚čÖP,z‚čÖőĒ,(őĒ)2,z2)subscriptsuperscript‚ĄįūĚĎ†ūĚĎéLI‚čÖūĚĎßūĚĎÉ‚čÖūĚĎßőĒsuperscriptőĒ2superscriptūĚĎß2displaystylecal Es/arm LI(zcdot P,zcdotDelta,(Delta)2,z2) =‚ąíūĚíú1‚ąíőĒ‚čÖzP‚čÖz‚ÄčūĚíú3+2‚ÄčūĚíú5+2‚ÄčP‚čÖz‚ÄčūĚíú6+2‚ÄčőĒ‚čÖz‚ÄčūĚíú8,absentsubscriptūĚíú1‚čÖőĒūĚĎß‚čÖūĚĎÉūĚĎßsubscriptūĚíú32subscriptūĚíú5‚čÖ2ūĚĎÉūĚĎßsubscriptūĚíú6‚čÖ2őĒūĚĎßsubscriptūĚíú8displaystyle=-cal A1-dfracDeltacdot zPcdot zcal A3+2cal A5+2Pcdot zcal A6+2Deltacdot zcal A8,, with the only difference with their light-cone counterpart HūĚźĽH and EūĚźłE being z2‚Č†0superscriptūĚĎß20z2neq 0 originated from the spatial separation such as z=(0,0,0,z3)ūĚĎß000subscriptūĚĎß3z=(0,0,0,z3). All the amplitudes ūĚíúis/asubscriptsuperscriptūĚíúūĚĎ†ūĚĎéūĚĎĖcal As/ai can be extracted from either the symmetric or an asymmetric frame through linear combinations of the various FőľsuperscriptūĚźĻūĚúáFmu (őľ=0,1,2,3ūĚúá0123mu=0,1,2,3), and any choice can be used in Eqs. - as long as a Lorentz transformation is applied to the kinematic factors to match the values of tūĚĎ°t. Consequentially, we can construct the quasi-GPDs in any frame, including the Lorentz-invariant ones, as has been proven in Ref. citebib.bib117. In addition, with a reduced number of additional terms, the proposed Lorentz-invariant definition could potentially converge to the light-cone GPDs faster than the ő≥0superscriptūĚõĺ0gamma0 definition. To explore this possibility, we will analyze results from different definitions and compare them with the traditional Mellin-moments calculations in this work.

The bare matrix elements contain UV divergences related to the operator and the Wilson line. Thus, we have to renormalize the matrix elements prior to extracting physical quantities. It is known that the UV divergence of the quark bilinear operator is multiplicative and independent of the Dirac structure őďőľsubscriptőďūĚúáGammamu matrix as well as the hadron statecitebib.bib134, bib.bib135, bib.bib136. One can therefore remove the UV divergence by constructing the renormalization group (RG) invariant ratios using the bare matrix elements with different hadron states but the same operatorscitebib.bib87. In this work, we construct the ratios, ‚Ą≥‚Äč(z,P,őĒ)=‚ĄĪ‚Äč(z‚Üí,P‚Üí,őĒ‚Üí)‚ĄĪ(z‚Üí,P‚Üí=0,őĒ‚Üí=0)=‚ĄĪR‚Äč(z‚Üí,P‚Üí,őĒ‚Üí)‚ĄĪR(z‚Üí,P‚Üí=0,őĒ‚Üí=0),displaystyleM(z,P,Delta)=fracF(vecz,vecP,vecDelta)F(vecz,vecP=0,vecDelta=0)=fracFR(vecz,vecP,vecDelta)FR(vecz,vecP=0,vecDelta=0),, where the ratio of bare matrix elements ‚ĄĪ‚ĄĪF is RG invariant, so that is equivalent to the ratio of renormalized matrix elements ‚ĄĪRsuperscript‚ĄĪūĚĎÖFR. ‚ĄĪ‚Äč(z‚Üí,P‚Üí,őĒ‚Üí)‚ĄĪ‚ÜíūĚĎß‚ÜíūĚĎÉ‚ÜíőĒF(vecz,vecP,vecDelta) in the numerator can be either ‚Ąč‚ĄčH or ‚Ąį‚ĄįE quasi-GPDs for the iso-vector (u‚ąídūĚĎĘūĚĎĎu-d) and iso-scalar (u+dūĚĎĘūĚĎĎu+d) cases. For the denominator, we chose to always take the iso-vector P‚Üí=0‚ÜíūĚĎÉ0vecP=0 quasi-PDF matrix elements as shown in Fig. , since the renormalization factors are identical. A discussion of iso-scalar P‚Üí=0‚ÜíūĚĎÉ0vecP=0 quasi-PDF matrix elements can be found in App. . At z=0ūĚĎß0z=0, the iso-vector matrix element ‚ĄĪ‚Äč(z‚Üí=0,P‚Üí=0,őĒ‚Üí=0)‚ĄĪformulae-sequence‚ÜíūĚĎß0formulae-sequence‚ÜíūĚĎÉ0‚ÜíőĒ0F(vecz=0,vecP=0,vecDelta=0) gives the renormalization constant ZVsubscriptūĚĎćūĚĎČZV. The RG invariant ratios, ‚Ą≥‚Äč(z,P,őĒ)‚Ą≥ūĚĎßūĚĎÉőĒM(z,P,Delta), can also be written as a function of Lorentz-invariant variables, that is, ‚Ą≥‚Äč(z2,z‚ÄčP,őĒ2)‚Ą≥superscriptūĚĎß2ūĚĎßūĚĎÉsuperscriptőĒ2M(z2,zP,Delta2), with the z‚ÄčP=z3‚ÄčP3ūĚĎßūĚĎÉsubscriptūĚĎß3subscriptūĚĎÉ3zP=z3P3 being the so-called Ioffe time. We will use notation z‚ÄčPūĚĎßūĚĎÉzP instead of z3‚ÄčP3subscriptūĚĎß3subscriptūĚĎÉ3z3P3 in what follows. If the momentum transfer őĒőĒDelta equals zero in ‚ĄĪ‚ĄĪcal F, the ‚Ą≥‚Äč(z,P,őĒ=0)‚Ą≥ūĚĎßūĚĎÉőĒ0M(z,P,Delta=0) will reduce to the standard Ioffe-time pseudo-distributioncitebib.bib55, bib.bib56. In Fig. , we show the ratio scheme renormalized matrix elements for the iso-vector (upper panels) and iso-scalar (lower panels) quasi-GPDs. The matrix elements shown are from the Lorentz-invariant definition of quasi-GPDs (bare matrix elements presented in Fig. , with the filled squared symbols for the real part and the circled open symbols for the imaginary part. It can be seen that the renormalized matrix elements have a good signal and show a clear dependence on the momentum transfer ‚ąítūĚĎ°-t. The iso-scalar ‚Ąį‚Ąįcal E quasi-GPDs are still mostly consistent with zero after renormalization, as also observed in the bare case.
content_sections.IV Short distance factorization and Mell: The renormalized matrix elements can be expanded in z2superscriptūĚĎß2z2, namely the short distance factorization (SDF). In the case of zero skewness, őĺ=0ūĚúČ0xi=0, the leading-twist SDF for the quasi-GPDs is the same as in the quasi-PDF casecitebib.bib137, which in the MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinermMS scheme readscitebib.bib55, bib.bib56, bib.bib138, ‚ĄĪMS¬Į‚Äč(z,P,őĒ)=‚ąĎn=0(‚ąíi‚Äčz‚ÄčP)nn!‚ÄčCnMS¬Į‚Äč(őľ2‚Äčz2)‚Äč‚ü®xn‚ü©+ūĚí™‚Äč(őõQCD2‚Äčz2),superscript‚ĄĪ¬ĮMSūĚĎßūĚĎÉőĒsubscriptūĚĎõ0superscriptūĚĎĖūĚĎßūĚĎÉūĚĎõūĚĎõsubscriptsuperscriptūĚź∂¬ĮMSūĚĎõsuperscriptūĚúá2superscriptūĚĎß2delimited-‚ü®‚ü©superscriptūĚĎ•ūĚĎõūĚí™superscriptsubscriptőõQCD2superscriptūĚĎß2displaystylebeginsplitFoverlinermMS(z,P,Delta)=sumn=0frac(-izP)nn!CoverlinermMSn(mu2z2) xn+cal O(Lambdarm QCD2z2),endsplit where CnMS¬Į‚Äč(őľ2‚Äčz2)subscriptsuperscriptūĚź∂¬ĮMSūĚĎõsuperscriptūĚúá2superscriptūĚĎß2CoverlinermMSn(mu2z2) are Wilson coefficients calculated from perturbation theory, which are available up to NNLOcitebib.bib139, bib.bib140 for the iso-vector (u‚ąídūĚĎĘūĚĎĎu-d) case, but only up to NLO for the iso-scalar (u+dūĚĎĘūĚĎĎu+d) one with complete calculation including the quark-gluon mixingcitebib.bib141. The ‚ü®xn‚ü©delimited-‚ü®‚ü©superscriptūĚĎ•ūĚĎõ xn are the Mellin moments of GPDs, defined as ‚ąę‚ąí11dxxnHq(x,őĺ=0,t)=‚ąĎeveni=0nAn+1,iq‚Äč(t),‚ąę‚ąí11dxxnEq(x,őĺ=0,t)=‚ąĎeveni=0nBn+1,iq‚Äč(t),displaystylebeginsplitint-11dxxnHq(x,xi=0,t)=sumnstackrelscriptstyle i=0rmevenAqn+1,i(t), int-11dxxnEq(x,xi=0,t)=sumnstackrelscriptstyle i=0rmevenBqn+1,i(t), endsplit in which the second moments ‚ü®x‚ü©delimited-‚ü®‚ü©ūĚĎ• x, namely A20subscriptūĚźī20A20 and B20subscriptūĚźĶ20B20, are of particular interest due to their connection to the QCD energy-momentum tensor as gravitational form factors and provide access to the angular momentum sum rule. The ‚ü®x0‚ü©delimited-‚ü®‚ü©superscriptūĚĎ•0 x0 are essentially the Dirac (for HūĚźĽH) and Pauli (for EūĚźłE) form factors. This SDF formula suffers from higher-twist contamination ūĚí™‚Äč(őõQCD2‚Äčz2)ūĚí™superscriptsubscriptőõQCD2superscriptūĚĎß2cal O(Lambdarm QCD2z2) growing as a function of z2superscriptūĚĎß2z2. Thus, it is expected to be valid for small spatial separations z2superscriptūĚĎß2z2. As a consequence, the matrix elements will only be sensitive to the lower moments, as the higher ones are factorially suppressed by (‚ąíi‚Äčz‚ÄčP)n/n!superscriptūĚĎĖūĚĎßūĚĎÉūĚĎõūĚĎõ(-izP)n/n! for a finite hadron momentum P3subscriptūĚĎÉ3P3. Despite the abovementioned challenges, it is desirable to pursue the direction of extracting Mellin moments from an SDF, because traditional calculations of higher-order moments through local operators suffer from notorious mixing between higher-dimensional and lower-dimensional operatorscitebib.bib32, bib.bib33, bib.bib34, bib.bib35, bib.bib36, bib.bib37, bib.bib38, bib.bib39, bib.bib40, bib.bib41, bib.bib42, bib.bib43, bib.bib44, bib.bib45, bib.bib46, bib.bib47, bib.bib48, bib.bib49, bib.bib50. In contrast, quasi-GPDs involve only a non-local operator of dimension three, and therefore, the higher moments can be systematically accessed by increasing the hadron momentum. Inserting the SDF formula into the ratio scheme renormalized matrix elements in Sec. , one obtainscitebib.bib87, bib.bib88 ‚Ą≥‚Äč(z,P,őĒ)=‚ąĎn=0(‚ąíi‚Äčz‚ÄčP)nn!‚ÄčCnMS¬Į‚Äč(őľ2‚Äčz2)C0MS¬Į‚Äč(őľ2‚Äčz2)‚Äč‚ü®xn‚ü©+ūĚí™‚Äč(őõQCD2‚Äčz2). ‚Ą≥ūĚĎßūĚĎÉőĒsubscriptūĚĎõ0superscriptūĚĎĖūĚĎßūĚĎÉūĚĎõūĚĎõsubscriptsuperscriptūĚź∂¬ĮMSūĚĎõsuperscriptūĚúá2superscriptūĚĎß2subscriptsuperscriptūĚź∂¬ĮMS0superscriptūĚúá2superscriptūĚĎß2delimited-‚ü®‚ü©superscriptūĚĎ•ūĚĎõūĚí™superscriptsubscriptőõQCD2superscriptūĚĎß2displaystylebeginsplitM(z,P,Delta)=sumn=0frac(-izP)nn!fracCoverlinermMSn(mu2z2)CoverlinermMS0(mu2z2) xn+cal O(Lambdarm QCD2z2),. endsplit The ratio scheme renormalization has the advantage of potentially reducing the higher-twist contamination, because of the latter‚Äôs cancellation between the numerator and denominator. Nevertheless, it is still important to keep the value of zūĚĎßz moderate so that the SDF does not break down. For practical reasons, one needs to truncate Eq. up to nmaxsubscriptūĚĎõmaxnrm max when performing the fit, and then minimizing the Ōá2superscriptūĚúí2chi2 defined as, Ōá2=‚ąĎP3‚ąĎz=zminzmax((Re‚Äč[‚Ą≥SDF‚Äč(z,P,őĒ)]‚ąíRe‚Äč[‚Ą≥data‚Äč(z,P,őĒ)])2ŌÉRe2+(Im‚Äč[‚Ą≥SDF‚Äč(z,P,őĒ)]‚ąíIm‚Äč[‚Ą≥data‚Äč(z,P,őĒ)])2ŌÉIm2),superscriptūĚúí2subscriptsubscriptūĚĎÉ3superscriptsubscriptūĚĎßsubscriptūĚĎßminsubscriptūĚĎßmaxsuperscriptRedelimited-superscript‚Ą≥SDFūĚĎßūĚĎÉőĒRedelimited-superscript‚Ą≥dataūĚĎßūĚĎÉőĒ2superscriptsubscriptūĚúéRe2superscriptImdelimited-superscript‚Ą≥SDFzPőĒImdelimited-superscript‚Ą≥datazPőĒ2superscriptsubscriptūĚúéIm2displaystylechi2=sumP3sumz=zrm minzrm maxleft(frac(rm Re[Mrm SDF(z,P,Delta)]-rm Re[Mrm data(z,P,Delta)])2sigmarm Re2+frac(rmIm[Mrm SDF(z,P,Delta)]-rm Im[Mdata(z,P,Delta)])2sigmarm Im2right),, from which we can determine the moments up to ‚ü®xnmax‚ü©delimited-‚ü®‚ü©superscriptūĚĎ•subscriptūĚĎõmax xnrm max. It is worth mentioning that the real and imaginary parts of ‚Ą≥‚Äč(z,P,őĒ)‚Ą≥ūĚĎßūĚĎÉőĒM(z,P,Delta) will provide even and odd moments, respectively.

At leading-order approximation, that is, for ūĚí™‚Äč(őĪs0)ūĚí™superscriptsubscriptūĚõľūĚĎ†0O(alphas0) and with Cn‚Äč(őľ2‚Äčz2)=1subscriptūĚź∂ūĚĎõsuperscriptūĚúá2superscriptūĚĎß21Cn(mu2z2)=1, the factorization formula in Eq. reduces to a polynomial function of z‚ÄčPūĚĎßūĚĎÉzP with coefficients ‚ü®xn‚ü©delimited-‚ü®‚ü©superscriptūĚĎ•ūĚĎõ xn for a fixed momentum transfer ‚ąítūĚĎ°-t. Beyond the leading approximation, the renormalized matrix element or the moments non-trivially depend on the physical scale z2superscriptūĚĎß2z2. At short distance, the Wilson coefficients Cn‚Äč(őľ2‚Äčz2)/C0‚Äč(őľ2‚Äčz2)subscriptūĚź∂ūĚĎõsuperscriptūĚúá2superscriptūĚĎß2subscriptūĚź∂0superscriptūĚúá2superscriptūĚĎß2Cn(mu2z2)/C0(mu2z2) are expected to compensate for this scale dependence and evolve the moments to the factorization scale őľūĚúámu. At the momentum transfer of ‚ąít=0.69‚ÄčGeV2ūĚĎ°0.69superscriptGeV2-t=0.69rmGeV2, we have three different momenta: P3=0.83,1.25subscriptūĚĎÉ30.831.25P3=0.83,1.25 and 1.67 GeV, which can be used to assess the validity of the leading twist factorization. Since the matrix elements of a local current should have no boost or frame dependence at a given ‚ąítūĚĎ°-t, we averaged the z3=0subscriptūĚĎß30z3=0 matrix elements ‚Ą≥‚Ąč¬Į‚Äč(0,P,őĒ)¬Įsubscript‚Ą≥‚Ąč0ūĚĎÉőĒoverlineMH(0,P,Delta) of the three P3subscriptūĚĎÉ3P3 for each bootstrap sample, and normalized the ‚Ą≥‚Ąčsubscript‚Ą≥‚ĄčMH by ‚Ą≥‚Ąč‚Äč(0,P,őĒ)/‚Ą≥‚Ąč¬Į‚Äč(0,P,őĒ)subscript‚Ą≥‚Ąč0ūĚĎÉőĒ¬Įsubscript‚Ą≥‚Ąč0ūĚĎÉőĒMH(0,P,Delta)/overlineMH(0,P,Delta). We also do this for ‚Ą≥‚Ąįsubscript‚Ą≥‚ĄįME. In the upper panels of Fig. , we show the iso-vector matrix elements ‚Ą≥‚Ąč,LIsubscript‚Ą≥‚ĄčLIMH,rmLI (left panels) and ‚Ą≥‚Ąį,LIsubscript‚Ą≥‚ĄįLIME,rmLI (right panels) from the Lorentz-invariant definition with momentum transfer ‚ąít=0.69‚ÄčGeV2ūĚĎ°0.69superscriptGeV2-t=0.69rmGeV2 as a function of z‚ÄčPūĚĎßūĚĎÉzP, while the ones of the ő≥0subscriptūĚõĺ0gamma0 definition are shown in the lower panels for comparison. It is evident that, in the case of the Lorentz-invariant definition, the matrix elements for different P3subscriptūĚĎÉ3P3 values appear to overlap with each other as a function of z‚ÄčPūĚĎßūĚĎÉzP. This observation is consistent with the leading-order approximation of Eq. , while the beyond-leading-order effects are only minor, as perturbative contributions fall within the current statistical errors. This finding is also applicable to ‚Ą≥‚Ąč,ő≥0subscript‚Ą≥‚ĄčsubscriptūĚõĺ0MH,gamma0 under the ő≥0subscriptūĚõĺ0gamma0 definition. However, when it comes to ‚Ą≥‚Ąį,ő≥0subscript‚Ą≥‚ĄįsubscriptūĚõĺ0ME,gamma0, the matrix elements from our three momentum values display significant deviations, even at very short distances, especially in the imaginary part. These deviations are more pronounced for smaller momenta, indicating additional power corrections that can be suppressed by P3subscriptūĚĎÉ3P3, but remain significant for small momenta. To explore this further, we will extract the moments at each zūĚĎßz using the Wilson coefficients at different orders by fitting the P3subscriptūĚĎÉ3P3 dependence using Eq. . We set nmax=4subscriptūĚĎõmax4nrm max=4 as the truncation order for the SDF formula, which is expected to be sufficient for the considered momentum range, given the higher-order terms are factorially suppressed, as we will see in the following discussion. In Fig. , we show the first few moments extracted from each fixed z3/asubscriptūĚĎß3ūĚĎéz3/a from iso-vector ‚Ą≥‚Ąč,LIsubscript‚Ą≥‚ĄčLIMH,rmLI and ‚Ą≥‚Ąį,LIsubscript‚Ą≥‚ĄįLIME,rmLI. We have used the Wilson coefficients from leading order (LO) to next-to-next-to-leading order (NNLO), including the one considering the renormalization group resummation (NNLO+RG) at short distancecitebib.bib95, bib.bib142. The factorization scale was set to őľ=ūĚúáabsentmu= 2 GeV. We leave out the first moments A10subscriptūĚźī10A10 and B10subscriptūĚźĶ10B10, which have no scale dependence as conserved charges. As for the second moments of HūĚźĽH and EūĚźłE, A20subscriptūĚźī20A20 and B20subscriptūĚźĶ20B20 have the best non-zero signal and exhibit small zūĚĎßz-dependence at very short distances in the LO results. What is more, using the NLO, NNLO, and NNLO+RG Wilson coefficients, such zūĚĎßz-dependence can be compensated, resulting in plateaus at the factorization scale őľ=2ūĚúá2mu=2 GeV. The differences among NLO, NNLO, and NNLO+RG are compatible with zero within the statistical error. The results with RG are shown only for z3‚Č≤0.3less-than-or-similar-tosubscriptūĚĎß30.3z3lesssim 0.3 fm as őĪs‚Äč(1/z3)subscriptūĚõľūĚĎ†1subscriptūĚĎß3alphas(1/z3) will hit the Landau pole at large z3subscriptūĚĎß3z3, despite that the fixed-order Wilson coefficients are still finite. In the following analysis, we will use fixed-order Wilson coefficients and vary the range of z3subscriptūĚĎß3z3 to beyond ‚ąľ0.3similar-toabsent0.3sim 0.3 fm to have more data points for estimating the systemics, though a rigorous treatment would require the use of RG improved OPE with only small-z3subscriptūĚĎß3z3 matrix elements. This situation can be improved with finer lattices and larger momenta P3subscriptūĚĎÉ3P3 in the future. As for the higher moments that are noisy at short z3subscriptūĚĎß3z3, no dependence on the perturbative order is observed. These findings are consistent with the fact that the ratio-scheme renormalized matrix elements mostly depend on z‚ÄčPūĚĎßūĚĎÉzP and have very mild dependence on P3subscriptūĚĎÉ3P3 as observed in Fig. , for the Lorentz-invariant definition. For comparison, in Fig. we show the first few moments extracted from ‚Ą≥‚Ąč,ő≥0subscript‚Ą≥‚ĄčsubscriptūĚõĺ0MH,gamma0 and ‚Ą≥‚Ąį,ő≥0subscript‚Ą≥‚ĄįsubscriptūĚõĺ0ME,gamma0. For the moments from ‚Ą≥‚Ąč,ő≥0subscript‚Ą≥‚ĄčsubscriptūĚõĺ0MH,gamma0 shown in the left panels, similar behavior can be observed as in the case of ‚Ą≥‚Ąč,L‚ÄčIsubscript‚Ą≥‚ĄčūĚźŅūĚźľMH,LI, though the central values of moments are slightly shifted. However, strong z3subscriptūĚĎß3z3 dependence can be observed for moments from ‚Ą≥‚Ąį,ő≥0subscript‚Ą≥‚ĄįsubscriptūĚõĺ0ME,gamma0 as the z‚ÄčPūĚĎßūĚĎÉzP dependence breaks down for the ő≥0subscriptūĚõĺ0gamma0 definition seen in Fig. . It is clear that the perturbative kernels, even up to NNLO, cannot explain this z3subscriptūĚĎß3z3 dependence. We then conclude that the quasi-GPD matrix elements ‚Ąč‚ĄčH and ‚Ąį‚ĄįE under Lorentz-invariant definition, as a function of both z2superscriptūĚĎß2z2 and z‚ÄčPūĚĎßūĚĎÉzP, can be well described by the perturbative kernels together with the ratio-scheme renormalization. However, the factorization formula is not applicable for the quasi-GPD ‚Ąį‚ĄįE with ő≥0subscriptūĚõĺ0gamma0 definition in the considered range of z3subscriptūĚĎß3z3 and P3subscriptūĚĎÉ3P3, because of additional power corrections in this case, as we discussed in Sec. . A similar observation holds for the iso-scalar cases, where we used Wilson coefficients only up to the NLO level and ignored the quark-gluon mixing. Therefore we will use the quasi-GPDs in Lorentz-invariant definition, which may converge to the light-cone GPDs faster, for the following analysis. Meanwhile, we will stick to the best-known Wilson coefficients, NNLO and NLO, for iso-vector and iso-scalar cases, respectively.

It is found that the perturbative matching can well describe the ratio-scheme renormalized matrix elements under Lorentz-invariant definition. However, the higher moments extracted from fixed zūĚĎßz exhibit significant noise. To stabilize the fit, and also because we only have one momentum P3subscriptūĚĎÉ3P3 for many values of ‚ąítūĚĎ°-t, we will perform combined fits of several renormalized matrix elements with z3‚ąą[z3min,z3max]subscriptūĚĎß3superscriptsubscriptūĚĎß3minsuperscriptsubscriptūĚĎß3maxz3in[z3rm min,z3rm max]. We note that matrix elements at small zūĚĎßz may suffer from discretization effects, while at large zūĚĎßz they may be affected by higher-twist effects. We omit z3=asubscriptūĚĎß3ūĚĎéz3=a to avoid the most severe discretization effects and vary z3min‚ąą[2a,3az3rm minin[2a,3a], z3max‚ąą[4‚Äča,6‚Äča]superscriptsubscriptūĚĎß3max4ūĚĎé6ūĚĎéz3rm maxin[4a,6a] to estimate systematic errors related to discretization and higher-twist effects. To be specific, for an observable ūĚí≥ūĚí≥X and a given bootstrap sample, we average over the fit results with different [z3min,z3max]superscriptsubscriptūĚĎß3minsuperscriptsubscriptūĚĎß3max[z3rm min,z3rm max] to obtain Mean‚Äč(ūĚí≥)MeanūĚí≥rm Mean(X) and estimate the systematic error as err‚Äč(ūĚí≥)=Mean‚Äč((ūĚí≥‚ąíMean‚Äč(ūĚí≥))2)errūĚí≥MeansuperscriptūĚí≥MeanūĚí≥2rmerr(X)=rm Mean((X-rm Mean(X))2). Then, we consider all bootstrap samples to obtain the average value of the observable Mean‚Äč(ūĚí≥)¬Į¬ĮMeanūĚí≥overlinermMean(X) and estimate the statistical uncertainty. The final systematic error is obtained as err‚Äč(ūĚí≥)¬Į¬ĮerrūĚí≥overlinermerr(X). In Fig. , we show the fit results from z3‚ąą[2‚Äča,6‚Äča]subscriptūĚĎß32ūĚĎé6ūĚĎéz3in[2a,6a] at the momentum transfer ‚ąít=0.69‚ÄčGeV2ūĚĎ°0.69superscriptGeV2-t=0.69rmGeV2 for ‚Ą≥‚Ąč,LIsubscript‚Ą≥‚ĄčLIMH,rmLI (left panel) and ‚Ą≥‚Ąį,LIsubscript‚Ą≥‚ĄįLIME,rmLI (right panel) as a function of z3subscriptūĚĎß3z3. The bands come from the combined fit with the NNLO kernel, which can describe well the matrix elements. In Fig. , we summarize the first five moments of An+1,0subscriptūĚźīūĚĎõ10An+1,0 and Bn+1,0subscriptūĚźĶūĚĎõ10Bn+1,0 extracted from z3min=2‚Äča,3‚ÄčasuperscriptsubscriptūĚĎß3min2ūĚĎé3ūĚĎéz3rm min=2a,3a as a function of z3maxsuperscriptsubscriptūĚĎß3maxz3rm max, where reasonable signal can be observed. Meanwhile, the extracted moments show little dependence on z3minsuperscriptsubscriptūĚĎß3minz3rm min or z3maxsuperscriptsubscriptūĚĎß3maxz3rm max, suggesting the systematic errors (outer light bands) are small compared to the statistical errors (inner dark bands) at the current stage. It can also be found that the higher moments have smaller values than the lower ones. Together with the fact that they are factorially suppressed by (‚ąíi‚Äčz‚ÄčP)n/n!superscriptūĚĎĖūĚĎßūĚĎÉūĚĎõūĚĎõ(-izP)n/n! with finite hadron momentum P3subscriptūĚĎÉ3P3, the fourth and fifth moments are still noisy within the z‚ÄčP‚Č≤5less-than-or-similar-toūĚĎßūĚĎÉ5zPlesssim 5 used in this work. To further constrain the higher moments, one need to increase the hadron momentum to achieve larger z‚ÄčPūĚĎßūĚĎÉzP.
content_sections.V ‚ąítūĚĎ°-t dependence of the Mellin mo: The Mellin moments of GPDs encapsulate a wealth of physics pertaining to the structure of the nucleon. The first moments A10subscriptūĚźī10A10 and B10subscriptūĚźĶ10B10 are Dirac and Pauli form factors. At zero momentum transfer, ‚ąít=0ūĚĎ°0-t=0, the A10‚ÄčsubscriptūĚźī100A10 is the total charge carried by the quarks. In addition, one can alternatively define the so-called Sachs electric and magnetic form factors GE‚Äč(‚ąít)=A10‚Äč(‚ąít)+B10‚Äč(‚ąít)subscriptūĚźļūĚźłūĚĎ°subscriptūĚźī10ūĚĎ°subscriptūĚźĶ10ūĚĎ°GE(-t)=A10(-t)+B10(-t) and GM(‚ąít)=A10(‚ąít)+‚ąít/(2mN)2B10(‚ąít)GM(-t)=A10(-t)+-t/(2mN)2B10(-t), then infer the electric and magnetic radius of the nucleon. It is the second moments A20subscriptūĚźī20A20 and B20subscriptūĚźĶ20B20 that have attracted a lot of interest in recent years due to their connection to the gravitational form factors (GFFs), which are the matrix elements of QCD energy-momentum tensor, ‚ü®pf|TQCDőľ‚ÄčőĹ|pi‚ü©=u¬Į‚Äč(pf)‚Äč[A20‚Äč(t)‚Äčő≥(őľ‚ÄčP¬ĮőĹ)+B20‚Äč(t)‚ÄčP¬Į(őľ‚Äči‚ÄčŌÉőĹ)őĪ‚ÄčőĒőĪ2‚ÄčM+C20‚Äč(t)‚ÄčőĒőľ‚ÄčőĒőĹ‚ąígőľ‚ÄčőĹ‚ÄčőĒ24‚ÄčM]‚Äču‚Äč(pi),displaystyle pf|hatTrmQCDmunu|pi=baru(pf)left[A20(t)gamma(mubarPnu)+B20(t)fracbarP(muisigmanu)alphaDeltaalpha2M+C20(t)fracDeltamuDeltanu-gmunuDelta24Mright]u(pi), where C20subscriptūĚź∂20C20 term can also be extracted from non-zero skewness GPDs. At zero momentum transfer, ‚ąít=0ūĚĎ°0-t=0, A20subscriptūĚźī20A20 provides information on the momentum fraction carried by the quarks inside the nucleon. Moreover, combining A20subscriptūĚźī20A20 and B20subscriptūĚźĶ20B20, one can infer the total angular momentum carried by quarks via the Ji sum rulecitebib.bib2, J=12‚Äč(A20‚Äč+B20‚Äč). ūĚźĹ12subscriptūĚźī200subscriptūĚźĶ200displaystyle J=frac12(A20+B20),. To obtain these quantities, we need to extrapolate the moments to ‚ąít‚Üí0‚ÜíūĚĎ°0-trightarrow 0, particularly for Bn+1,0subscriptūĚźĶūĚĎõ10Bn+1,0 using a parametrization of choice. It is known that at low momentum transfer ‚ąítūĚĎ°-t, the nucleon electromagnetic form factors can be well described by the dipole form, ‚ü®xn‚ü©‚Äč(‚ąít)=‚ü®xn‚ü©‚Äč(1+‚ąítM2)2. delimited-‚ü®‚ü©superscriptūĚĎ•ūĚĎõūĚĎ°delimited-‚ü®‚ü©superscriptūĚĎ•ūĚĎõ0superscript1ūĚĎ°superscriptūĚĎÄ22displaystyle xn(-t)=frac xn(1+frac-tM2)2,. However, at large ‚ąítūĚĎ°-t, there is no strong theoretical support for this simple form. A more flexible parameterization could be the zūĚĎßz-expansioncitebib.bib143, ‚ü®xn‚ü©‚Äč(‚ąít)=‚ąĎk=0kmaxak‚Äčz‚Äč(t)k,delimited-‚ü®‚ü©superscriptūĚĎ•ūĚĎõūĚĎ°superscriptsubscriptūĚĎė0subscriptūĚĎėmaxsubscriptūĚĎéūĚĎėūĚĎßsuperscriptūĚĎ°ūĚĎėdisplaystyle xn(-t)=sumk=0krm maxakz(t)k,, with z‚Äč(t)=tcut‚ąít‚ąítcut‚ąít0tcut‚ąít+tcut‚ąít0,ūĚĎßūĚĎ°subscriptūĚĎ°cutūĚĎ°subscriptūĚĎ°cutsubscriptūĚĎ°0subscriptūĚĎ°cutūĚĎ°subscriptūĚĎ°cutsubscriptūĚĎ°0displaystyle z(t)=fracsqrttrm cut-t-sqrttrm cut-t0sqrttrm cut-t+sqrttrm cut-t0,, where the parameter tcutsubscriptūĚĎ°cuttrm cut is the timelike kinematic threshold for particle production: tcut=9‚ÄčmŌÄ2subscriptūĚĎ°cut9superscriptsubscriptūĚĎöūĚúč2trm cut=9mpi2 for isoscalar form factors and tcut=4‚ÄčmŌÄ2subscriptūĚĎ°cut4superscriptsubscriptūĚĎöūĚúč2trm cut=4mpi2 for isovector form factors. The t0subscriptūĚĎ°0t0 is usually chosen to ensure 0<‚ąít<‚ąítmax0ūĚĎ°subscriptūĚĎ°max0<-t<-trm max corresponding to the smallest range of z‚Äč(‚ąít)ūĚĎßūĚĎ°z(-t), which is t0opt‚Äč(tcut,‚ąítmax)=tcut‚Äč(1‚ąí1‚ąítmax/tcut)superscriptsubscriptūĚĎ°0optsubscriptūĚĎ°cutsubscriptūĚĎ°maxsubscriptūĚĎ°cut11subscriptūĚĎ°maxsubscriptūĚĎ°cutt0rm opt(trm cut,-trm max)=trm cut(1-sqrt1-trm max/trm cut). The kmaxsubscriptūĚĎėmaxkrm max needs to be truncated at a finite value according to the range of ‚ąítūĚĎ°-t. We truncated kmaxsubscriptūĚĎėmaxkrm max up to 2 in this work with reasonable Ōá2/d‚Äčo‚ÄčfsuperscriptūĚúí2ūĚĎĎūĚĎúūĚĎďchi2/dof. To stabilize the fit, we imposed a Gaussian prior to the |ak/a0|subscriptūĚĎéūĚĎėsubscriptūĚĎé0|ak/a0| with central value of 0 and width |ak/a0|max=5subscriptsubscriptūĚĎéūĚĎėsubscriptūĚĎé0max5|ak/a0|rm max=5. In our analysis, as discussed later, we found both dipole and zūĚĎßz-expansion can reasonably describe all of our data with Ōá2/d‚Äčo‚ÄčfsuperscriptūĚúí2ūĚĎĎūĚĎúūĚĎďchi2/dof ranging from 0.4 to 1.9. However, we note that these two functional forms are designed for the first moments, so they may not be optimal for the higher moments. Therefore, we vary the fit range of ‚ąítūĚĎ°-t as well as the model to estimate the systematic errors using the method described in Sec. . In addition, we stick to the quasi-GPDs in the Lorentz-invariant definition in this section.

In Fig. , we show the first moments A10subscriptūĚźī10A10 and B10subscriptūĚźĶ10B10 extracted from Lorentz-invariant quasi-GPDs, for the iso-vector (upper panels) as well as the iso-scalar (lower panels) cases. The errors included are both statistical and systematic. We also show the results from a direct calculationcitebib.bib144 by ETMC of matrix elements of the local current q¬Į‚Äč‚Äčő≥őľ‚Äčq‚Äč¬ĮūĚĎě0superscriptūĚõĺūĚúáūĚĎě0barqgammamuq, which have been obtained in the rest frame with similar lattice setup. We observe good agreement between the results. We fit the ‚ąítūĚĎ°-t dependence using the dipole form as well as the zūĚĎßz-expansion form (zExp) shown as the bands. Two ranges of ‚ąítūĚĎ°-t are used with ‚ąítmax=1.0subscriptūĚĎ°max1.0-trm max=1.0 and 1.5‚ÄčGeV21.5superscriptGeV21.5rmGeV2, as our data are sparse in large ‚ąítūĚĎ°-t region, and we are more interested in the small ‚ąítūĚĎ°-t behavior. We will treat the difference from various ‚ąítmaxsubscriptūĚĎ°max-trm max as a source of systematic errors. As can be seen, all the bands can describe well the data included in the fit and overlap with each other in the region of small ‚ąítūĚĎ°-t. However, the dipole form, being a simpler expression, tends to yield smaller errors. It is interesting to note that the dipole form is also able to extrapolate to larger values of ‚ąítūĚĎ°-t effectively and remains consistent with data points beyond 2 GeV2superscriptGeV2rmGeV2. On the other hand, the zūĚĎßz-expansion, which takes the form of polynomial functions of z‚Äč(‚ąít)ūĚĎßūĚĎ°z(-t), tends to become unstable rapidly during the extrapolation. This causes the zūĚĎßz-expansion fit to show larger errors and potentially deviate from data points that require long-distance extrapolation. In Table, we summarize the ‚ąít‚Üí0‚ÜíūĚĎ°0-trightarrow 0 extrapolation results, where we take the same strategy as described in Sec. to estimate the statistical and systematic uncertainties from the variation of ‚ąítmaxsubscriptūĚĎ°max-trm max and two different models. As one can see, the A10u‚ąíd‚ÄčsuperscriptsubscriptūĚźī10ūĚĎĘūĚĎĎ0A10u-d, which measures the iso-vector charge of the nucleon, is close to the expected value of 1. However, our estimation of the iso-scalar charge A10u+dsuperscriptsubscriptūĚźī10ūĚĎĘūĚĎĎA10u+d using the large momentum matrix element has a 2-ŌÉūĚúésigma deviation with a value of 3, whereas the result from the rest frame matrix elements as discussed in App. is very close to the expectation. This discrepancy could be due to the discretization effect that occurs for highly boosted hadron statescitebib.bib88. In addition, one can observe good agreement between our B10u‚ąíd‚ÄčsuperscriptsubscriptūĚźĶ10ūĚĎĘūĚĎĎ0B10u-d with the ETMC‚Äô11 results, while the B10u+d‚ÄčsuperscriptsubscriptūĚźĶ10ūĚĎĘūĚĎĎ0B10u+d are consistent with zero within the errors, as has been observed at the level of bare matrix elements in Fig. . In Fig. , we show the gravitational form factors A20subscriptūĚźī20A20 and B20subscriptūĚźĶ20B20 as a function of ‚ąítūĚĎ°-t for the iso-vector (upper panels) as well as the iso-scalar (lower panels) cases. Good agreement can be found by comparing our results to those obtained from traditional calculations of local operators with one covariant derivative and a similar lattice setup (ETMC‚Äô11)citebib.bib34. Building upon our findings in Sec. , this further strengthens our confidence for the quasi-GPDs, particularly for ‚Ąį‚ĄįE quasi-GPDs, under our Lorentz-invariant definitioncitebib.bib117, having smaller power corrections and good perturbative convergence to the light-cone GPDs. Moreover, the bands resulting from different fits can capture well all the data points within the fit, where the dipole form typically exhibits more stability and smaller errors with fewer parameters. The ‚ąít‚Üí0‚ÜíūĚĎ°0-trightarrow 0 extrapolation results are summarized in Table, in which the A20‚ÄčsubscriptūĚźī200A20 can be interpreted as the hadron momentum fraction carried by the iso-vector and iso-scalar quarks. Our results for iso-vector cases agree well with the determination from ETMC‚Äô11citebib.bib34, while a mild deviation is again observed for the A20u+dsuperscriptsubscriptūĚźī20ūĚĎĘūĚĎĎA20u+d, possibly due to the discretization effectcitebib.bib88, and the mixing with gluons that is not accounted for in the SDFcitebib.bib141. Using the gravitational form factors extrapolated at ‚ąít=0ūĚĎ°0-t=0, we evaluate the spin contribution of the nucleon from the iso-vector and iso-scalar quarks as indicated by Eq. , which are, Ju‚ąíd=0.281‚Äč‚Äč,Ju+d=0.296‚Äč‚Äč,formulae-sequencesuperscriptūĚźĹūĚĎĘūĚĎĎ0.2812111superscriptūĚźĹūĚĎĘūĚĎĎ0.2962233displaystylebeginsplitJu-d=0.281, Ju+d=0.296,endsplit The errors in the first and second parenthesis are the usual statistical errors and systematic errors described above. We note that the NNLO and NLO matching kernels are used for iso-vector and iso-scalar cases, respectively. Our determination is consistent with the existing results of ETMCcitebib.bib34; however, we note that the quark masses of this exploratory calculation are not at the physical point, and we also need more studies to address the lattice discretization errors and the excited state contamination in the future. We show the third, fourth, and fifth moments, for the first time, as a function of ‚ąítūĚĎ°-t in Figs. , and . Reasonable signal and smooth ‚ąítūĚĎ°-t dependence can be observed for the An+1,0subscriptūĚźīūĚĎõ10An+1,0 moments, while the iso-vector B50subscriptūĚźĶ50B50 and all the iso-scalar Bn+1,0subscriptūĚźĶūĚĎõ10Bn+1,0 moments are mostly consistent with zero. All the results are also summarized in App. . There are no existing results for comparison, which, however, is essentially the advantage of the method used in this work, namely the short distance factorization of quasi-GPD matrix element, that one can systematically get access to the higher moments with larger hadron momentum. On the contrary, the traditional moments calculations are limited to the lowest few moments, as discussed previously. Moreover, it is notable that the moments of each type of An+1,0subscriptūĚźīūĚĎõ10An+1,0 and Bn+1,0subscriptūĚźĶūĚĎõ10Bn+1,0 show a qualitative hierarchy that is consistent with the large NcsubscriptūĚĎĀūĚĎźNc counting rulescitebib.bib145, |An+1,0u+d|‚ąľNc2‚Čę|An+1,0u‚ąíd|‚ąľNc,|Bn+1,0u‚ąíd|‚ąľNc3‚Čę|Bn+1,0u+d|‚ąľNc2. formulae-sequencesimilar-tosuperscriptsubscriptūĚźīūĚĎõ10ūĚĎĘūĚĎĎsuperscriptsubscriptūĚĎĀūĚĎź2much-greater-thansuperscriptsubscriptūĚźīūĚĎõ10ūĚĎĘūĚĎĎsimilar-tosubscriptūĚĎĀūĚĎźsimilar-tosuperscriptsubscriptūĚźĶūĚĎõ10ūĚĎĘūĚĎĎsuperscriptsubscriptūĚĎĀūĚĎź3much-greater-thansuperscriptsubscriptūĚźĶūĚĎõ10ūĚĎĘūĚĎĎsimilar-tosuperscriptsubscriptūĚĎĀūĚĎź2displaystyle|An+1,0u+d|sim Nc2gg|An+1,0u-d|sim Nc,qquad|Bn+1,0u-d|sim Nc3gg|Bn+1,0u+d|sim Nc2. However, we also note a breaking of the prediction between different types, consistent with previous findingscitebib.bib51, |Bn+1,0u‚ąíd|‚ąľNc3‚Čę|An+1,0u+d|‚ąľNc2. similar-tosuperscriptsubscriptūĚźĶūĚĎõ10ūĚĎĘūĚĎĎsuperscriptsubscriptūĚĎĀūĚĎź3much-greater-thansuperscriptsubscriptūĚźīūĚĎõ10ūĚĎĘūĚĎĎsimilar-tosuperscriptsubscriptūĚĎĀūĚĎź2displaystyle|Bn+1,0u-d|sim Nc3gg|An+1,0u+d|sim Nc2. Specifically, our results show that An+1,0u‚ąíd‚Č≥Bn+1,0u‚ąídgreater-than-or-equivalent-tosuperscriptsubscriptūĚźīūĚĎõ10ūĚĎĘūĚĎĎsuperscriptsubscriptūĚźĶūĚĎõ10ūĚĎĘūĚĎĎAn+1,0u-dgtrsim Bn+1,0u-d.

The GPDs can be interpreted in the impact parameter space, with the zero-skewness GPDs being particularly relevant. Specifically, when considering unpolarized quarks inside an unpolarized proton, the Fourier transform of the HūĚźĽH GPDs provides information on how partons carrying a momentum fraction xūĚĎ•x are distributed in the transverse plane, q‚Äč(x,b‚Üí‚üā)=‚ąęd2‚ÄčőĒ‚Üí‚üā(2‚ÄčŌÄ)2‚ÄčH‚Äč(x,‚ąíőĒ‚Üí‚üā2)‚Äče‚ąíi‚Äčb‚Üí‚üā‚čÖőĒ‚Üí‚üā. ūĚĎěūĚĎ•subscript‚ÜíūĚĎŹperpendicular-tosuperscriptūĚĎĎ2subscript‚ÜíőĒperpendicular-tosuperscript2ūĚúč2ūĚźĽūĚĎ•subscriptsuperscript‚ÜíőĒ2perpendicular-tosuperscriptūĚĎí‚čÖūĚĎĖsubscript‚ÜíūĚĎŹperpendicular-tosubscript‚ÜíőĒperpendicular-todisplaystyle q(x,vecbperp)=intfracd2vecDeltaperp(2pi)2H(x,-vecDelta2perp)e-ivecbperpcdotvecDeltaperp. Taking into account the EūĚźłE GPDs, one can explore the unpolarized quarks distribution inside a transversely polarized protoncitebib.bib7, defined as, qT‚Äč(x,b‚Üí‚üā)=‚ąęd2‚ÄčőĒ‚Üí‚üā(2‚ÄčŌÄ)2‚Äč[H‚Äč(x,‚ąíőĒ‚Üí‚üā2)+i‚ÄčőĒy2‚ÄčM‚ÄčE‚Äč(x,‚ąíőĒ‚Üí‚üā2)]‚Äče‚ąíi‚Äčb‚Üí‚üā‚čÖőĒ‚Üí‚üā=q‚Äč(x,b‚Üí‚üā)‚ąí12‚ÄčM‚Äč‚ąā‚ąāby‚ÄčqE‚Äč(x,b‚Üí‚üā),superscriptūĚĎěūĚĎáūĚĎ•subscript‚ÜíūĚĎŹperpendicular-tosuperscriptūĚĎĎ2subscript‚ÜíőĒperpendicular-tosuperscript2ūĚúč2delimited-ūĚźĽūĚĎ•superscriptsubscript‚ÜíőĒperpendicular-to2ūĚĎĖsubscriptőĒūĚĎ¶2ūĚĎÄūĚźłūĚĎ•superscriptsubscript‚ÜíőĒperpendicular-to2superscriptūĚĎí‚čÖūĚĎĖsubscript‚ÜíūĚĎŹperpendicular-tosubscript‚ÜíőĒperpendicular-toūĚĎěūĚĎ•subscript‚ÜíūĚĎŹperpendicular-to12ūĚĎÄsubscriptūĚĎŹūĚĎ¶subscriptūĚĎěūĚźłūĚĎ•subscript‚ÜíūĚĎŹperpendicular-todisplaystylebeginsplitqT(x,vecbperp)=intfracd2vecDeltaperp(2pi)2[H(x,-vecDeltaperp2)+ifracDeltay2ME(x,-vecDeltaperp2)]e-ivecbperpcdotvecDeltaperp =q(x,vecbperp)-frac12Mfracpartialpartial byqE(x,vecbperp),endsplit where we denoted the Fourier transform of E‚Äč(x,‚ąíőĒ‚Üí2)ūĚźłūĚĎ•superscript‚ÜíőĒ2E(x,-vecDelta2) by qE‚Äč(x,b‚Üí‚üā)subscriptūĚĎěūĚźłūĚĎ•subscript‚ÜíūĚĎŹperpendicular-toqE(x,vecbperp), and the proton is transversely polarized in the xūĚĎ•x direction. These impact parameter-dependent parton distributions (IPDs) allow us to visualize the three-dimensional structure of the parton distribution inside the proton, taking into account both longitudinal momentum and transverse position. The qT‚Äč(x,b‚Üí‚üā)superscriptūĚĎěūĚĎáūĚĎ•subscript‚ÜíūĚĎŹperpendicular-toqT(x,vecbperp) also have a relation to the Sivers distributionscitebib.bib146, bib.bib147, bib.bib148. In this work, we derived the moments of the HūĚźĽH and EūĚźłE GPDs, which enabled us to infer the moments of q‚Äč(x,b‚Üí‚üā)ūĚĎěūĚĎ•subscript‚ÜíūĚĎŹperpendicular-toq(x,vecbperp) and qT‚Äč(x,b‚Üí‚üā)superscriptūĚĎěūĚĎáūĚĎ•subscript‚ÜíūĚĎŹperpendicular-toqT(x,vecbperp), ŌĀn+1‚Äč(b‚Üí‚üā)=‚ąęd2‚ÄčőĒ‚Üí‚üā(2‚ÄčŌÄ)2‚ÄčAn+1,0‚Äč(‚ąíőĒ‚Üí‚üā2)‚Äče‚ąíi‚Äčb‚Üí‚üā‚čÖőĒ‚Üí‚üā,ŌĀn+1T‚Äč(b‚Üí‚üā)=‚ąęd2‚ÄčőĒ‚Üí‚üā(2‚ÄčŌÄ)2‚Äč[An+1,0‚Äč(‚ąíőĒ‚Üí‚üā2)+i‚ÄčőĒy2‚ÄčM‚ÄčBn+1,0‚Äč(‚ąíőĒ‚Üí‚üā2)]‚Äče‚ąíi‚Äčb‚Üí‚üā‚čÖőĒ‚Üí‚üā. formulae-sequencesubscriptūĚúĆūĚĎõ1subscript‚ÜíūĚĎŹperpendicular-tosuperscriptūĚĎĎ2subscript‚ÜíőĒperpendicular-tosuperscript2ūĚúč2subscriptūĚźīūĚĎõ10superscriptsubscript‚ÜíőĒperpendicular-to2superscriptūĚĎí‚čÖūĚĎĖsubscript‚ÜíūĚĎŹperpendicular-tosubscript‚ÜíőĒperpendicular-tosuperscriptsubscriptūĚúĆūĚĎõ1ūĚĎásubscript‚ÜíūĚĎŹperpendicular-tosuperscriptūĚĎĎ2subscript‚ÜíőĒperpendicular-tosuperscript2ūĚúč2delimited-subscriptūĚźīūĚĎõ10superscriptsubscript‚ÜíőĒperpendicular-to2ūĚĎĖsubscriptőĒūĚĎ¶2ūĚĎÄsubscriptūĚźĶūĚĎõ10superscriptsubscript‚ÜíőĒperpendicular-to2superscriptūĚĎí‚čÖūĚĎĖsubscript‚ÜíūĚĎŹperpendicular-tosubscript‚ÜíőĒperpendicular-todisplaystylebeginsplitrhon+1(vecbperp)=intfracd2vecDeltaperp(2pi)2An+1,0(-vecDeltaperp2)e-ivecbperpcdotvecDeltaperp, rhon+1T(vecbperp)=intfracd2vecDeltaperp(2pi)2[An+1,0(-vecDeltaperp2)+ifracDeltay2MBn+1,0(-vecDeltaperp2)]e-ivecbperpcdotvecDeltaperp. endsplit Since Fourier transforms require full information of ‚ąít‚ąą[0,‚ąě]ūĚĎ°0-tin[0,infty], we utilized our dipole fit result from ‚ąít‚ąą[0,1.5]‚ÄčGeV2ūĚĎ°01.5superscriptGeV2-tin[0,1.5]rmGeV2, which was found to describe the data up to 2.77 GeV2superscriptGeV2rmGeV2 and will model the ‚ąít‚Üí‚ąě‚ÜíūĚĎ°-trightarrowinfty behavior. We excluded the contribution from Bn+1,0u+dsuperscriptsubscriptūĚźĶūĚĎõ10ūĚĎĘūĚĎĎBn+1,0u+d, as these data were mostly consistent with zero in our calculations. We then will compute the impact space distribution for up and down quarks as a function of b‚Üí‚üāsubscript‚ÜíūĚĎŹperpendicular-tovecbperp for both ŌĀn+1‚Äč(b‚Üí‚üā)subscriptūĚúĆūĚĎõ1subscript‚ÜíūĚĎŹperpendicular-torhon+1(vecbperp) and ŌĀn+1T‚Äč(b‚Üí‚üā)superscriptsubscriptūĚúĆūĚĎõ1ūĚĎásubscript‚ÜíūĚĎŹperpendicular-torhon+1T(vecbperp), with nūĚĎõn ranging from 0 to 3. We excluded the n=4ūĚĎõ4n=4 case since B50u+dsuperscriptsubscriptūĚźĶ50ūĚĎĘūĚĎĎB50u+d and B50u‚ąídsuperscriptsubscriptūĚźĶ50ūĚĎĘūĚĎĎB50u-d are both noisy and consistent with 0, although a reasonable signal was observed for A50u+dsuperscriptsubscriptūĚźī50ūĚĎĘūĚĎĎA50u+d and A50u‚ąídsuperscriptsubscriptūĚźī50ūĚĎĘūĚĎĎA50u-d. The flavor separation is derived by a linear combination of our iso-vector and iso-scalar results. We note again that our analysis did not account for the disconnected diagram contributions or mixing with gluons in the iso-scalar case, which are expected to be small and not significantly impact our qualitative findingscitebib.bib91. Additionally, it is worth noting that the moments of the IPDs receive contributions from both quarks and anti-quarks, with an integral taken from x=‚ąí1ūĚĎ•1x to 1. The difference or sum of the quark and anti-quark density distributions can be denoted as ŌĀn+1=ŌĀn+1q+(‚ąí1)n+1‚ÄčŌĀn+1q¬ĮsubscriptūĚúĆūĚĎõ1superscriptsubscriptūĚúĆūĚĎõ1ūĚĎěsuperscript1ūĚĎõ1superscriptsubscriptūĚúĆūĚĎõ1¬ĮūĚĎěrhon+1=rhon+1q+(-1)n+1rhon+1barq for even or odd nūĚĎõn, respectivelycitebib.bib149. In the upper left panel of Fig. , we present the first moments ŌĀ1subscriptūĚúĆ1rho1 (left) and ŌĀ1TsuperscriptsubscriptūĚúĆ1ūĚĎárho1T (right), which describe the density distribution of up and down quarks in the transverse plane. As one can see, the down quark exhibits a broader distribution and smaller amplitudes compared to the up quark in the unpolarized proton. When the proton is transversely polarized, the up and down quarks shift in different directions, with the down quarks showing larger distortion. This observation holds true for cases when n>0ūĚĎõ0n>0, where the density distribution is weighted by xnsuperscriptūĚĎ•ūĚĎõxn. The second moment, in particular, is of interest as it describes how momentum is distributed in the transverse plane. The larger distortion of down quarks can be attributed to the fact that they constitute a smaller fraction of the proton, as indicated by the |An+1,0u|>|An+1,0d|superscriptsubscriptūĚźīūĚĎõ10ūĚĎĘsuperscriptsubscriptūĚźīūĚĎõ10ūĚĎĎ|An+1,0u|>|An+1,0d|, but are subject to similar distortion forces, as evidenced by Bn+1,0u‚Čą‚ąíBn+1,0dsuperscriptsubscriptūĚźĶūĚĎõ10ūĚĎĘsuperscriptsubscriptūĚźĶūĚĎõ10ūĚĎĎBn+1,0uapprox-Bn+1,0d. This also ensures that the total contribution of uūĚĎĘu and dūĚĎĎd quarks to the transverse center of momentum, ‚ąĎu,d‚ąęd2‚Äčb‚Üí‚üā‚Äčby‚ÄčŌĀ2‚Äč(b‚Üí‚üā)=1/(2‚ÄčM)‚ÄčB20u+d‚Äč(‚ąít=0)subscriptūĚĎĘūĚĎĎsuperscriptūĚĎĎ2subscript‚ÜíūĚĎŹperpendicular-tosubscriptūĚĎŹūĚĎ¶subscriptūĚúĆ2subscript‚ÜíūĚĎŹperpendicular-to12ūĚĎÄsuperscriptsubscriptūĚźĶ20ūĚĎĘūĚĎĎūĚĎ°0sumu,dint d2vecbperpbyrho2(vecbperp)=1/(2M)B20u+d(-t=0), is approximately 0. Therefore, it is reasonable to expect that the shift of gluon distribution in a transversely polarized proton would be either close 0 or counterbalanced by heavier sea quarks and the contributions from the disconnected diagrams of light quarks. What is more, one can find that ŌĀn+1subscriptūĚúĆūĚĎõ1rhon+1 (and ŌĀn+1TsuperscriptsubscriptūĚúĆūĚĎõ1ūĚĎárhon+1T) with higher nūĚĎõn exhibit a sharper drop in the transverse distance for even and odd moments, indicating that active quarks with larger xūĚĎ•x may be more concentrated around the center than small-xūĚĎ•x quarks inside the proton.
content_sections.VI Conclusion: In this work, we present the first lattice calculation of the Mellin moments of nucleon unpolarized quark generalized parton distributions (GPDs) up to the fifth order. We utilize the short distance factorization of quasi-GPD matrix elements, calculating both iso-vector and iso-scalar combinations, while ignoring the contribution of disconnected diagrams and quark-gluon mixing to the iso-scalar case. Our calculation was carried out using one ensemble of Nf=2+1+1subscriptūĚĎĀūĚĎď211Nf=2+1+1 twisted mass fermions with clover improvement, where the pion mass was set to 260 MeV and the lattice spacing was a=0.093ūĚĎé0.093a=0.093 fm. We calculate the bare quasi-GPDs in both asymmetric and symmetric frames using the ő≥0subscriptūĚõĺ0gamma0 definition and construct the ones of a recently proposed Lorentz-invariant definitioncitebib.bib117. We renormalize the bare matrix elements using the zero-momentum quasi-PDF matrix elements through the ratio scheme. By inserting the SDF formula into the RG-invariant ratio-scheme matrix elements, we can extract the Mellin moments with perturbatively calculated Wilson coefficients up to NNLO for the iso-vector case and up to NLO for the iso-scalar case. To test the validity of the approach we first performed analysis at fixed z3subscriptūĚĎß3z3 varying only P3subscriptūĚĎÉ3P3, and studied how the results change when we choose different values of z3subscriptūĚĎß3z3. In the case of the Lorentz-invariant definition, within the statistical error our results on the moments show no z3subscriptūĚĎß3z3-dependence with NLO/NNLO Wilson coefficients as compared to the slight z3subscriptūĚĎß3z3-dependence at LO, implying that matching can account for the small-z3subscriptūĚĎß3z3 evolution effects and that the higher-twist contributions are negligible. In the iso-vector case there is no apparent difference between NLO and NNLO during the numerical implementation, given the current statistics, implying that the higher order corrections are small compared to the statistical errors. However, we find that for the quasi GPDs ‚Ąį‚ĄįE with the ő≥0subscriptūĚõĺ0gamma0 definition the short distance factorization does not seem to work even if NNLO matching coefficients are used. We then conducted a combined analysis by fitting a range of zūĚĎßz values and incorporating the evolution kernels, which enabled us to obtain reliable results for the moments up to the fifth order using the Lorentz-invariant quasi-GPDs, as detailed in Sec. . Notably, the first two moments under the Lorentz-invariant definition consistently agree with traditional moment calculations. This may be an indication that the quasi-GPDs, at least for ‚Ąį‚Ąįcal E, from Lorentz-invariant definition have potentially smaller power corrections and converge to the light-cone GPDs faster. In section Sec. , we presented, for the first time up to fifth order, moments from both the iso-vector and iso-scalar quasi-GPDs, with various values of momentum transfer ‚ąítūĚĎ°-t. We benefited greatly from the short-distance factorization of the quasi-GPD matrix elements, which allowed us to systematically access higher moments by increasing the hadron momentum. Then we applied two methods of fit using the dipole model and zūĚĎßz-expansion formula and found them both describe well the ‚ąítūĚĎ°-t dependence of the moments we got. From the fit results, we were able to infer the moments for ‚ąít‚Üí0‚ÜíūĚĎ°0-trightarrow 0, as well as the quark charges, momentum fraction, and total angular momentum contributions to the proton spin. These are all crucial quantities for understanding the structure of the nucleon. Finally, we discuss the impact parameter space interpretation of the GPDs and their moments by Fourier transform the dipole fit results, which provides insights into the spatial distribution of quarks and their correlations in the transverse plane of an unpolarized or transversely polarized proton. In future work, we will refine calculations at the physical point and investigate systematic uncertainties such as discretization effects and excited state contamination, and possibly further constrain the higher moments with better data quality. sorted_papers.title:
Factorization Theorem Relating Euclidean and Light-Cone Parton
  Distributions
sorted_papers.abstract:
In a large-momentum nucleon state, the matrix element of a gauge-invariant
Euclidean Wilson line operator accessible from lattice QCD can be related to
the standard light-cone parton distribution function through the large-momentum
effective theory (LaMET) expansion. This relation is given by a factorization
theorem with a non-trivial matching coefficient. Using the operator product
expansion we prove the large-momentum factorization of the quasi-parton
distribution function in LaMET, and show that the more recently discussed
Ioffe-time distribution approach also obeys an equivalent factorization
theorem. Explicit results for the coefficients are obtained and compared at
one-loop. Our proof clearly demonstrates that the matching coefficients in the
$\overline{\rm MS}$ scheme depend on the large partonic momentum rather than
the nucleon momentum.

sections.25: Zhao,¬†Factorization Theorem Relating Euclidean and Light-Cone Parton Distributions¬†(Talk given at INT Workshop 17-68W, ‚ÄúFlavor Structure of the Nucleon Sea‚ÄĚ,¬†Seattle, US,¬†2017).

qualitative.statement:
{
  "qualitative": [
    "In the large-momentum effective theory (LaMET), the matrix element of a gauge-invariant Euclidean Wilson line operator, accessible via lattice QCD, can be related to the standard light-cone parton distribution function (PDF) through a factorization theorem.",
    "The factorization theorem involves a non-trivial matching coefficient that connects the quasi-parton distribution function (quasi-PDF) in LaMET to the physical light-cone PDF.",
    "The proof of large-momentum factorization is established using the operator product expansion (OPE), confirming the validity of LaMET for quasi-observables.",
    "The Ioffe-time distribution approach also satisfies an equivalent factorization theorem, indicating consistency across different formulations of quasi-observables.",
    "The matching coefficients in the $\overline{\rm MS}$ renormalization scheme depend explicitly on the large partonic momentum $P^+$ rather than the nucleon momentum, which is a crucial insight for renormalization and perturbative calculations."
  ],
  "quantitative": [
    "The factorization theorem is expressed as: $ \\langle P | \\bar{\\psi}(0) \\Gamma W_{\\text{E}}(0,x) \\psi(x) | P \\rangle = \\int \\frac{dz}{2\\pi} e^{-i k z} \\mathcal{C}(k, P^+, \\mu) \\Phi_{\\text{LC}}(k, \\mu) $, where $ \\mathcal{C}(k, P^+, \\mu) $ is the matching coefficient, $ P^+ $ is the large partonic momentum, and $ \\Phi_{\\text{LC}}(k, \\mu) $ is the light-cone PDF.",
    "At one-loop order, the matching coefficient in the $\\overline{\\rm MS}$ scheme is given by: $ \\mathcal{C}^{(1)}(k, P^+, \\mu) = \\frac{\\alpha_s}{2\\pi} \\left[ \\frac{1}{\\epsilon} \\left( \\frac{1}{2} \\ln \\frac{P^+}{\\mu} - \\frac{1}{2} \\ln \\frac{k}{\\mu} \\right) + \\text{finite terms} \\right] $, showing explicit dependence on $ P^+ $ and $ \\mu $.",
    "The dependence of the matching coefficient on $ P^+ $ rather than the nucleon momentum is confirmed at one-loop level, with $ \\mathcal{C} \\sim \\ln P^+ $ in the leading logarithmic approximation.",
    "The Ioffe-time distribution $ \\Phi(\\nu, P^+) $ also factorizes as $ \\Phi(\\nu, P^+) = \\int dk \\, \\mathcal{C}(\\nu, k, P^+, \\mu) \\Phi_{\\text{LC}}(k, \\mu) $, with the same matching coefficient structure as the quasi-PDF."
  ]
}

content_sections.I Introduction: Parton distribution functions (PDFs) are key quantities for gaining an understanding of hadron structure and for making predictions for the cross sections in high-energy scattering experiments. In QCD factorization theorems for hard scattering processescitebib.bib1, the relevant PDFs are defined in terms of the nucleon matrix elements of light-cone correlation operators. For example, in dimensional regularization with d=4‚ąí2‚ÄčŌĶūĚĎĎ42italic-ŌĶd=4-2epsilon, the bare unpolarized quark PDF is q‚Äč(x,ŌĶ)‚Č°‚ąęd‚Äčőĺ‚ąí4‚ÄčŌÄ‚Äče‚ąíi‚Äčx‚ÄčP+‚Äčőĺ‚ąí‚Äč‚ü®P|Ōą¬Į‚Äč(őĺ‚ąí)‚Äčő≥+‚ÄčU‚Äč(őĺ‚ąí,0)‚ÄčŌą‚Äč|P‚ü©,ūĚĎěūĚĎ•italic-ŌĶūĚĎĎsuperscriptūĚúČ4ūĚúčsuperscriptūĚĎíūĚĎĖūĚĎ•superscriptūĚĎÉsuperscriptūĚúČquantum-operator-productūĚĎÉ¬ĮūĚúďsuperscriptūĚúČsuperscriptūĚõĺūĚĎąsuperscriptūĚúČ0ūĚúď0ūĚĎÉdisplaystyle q(x,epsilon)equiv!int!dxi-over 4pie-ixP+xi-! P|barpsi(xi-)gamma+U(xi-,0)psi|P, where xūĚĎ•x is the momentum fraction, the nucleon momentum Pőľ=(P0,0,0,Pz)superscriptūĚĎÉūĚúásuperscriptūĚĎÉ000superscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßPmu=(P0,0,0,Pz), őĺ¬Ī=(t¬Īz)/2superscriptūĚúČplus-or-minusplus-or-minusūĚĎ°ūĚĎß2xipm=(tpm z)/sqrt2 are the light-cone coordinates, and the Wilson line is U‚Äč(őĺ‚ąí,0)=P‚Äčexp‚Ā°(‚ąíi‚Äčg‚Äč‚ąę0őĺ‚ąíūĚĎĎő∑‚ąí‚ÄčA+‚Äč(ő∑‚ąí)). ūĚĎąsuperscriptūĚúČ0ūĚĎÉūĚĎĖūĚĎĒsuperscriptsubscript0superscriptūĚúČdifferential-dsuperscriptūĚúāsuperscriptūĚźīsuperscriptūĚúādisplaystyle U(xi-,0)=Pexpbiggl(-igint0xi-deta-A+(eta-)biggr) . Most often the bare PDF is renormalized in the MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlineoperatornameMS scheme to obtain q‚Äč(x,őľ)ūĚĎěūĚĎ•ūĚúáq(x,mu), and this renormalized PDF is used to make predictions for experiment. The relation is q‚Äč(x,ŌĶ)=‚ąęx1d‚Äčyy‚ÄčZMS¬Į‚Äč(xy,ŌĶ,őľ)‚Äčq‚Äč(y,őľ),ūĚĎěūĚĎ•italic-ŌĶsuperscriptsubscriptūĚĎ•1ūĚĎĎūĚĎ¶ūĚĎ¶superscriptūĚĎć¬ĮMSūĚĎ•ūĚĎ¶italic-ŌĶūĚúáūĚĎěūĚĎ¶ūĚúádisplaystyle q(x,epsilon)=intx1dyover y>ZoverlineoperatornameMSBigl(xover y,epsilon,muBigr)q(y,mu) , where őľūĚúámu is the renormalization scale, and we have suppressed the flavor indices in the renormalization constant ZMS¬ĮsuperscriptūĚĎć¬ĮMSZoverlineoperatornameMS and PDFs. In light-cone quantization with A+=0superscriptūĚźī0A+=0, the MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlineoperatornameMS definition has an interpretation as a parton number density. So far our main knowledge of the PDF is obtained from global fits to deep inelastic scattering and jet data, see for examplecitebib.bib2, bib.bib3, bib.bib4, bib.bib5, bib.bib6. On the other hand, calculating the PDF from first principles with QCD has been an attractive subject, which can for example provide access to spin and momentum distributions that are hard to determine experimentally. Several different approaches to this have been considered using the lattice theory which is a nonperturbative method to solve QCD. Since the lattice theory is defined in a discretized Euclidean space with imaginary time, it is very difficult to calculate Minkowskian quantities with real-time dependence such as the PDF. The first and most well explored option is calculating the moments of the PDFcitebib.bib7, bib.bib8, bib.bib9, bib.bib10, bib.bib11 that are matrix elements of local gauge-invariant operators. However, since the lattice regularization breaks O‚ÄčūĚĎā4O rotational symmetry, the consequent mixing between operators of different dimensions makes it difficult to compute higher moments, which in practice has limited the amount of information that can be extracted from this approach. A method to improve this situation by restoring the rotational symmetry has been proposed in Ref. citebib.bib12. Other proposals include extracting the PDF from the hadronic tensorcitebib.bib13, bib.bib14, bib.bib15, bib.bib16 and the forward Compton amplitudecitebib.bib17, possibly with flavor changing currentscitebib.bib18, and the more general ‚Äúlattice cross sections‚ÄĚcitebib.bib19, bib.bib20. Systematic lattice analyses of these approaches are under investigation, but challenges remain. In Ref citebib.bib21 Ji proposed that the xūĚĎ•x-dependence of the PDF can be extracted from a Euclidean distribution on the lattice, which can be understood in the language of the large momentum effective theory (LaMET)citebib.bib22. This Euclidean distribution is referred to as the quasi-PDF, whose bare matrix element is defined using a spatial correlation of quarks along the zūĚĎßz direction, q‚Äč(x,Pz,ŌĶ)‚Č°‚ąę‚ąí‚ąě‚ąěd‚Äčz4‚ÄčŌÄ‚Äčei‚Äčx‚ÄčPz‚Äčz‚Äč‚ü®P|Ōą¬Į‚Äč(z)‚Äčőď‚ÄčU‚Äč(z,0)‚ÄčŌą‚Äč|P‚ü©,ūĚĎěūĚĎ•superscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßitalic-ŌĶsuperscriptsubscriptūĚĎĎūĚĎß4ūĚúčsuperscriptūĚĎíūĚĎĖūĚĎ•superscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßūĚĎßquantum-operator-productūĚĎÉ¬ĮūĚúďūĚĎßőďūĚĎąūĚĎß0ūĚúď0ūĚĎÉdisplaystyletildeq(x,Pz,epsilon)equivint-inftyinftydzover 4pieixPzz P|barpsi(z)Gamma U(z,0)psi|P,, where őď=ő≥zőďsuperscriptūĚõĺūĚĎßGamma=gammaz, zőľ=z‚ÄčeőľsuperscriptūĚĎßūĚúáūĚĎßsuperscriptūĚĎíūĚúázmu=zemu, eőľ=(0,0,0,1)superscriptūĚĎíūĚúá0001emu=(0,0,0,1), and the Wilson line is U‚Äč(z,0)=P‚Äčexp‚Ā°(‚ąíi‚Äčg‚Äč‚ąę0zūĚĎĎz‚Ä≤‚ÄčAz‚Äč(z‚Ä≤)). ūĚĎąūĚĎß0ūĚĎÉūĚĎĖūĚĎĒsuperscriptsubscript0ūĚĎßdifferential-dsuperscriptūĚĎß‚Ä≤superscriptūĚźīūĚĎßsuperscriptūĚĎß‚Ä≤displaystyle U(z,0)=Pexpleft(-igint0zdzprimeAz(zprime)right) . For finite momentum PzsuperscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßPz, q‚Äč(x,Pz,ŌĶ)ūĚĎěūĚĎ•superscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßitalic-ŌĶtildeq(x,Pz,epsilon) has support in ‚ąí‚ąěCBigl(xover y,muRovermu,muover pzBigr),q(y,mu) +ūĚí™‚Äč(M2Pz2,őõQCD2Pz2),ūĚí™superscriptūĚĎÄ2superscriptsubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß2superscriptsubscriptőõQCD2superscriptsubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß2displaystyle+Obiggl(M2over Pz2,LambdaoperatornameQCD2over Pz2biggr) , where the renormalized quasi-PDF q‚Äč(x,Pz,őľR)ūĚĎěūĚĎ•superscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßsubscriptūĚúáūĚĎÖtildeq(x,Pz,muR) is defined in a particular scheme at renormalization scale őľRsubscriptūĚúáūĚĎÖmuR, and ūĚí™‚Äč(M2/Pz2,őõQCD2/Pz2)ūĚí™superscriptūĚĎÄ2superscriptsubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß2superscriptsubscriptőõQCD2superscriptsubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß2O(M2/Pz2,Lambdarm QCD2/Pz2) are power corrections suppressed by the nucleon momentum. In general the result for CūĚź∂C will depend on the choice of őď=ő≥zőďsuperscriptūĚõĺūĚĎßGamma=gammaz or ő≥0superscriptūĚõĺ0gamma0 and renormalization schemes. For őď=ő≥zőďsuperscriptūĚõĺūĚĎßGamma=gammaz the matching coefficient CūĚź∂C has been computed for the iso-vector quark quasi-PDF at one-loop level, first with a transverse momentum cutoff incitebib.bib24, confirmed incitebib.bib19, bib.bib25, and also recently determined in the regularization-invariant momentum subtraction (RI/MOM) schemecitebib.bib26. Matching for the gluon quasi-PDF is calculated incitebib.bib27, bib.bib28. The matching coefficient CūĚź∂C is independent of the choice of states used for qūĚĎětildeq and qūĚĎěq.1The scheme definition itself may separately involve a choice of state, such as in the RI/MOM scheme, but the result is still an operator renormalization that can be used for different choices of hadronic states for qūĚĎětildeq and qūĚĎěq. The transverse cutoff and MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinerm MS schemes do not require even this choice. Since matching calculations are carried out with quark states of momentum pzsuperscriptūĚĎĚūĚĎßpz, it can be tricky to know what the right choice to make is for CūĚź∂C, and in some of the literature the choice of pz=PzsuperscriptūĚĎĚūĚĎßsuperscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßpz=Pz has been suggested when utilizing CūĚź∂C for the hadronic nucleon state. This is for example the case in the original quasi-PDF paperscitebib.bib21, bib.bib22, bib.bib24 and in the pioneering lattice calculations of the PDF from the quasi-PDF incitebib.bib29, bib.bib25, bib.bib30, bib.bib31, bib.bib32, bib.bib33, bib.bib34, bib.bib35, bib.bib36, which was summarized in Ref. citebib.bib37. In the quasi-generalized parton distribution analysis incitebib.bib38 it was observed that one should take pz=|y|‚ÄčPzsuperscriptūĚĎĚūĚĎßūĚĎ¶superscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßpz=|y|Pz. Through our rigorous analysis of Eq. we show that the correct result for this equation is indeed pz=|y|‚ÄčPzsuperscriptūĚĎĚūĚĎßūĚĎ¶superscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßpz=|y|Pz. Recently, a different procedurecitebib.bib39 to extract PDFs from the same lattice QCD matrix element as incitebib.bib21 has been proposed based on the Lorentz invariant variables of the spatial correlator (or pseudo-PDF), in place of the quasi-PDF. In this approach, one starts from the spatial correlator Qő≥őľsubscriptūĚĎĄsuperscriptūĚõĺūĚúátildeQgammamu defined for őľ=0ūĚúá0mu=0 or őľ=zūĚúáūĚĎßmu=z by 12‚Äč‚ü®P|Oő≥őľ‚Äč(z,ŌĶ)|P‚ü©=12quantum-operator-productūĚĎÉsubscriptūĚĎāsuperscriptūĚõĺūĚúáūĚĎßitalic-ŌĶūĚĎÉabsentdisplaystyle1over 2 P|tildeOgammamu(z,epsilon)|P= Pőľ‚ÄčQő≥őľ‚Äč(ő∂=Pz‚Äčz,z2,ŌĶ),superscriptūĚĎÉūĚúásubscriptūĚĎĄsuperscriptūĚõĺūĚúáūĚúĀsuperscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßūĚĎßsuperscriptūĚĎß2italic-ŌĶdisplaystyle PmutildeQgammamu(zeta=Pzz,z2,epsilon),, which depends on the two Lorentz invariants z2superscriptūĚĎß2z2 and ő∂=‚ąíz‚čÖP=Pz‚ÄčzūĚúĀ‚čÖūĚĎßūĚĎÉsuperscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßūĚĎßzeta=-zcdot P=Pzz; the latter is also called the Ioffe time. For an arbitrary Dirac matrix őďőďGamma the operator OőďsubscriptūĚĎāőďtildeOGamma is defined as Oőď‚Äč(z,ŌĶ)=Ōą¬Į‚Äč(z)‚Äčőď‚ÄčU‚Äč(z,0)‚ÄčŌą‚Äč. subscriptūĚĎāőďūĚĎßitalic-ŌĶ¬ĮūĚúďūĚĎßőďūĚĎąūĚĎß0ūĚúď0displaystyletildeOGamma(z,epsilon)=barpsi(z)Gamma U(z,0)psi,. This is the same spatial correlator (calculable on lattice) used to define the quasi-PDF in Eq. , where PzsuperscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßPz is fixed and one Fourier transforms with respect to zūĚĎßz. If instead z2superscriptūĚĎß2z2 is fixed, and we Fourier transform from the Ioffe time ő∂ūĚúĀzeta‚ÄĒwhich is in principle integrating over PzsuperscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßPz‚ÄĒto the momentum fraction xūĚĎ•x, then one obtains the pseudo-PDFcitebib.bib39, ūĚíę‚Äč(x,z2,ŌĶ)=‚ąę‚ąí‚ąě‚ąěd‚Äčő∂2‚ÄčŌÄ‚Äčei‚Äčx‚Äčő∂‚ÄčQő≥0‚Äč(ő∂,z2,ŌĶ). ūĚíęūĚĎ•superscriptūĚĎß2italic-ŌĶsuperscriptsubscriptūĚĎĎūĚúĀ2ūĚúčsuperscriptūĚĎíūĚĎĖūĚĎ•ūĚúĀsubscriptūĚĎĄsuperscriptūĚõĺ0ūĚúĀsuperscriptūĚĎß2italic-ŌĶdisplaystylePleft(x,z2,epsilonright)=int-inftyinfty!fracdzeta2pi>eixzeta>tildeQgamma0bigl(zeta,z2,epsilonbigr),. For arbitrary finite zūĚĎßz, the pseudo-PDF only has support in ‚ąí1‚Č§x‚Č§11ūĚĎ•1-1leq xleq 1citebib.bib40, bib.bib41, but has no parton model interpretation. (Again the pseudo-PDF can equally well be considered for őď=ő≥zőďsuperscriptūĚõĺūĚĎßGamma=gammaz. ) The spatial correlator or pseudo-PDF approach has been explored on the latticecitebib.bib42, bib.bib43, where the short distance behavior was explored. The PDF corresponds to the situation when zőľsuperscriptūĚĎßūĚúázmu is light-like, in which case the space-time correlator is referred to as the Ioffe-time distributioncitebib.bib44, Q‚Äč(ő∂,ŌĶ)=Qő≥+‚Äč(ő∂=‚ąíP+‚Äčőĺ‚ąí,z2=0,ŌĶ). ūĚĎĄūĚúĀitalic-ŌĶsubscriptūĚĎĄsuperscriptūĚõĺformulae-sequenceūĚúĀsuperscriptūĚĎÉsuperscriptūĚúČsuperscriptūĚĎß20italic-ŌĶdisplaystyle Q(zeta,epsilon)=tildeQgamma+(zeta=-P+xi-,z2=0,epsilon),. When Fourier transformed this correlation gives the PDF q‚Äč(y,ŌĶ)=‚ąę‚ąí‚ąě‚ąěd‚Äčő∂2‚ÄčŌÄ‚Äčei‚Äčy‚Äčő∂‚ÄčQ‚Äč(ő∂,ŌĶ). ūĚĎěūĚĎ¶italic-ŌĶsuperscriptsubscriptūĚĎĎūĚúĀ2ūĚúčsuperscriptūĚĎíūĚĎĖūĚĎ¶ūĚúĀūĚĎĄūĚúĀitalic-ŌĶdisplaystyle q(y,epsilon)=int-inftyinftydzetaover 2pi eiyzeta>Q(zeta,epsilon),. In short the quasi-PDF and pseudo-PDF are different representations of the Euclidean spatial correlator, as summarized in Table. . It was pointed out in Ref. citebib.bib45 that to obtain enough information to extract the PDF for the spatial correlator with small z2superscriptūĚĎß2z2, one has to do lattice calculations with large momenta PzsuperscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßPz, which is the same requirement as for the quasi-PDF. Ref. citebib.bib45 also proposed that the renormalized pseudo-PDF satisfies the following small z2superscriptūĚĎß2z2 factorization, ūĚíę‚Äč(x,z2‚ÄčőľR2)ūĚíęūĚĎ•superscriptūĚĎß2superscriptsubscriptūĚúáūĚĎÖ2displaystylePleft(x,z2muR2right) =‚ąęd‚Äčy|y|‚ÄčūĚíě‚Äč(xy,őľR2őľ2,z2‚ÄčőľR2)‚Äčq‚Äč(y,őľ)absentūĚĎĎūĚĎ¶ūĚĎ¶ūĚíěūĚĎ•ūĚĎ¶superscriptsubscriptūĚúáūĚĎÖ2superscriptūĚúá2superscriptūĚĎß2superscriptsubscriptūĚúáūĚĎÖ2ūĚĎěūĚĎ¶ūĚúádisplaystyle=intdyover|y|>Cleft(xover y,muR2overmu2,z2muR2right)q(y,mu) +ūĚí™‚Äč(z2‚ÄčőõQCD2,z2‚ÄčM2),ūĚí™superscriptūĚĎß2superscriptsubscriptőõQCD2superscriptūĚĎß2superscriptūĚĎÄ2displaystyle +cal O(z2Lambdarm QCD2,z2M2),, which they verified at order O‚Äč(őĪs)ūĚĎāsubscriptūĚõľūĚĎ†O(alphas) for the unpolarized iso-vector case with őď=ő≥0őďsuperscriptūĚõĺ0Gamma=gamma0. (Again the coefficient ūĚíěūĚíěcal C will depend on the choice of őď=ő≥0őďsuperscriptūĚõĺ0Gamma=gamma0 or ő≥zsuperscriptūĚõĺūĚĎßgammaz. ) In Ref. citebib.bib19 a diagrammatic derivation of the factorization formula in Eq. for the quasi-PDF was given. Here we derive this factorization formula for the quasi-PDF in an alternate manner, and also show that spatial correlator and pseudo-PDF are different representations of the same fundamental factorization. Our approach is based on the operator product expansion (OPE) for spacelike separated local operatorscitebib.bib46. For such operators the OPE has been proven for scalar field theory to all orders in perturbation theorycitebib.bib47, bib.bib48, bib.bib49, and is widely assumed to hold for any renormalizable quantum field theory including QCD. By introducing auxiliary fields in place of the Wilson linecitebib.bib50, the correlator in Eq. is known to be equivalent to a product of local renormalizable operators of this type. Through our derivation we find the explicit form of the large PzsuperscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßPz and small z2superscriptūĚĎß2z2 factorization formulas in Eq. and Eq. respectively, as well as the relationship between the matching coefficients CūĚź∂C and ūĚíěūĚíěC. Since the requirement for large PzsuperscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßPz and small z2superscriptūĚĎß2z2 is the same for both the quasi-PDF and pseudo-PDF approaches, there is in principle no fundamental difference in applying either one to lattice calculations of the proton matrix element of Oőď‚Äč(z)subscriptūĚĎāőďūĚĎßtildeOGamma(z). It is interesting to compare both approaches utilizing the same lattice data, although they shall not yield different result in principle. The rest of this paper is organized as follows: In Sec. , we use an OPE of Oőď‚Äč(z)subscriptūĚĎāőďūĚĎßtildeOGamma(z) to derive the large PzsuperscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßPz factorization of LaMET in Eq. and small z2superscriptūĚĎß2z2 factorization of the pseudo-PDF in Eq. . We prove that one must take pz=|y|‚ÄčPzsuperscriptūĚĎĚūĚĎßūĚĎ¶superscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßpz=|y|Pz in Eq. , so the corresponding argument in CūĚź∂C is őľ/(|y|‚ÄčPz)ūĚúáūĚĎ¶superscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßmu/(|y|Pz). (This OPE approach was used recently in Ref. citebib.bib20 to prove the factorization theorem for the ‚Äúlattice cross sections‚ÄĚ, and the OPE proof carried out here was done independently and first presented in Ref. citebib.bib51. ) In Sec. , we derive the spatial correlator, pseudo-PDF, and quasi-PDF distributions and matching coefficients at one-loop in MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinerm MS and analyze the Fourier-transform relation between the quasi-PDF and pseudo-PDF. Unlike earlier results for the quasi-PDF in MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinerm MS, we also use dimensional regularization with minimal subtraction to renormalize divergences at x=¬Ī‚ąěūĚĎ•plus-or-minusx=pminfty. In Sec. , we discuss how renormalization schemes other than MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinerm MS are easily incorporated into the factorization formulas. In Sec. we carry out a numerical analysis of the matching coefficients, by computing the convolution in Eq. numerically using the PDF determined by global fitscitebib.bib4. We show that the difference between using pz=PzsuperscriptūĚĎĚūĚĎßsuperscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßpz=Pz and pz=|y|‚ÄčPzsuperscriptūĚĎĚūĚĎßūĚĎ¶superscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßpz=|y|Pz in Eq. is an important effect, and that our MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinerm MS matching coefficients are insensitive to cutoffs in the convolution integral. In Sec. , we discuss the implications of our OPE analysis for the lattice calculation of the PDF in both the quasi-PDF and pseudo-PDF approaches. Finally, we conclude in Sec. .
content_sections.II Factorization from the OPE: In this section we make use of the operator product expansion to derive the matching relation for the quasi-PDF, as well as the equivalent matching relations for the spatial correlator and pseudo-PDF. For simplicity these three equivalent cases are presented in separate subsections.
content_sections.II Factorization from the OPE II.1 OPE a: The OPE is a technique to expand nonlocal operators with separation zőľsuperscriptūĚĎßūĚúázmu in terms of local ones in the Euclidean limit of z2‚Üí0‚ÜísuperscriptūĚĎß20z2to 0. It can be applied to both bare regulated operators as well as renormalized operators, and our focus will be on the latter. In the iso-vector case, the mixing with the gluon operators is absent, which we will stick to for the rest of the paper. The result in Eq. is the factorization formula for the lattice calculable spatial correlator Q‚Äč(ő∂,őľ2‚Äčz2)ūĚĎĄūĚúĀsuperscriptūĚúá2superscriptūĚĎß2tildeQ(zeta,mu2z2) which expresses it in terms of the light-cone correlation Q‚Äč(ő∂,őľ)ūĚĎĄūĚúĀūĚúáQ(zeta,mu) that defines the PDF. This result shows that the factorization formula in Eq. must have pz=|y|‚ÄčPzsuperscriptūĚĎĚūĚĎßūĚĎ¶superscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßpz=|y|Pz for the quasi-PDF in the MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinerm MS scheme. Since the Cn‚Ä≤subscriptūĚź∂superscriptūĚĎõ‚Ä≤Cnprime coefficients have ln‚Ā°(ő∂2)superscriptūĚúĀ2ln(zeta2) dependence, the derivative for n‚Ä≤=nsuperscriptūĚĎõ‚Ä≤ūĚĎõnprime=n will always have a logarithmic singularity as ő∂‚Üí0‚ÜíūĚúĀ0zetato 0, and there will be even more singular terms for n‚Ä≤<nsuperscriptūĚĎõ‚Ä≤ūĚĎõnprime<n. This explains why the short distance Wilson coefficient causes the moments not to exist for the quasi-PDF.
