To obtain the quasi-DAs from the lattice simulations, one needs to consider their short and long-distance properties separately. From this viewpoint, it is advantageous to work in the coordinate space. To explicitly demonstrate the ultraviolet and infrared structures, we perform a perturbative calculation of quasi-DAs by sandwiching the operators between the vacuum state ‚ü®0|bra0left 0|right. and the lowest-order Fock state |u‚Äčd‚Äčs‚ü©ketūĚĎĘūĚĎĎūĚĎ†left. |udsright: Mp(z1,z2,z3=0,Pz,őľ)=‚ü®0|uT(z1)őďd(z2)s|u(x1P)d(x2P)s(x3P)‚ü©R,Mp(z1,z2,z3=0,Pz,mu)=left 0left|uTleft(z1right)widetildeGammadleft(z2right)sleft(0right)right|u(x1P)d(x2P)s(x3P)rightR, where the lower index ‚ÄúpūĚĎĚp‚ÄĚ in MpsubscriptūĚĎÄūĚĎĚMp denotes the perturbative calculation. The next-to-leading order Feynman diagrams are shown in Fig. , and the calculation details are collected in Appendix. The diagrams in Fig. are categorized by the different divergences that show up, which is equivalent to the manner of gluon attachment to quarks or Wilson lines. For the quark-gluon-quark pattern collected in the first line, only infrared (IR) divergences exist. For the Wislon-line self-energy type diagrams shown in the fourth line, there are only ultraviolet (UV) divergences. For the quark-gluon-Wilson-line exchange diagrams in the second and third lines, both UV and IR divergences appear in the perturbative result. In the large PzsuperscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßPz limit, the quasi and lightcone distribution amplitudes can be related through the QCD factorization. After separating the hard and collinear contributions, one can factorize the quasi-DAs in terms of the LCDAs and a hard kernel which can be perturbatively calculated. x1y1+y2. endarrayright. It should be noticed that the above matching of quasi-DAs in the MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinerm MS scheme is problematic which can be understood as follows. As shown in Eq. , the double plus function contains an integral over the momentum fractions x1‚Ä≤superscriptsubscriptūĚĎ•1‚Ä≤x1prime and x2‚Ä≤superscriptsubscriptūĚĎ•2‚Ä≤x2prime which arises from the so-called virtual corrections to DAscitebib.bib13. In the x1‚Ä≤,x2‚Ä≤‚Üí‚ąě‚ÜísuperscriptsubscriptūĚĎ•1‚Ä≤superscriptsubscriptūĚĎ•2‚Ä≤x1prime,x2primetoinfty limit the integrals have the asymptotic form ‚ąęūĚĎĎx‚Ä≤/x‚Ä≤differential-dsuperscriptūĚĎ•‚Ä≤superscriptūĚĎ•‚Ä≤int dxprime/xprime and are then divergent. This divergence corresponds to the logarithmic UV divergence and should be renormalized. However, in deriving the above matching kernel, one has only calculated the so-called real diagrams to quasi-DAs and assumed that virtual contributions are the integration of real diagrams and thus combining them gives the double plus function. Keeping the remnant UV divergence leads to an improper definition of double plus function. In Ref. citebib.bib39, a properly defined plus function is introduced for quasi parton distribution functions through the subtraction at the infinite momentum, while in Ref. citebib.bib13, the RI/MOM renormalization scheme has been employed to subtract the asymptotic contributions for the quasi-DAs so that the plus function becomes well-defined. However it has been shown that implementing this scheme in the analysis of PDFs introduces unavoidably additional nonperturbative effectscitebib.bib21 and thus the final results contain uncontrollable systematic uncertainties.
content_sections.3 Hybrid renormalization scheme for bary: In this section, we will present the hybrid scheme to address the problems in the matching as stated in the previous section. The aim of this scheme is to remove the UV divergences in quasi-DAs without introducing nonperturbative effects, and meanwhile, this scheme can be properly implemented on the lattice. Conceptually, the UV divergences correspond to the small-distance behavior, while nonperturbative contributions are due to the long-distance behavior. So from the viewpoint of separating these contributions, it is advantageous to work in the coordinate space. In coordinate space, the results of quasi-DAs and the hard kernel can be obtained by making a Fourier transformation of the results in momentum space. We have also directly calculated the perturbative contributions to the quasi-DAs in Appendix, and checked the consistency. At short distances, e. g. z1‚Üí0‚ÜísubscriptūĚĎß10z1rightarrow 0, z2‚Üí0‚ÜísubscriptūĚĎß20z2rightarrow 0 or |z1‚ąíz2|‚Üí0‚ÜísubscriptūĚĎß1subscriptūĚĎß20|z1-z2|rightarrow 0 in Eq. with z3=0subscriptūĚĎß30z3=0, perturbative quasi-DAs behave as the logarithmic terms like ln‚Ā°(z12)superscriptsubscriptūĚĎß12ln(z12), ln‚Ā°(z22)superscriptsubscriptūĚĎß22ln(z22) and ln‚Ā°((z1‚ąíz2)2)superscriptsubscriptūĚĎß1subscriptūĚĎß22ln((z1-z2)2). As stated in the above section, the ln‚Ā°zi2superscriptsubscriptūĚĎßūĚĎĖ2ln zi2 terms in the real diagram of the perturbative matrix elements prevent them from performing z‚Üí0‚ÜíūĚĎß0zto 0. This is also reflected in our virtual contribution definition in which corresponding logarithm UV divergence had been kept. Subsequently, the double plus function is also not well-defined. By dividing the perturbative matrix element by a proper zero momentum matrix element, which is called the ratio schemecitebib.bib40, bib.bib41, bib.bib42, all these issues arising in the perturbative matrix elements calculation can be resolved. By construction, in the ratio scheme, the ln‚Ā°zi2superscriptsubscriptūĚĎßūĚĎĖ2ln zi2 terms in the numerator and the denominator are equal, then will be canceled. The corresponding virtual contributions will no longer be divergent. Then the double plus function can be guaranteed to be well-defined. Since now the perturbative matrix element is fine for z‚Üí0‚ÜíūĚĎß0zto 0, the corresponding lattice matrix elements can also be constructed and used for matching. It should be noted that the ratio scheme is only available in the perturbative region. If one performs it over the long-distance region, the IR structure may be changed. These require that only zero momentum matrix elements in the short-distance region can be chosen as denominators and the renormalization for the matrix elements in the long-distance region needs additional consideration. At large distances, e. g. z1‚ąľ1/őõQCDsimilar-tosubscriptūĚĎß11subscriptőõQCDz1sim 1/Lambdarm QCD, z2‚ąľ1/őõQCDsimilar-tosubscriptūĚĎß21subscriptőõQCDz2sim 1/Lambdarm QCD or |z1‚ąíz2|‚ąľ1/őõQCDsimilar-tosubscriptūĚĎß1subscriptūĚĎß21subscriptőõQCD|z1-z2|sim 1/Lambdarm QCD in Eq. , a proper renormalization scheme should not introduce extra non-perturbative effects. In this work, we use the self-renormalization scheme as advocated in Ref. citebib.bib21. In the self-renormalization scheme, a renormalization factor ZRsubscriptūĚĎćūĚĎÖZR, which includes all typical divergences and discretion errors, is defined. One can use this renormalization factor to convert the lattice matrix elements to continuous matrix elements without bringing in any non-perturbative effects. Besides the UV divergence inherited by dimensional regularization, the linear divergence from the lattice simulation is eliminated as well. By definition, the calculation of the renormalization factors requires the input of the UV divergences and some parameters. Specifically, the UV divergences are fitted from zero momentum lattice matrix elements at small lattice spacings, and these parameters can be obtained through matching the renormalized lattice matrix element to the continuum perturbative matrix element in the perturbative region. As a result, the long-distance regions which merely involve non-perturbative scales in the renormalization can also be handled. A subtlety in applying the hybrid renormalization scheme to quasi-DAs of a light baryon is that there are multi regions involving both perturbative and non-perturbative scales, e. g. (z1‚Üí0‚ÜísubscriptūĚĎß10z1rightarrow 0 and z2‚ąľ1/őõQCDsimilar-tosubscriptūĚĎß21subscriptőõQCDz2sim 1/Lambdarm QCD), (z2‚Üí0‚ÜísubscriptūĚĎß20z2rightarrow 0 and z1‚ąľ1/őõQCDsimilar-tosubscriptūĚĎß11subscriptőõQCDz1sim 1/Lambdarm QCD), or (|z1‚ąíz2|‚Üí0‚ÜísubscriptūĚĎß1subscriptūĚĎß20|z1-z2|rightarrow 0, z1‚ąľ1/őõQCDsimilar-tosubscriptūĚĎß11subscriptőõQCDz1sim 1/Lambdarm QCD and z2‚ąľ1/őõQCDsimilar-tosubscriptūĚĎß21subscriptőõQCDz2sim 1/Lambdarm QCD), see the blue bands in Fig. except for the one around z1‚ąľ‚ąíz2similar-tosubscriptūĚĎß1subscriptūĚĎß2z1sim-z2. However, if the logarithmic divergences regarding different scales (z1subscriptūĚĎß1z1, z2subscriptūĚĎß2z2 and |z1‚ąíz2|subscriptūĚĎß1subscriptūĚĎß2|z1-z2|) can be factorized separately, the UV divergences regarding those scales can be multiplicatively renormalized separately. If so, the ratio scheme can be taken for the perturbative scale and the self renormalization can be performed for the non-perturbative scale. From the one-loop result in Eq. , one can see that the logarithmic divergences indeed factorize, which allows to perform the hybrid renormalization. It is non-trivial to prove that they can be factorized out, which requires further detailed investigations. We only focus on the renormalization and matching up to the one loop accuracy in this work. In this section, we will give the normalization and self renormalization in the beginning, which provide the building blocks for hybrid renormalization. Then we provide a method for hybrid renormalization. The matching kernel in the hybrid scheme is presented at the end.

A hybrid renormalization method is presented in this subsection, based on the building blocks provided in the previous subsections, such as the normalized lattice matrix element Eq. , the renormalization factor Eq. and the renormalized lattice matrix element in MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinerm MS scheme Eq. . In practical calculations, it is difficult to directly perform the matching in MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinerm MS scheme since there are inconsistencies between the lattice matrix element and continuum scheme at short distances. The lattice matrix elements are finite as z1‚Üí0‚ÜísubscriptūĚĎß10z1rightarrow 0 or z2‚Üí0‚ÜísubscriptūĚĎß20z2rightarrow 0 while the perturbative matrix elements, with the logarithmic terms ln‚Ā°(z12)superscriptsubscriptūĚĎß12ln(z12), ln‚Ā°(z22)superscriptsubscriptūĚĎß22ln(z22) and ln‚Ā°((z1‚ąíz2)2)superscriptsubscriptūĚĎß1subscriptūĚĎß22ln((z1-z2)2), are divergent as z1‚Üí0‚ÜísubscriptūĚĎß10z1rightarrow 0 or z2‚Üí0‚ÜísubscriptūĚĎß20z2rightarrow 0. Moreover, those logarithmic terms correspond to slowly decaying terms ‚ąľ1|x1‚Ä≤|similar-toabsent1subscriptsuperscriptūĚĎ•‚Ä≤1simdisplaystylefrac1|xprime1| and 1|x2‚Ä≤|1subscriptsuperscriptūĚĎ•‚Ä≤2displaystylefrac1|xprime2| in the matching kernel, which increase the difficulties of numerical calculations in preserving the normalization, though not impossible. The hybrid scheme can be viewed as a modification of MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinerm MS scheme at short distance. Through the ratio at short distance, part of the discretization effects are canceled in the lattice matrix elements and the singular log terms are canceled in the perturbative matrix elements. So the lattice matrix elements become more consistent with the continuum scheme under the hybrid scheme, where it is easier to preserve the normalization. Thus there are several principles in designing the hybrid schemecitebib.bib21: 1) Eliminate all the singular logarithmic terms in the perturbative matrix elements including ln‚Ā°(z12)superscriptsubscriptūĚĎß12ln(z12), ln‚Ā°(z22)superscriptsubscriptūĚĎß22ln(z22) and ln‚Ā°((z1‚ąíz2)2)superscriptsubscriptūĚĎß1subscriptūĚĎß22ln((z1-z2)2) through the ratio; 2) Avoid introducing extra effects that are not perturbatively controllable at large distances; 3) Keep the renormalized matrix element continuous and the method as simple as possible. Before closing this subsection, we point out that in the hybrid renormalization the two schemes are bridged at z=zsūĚĎßsubscriptūĚĎßūĚĎ†z=zs: for zizssubscriptūĚĎßūĚĎĖsubscriptūĚĎßūĚĎ†zi>zs, the self renormalization is adopted. On the one hand the zssubscriptūĚĎßūĚĎ†zs should be in principle larger than the lattice spacing aūĚĎéa so that discretization effects can be suppressed through the ratio at short distance. On the other hand, zssubscriptūĚĎßūĚĎ†zs should be smaller than 1/őõQCD1subscriptőõQCD1/Lambdarm QCD so that no extra non-perturbative effects are introduced at large distance. To conclude, zssubscriptūĚĎßūĚĎ†zs is the point where the continuum perturbation theory matches lattice data. Explicitly the zssubscriptūĚĎßūĚĎ†zs might be chosen around 0.1‚ąí0.20.10.20.1-0.2 fm. For example, it is shown in Figs.11, 12-16 (d) of Ref. citebib.bib21 and Fig.1 of Ref. citebib.bib25 that the continuum perturbation theory can describe lattice matrix element around 0.1‚ąí0.20.10.20.1-0.2 fm. This value is much smaller than the proton charge radius around 0.80.80.8 fm where the non-perturbative effects are important. Also this corresponds to the energy scale 1‚ąí2121-2 GeV, which is typically larger than the non-perturbative scale őõQCD‚ąľ300similar-tosubscriptőõQCD300Lambdarm QCDsim 300 MeV.

A necessary condition for the pure double plus function is that there are no linearly decaying terms ‚ąľ1|x1‚Ä≤|similar-toabsent1subscriptsuperscriptūĚĎ•‚Ä≤1simdisplaystylefrac1|xprime1| and 1|x2‚Ä≤|1subscriptsuperscriptūĚĎ•‚Ä≤2displaystylefrac1|xprime2| for |x1‚Ä≤|‚Čę1much-greater-thansubscriptsuperscriptūĚĎ•‚Ä≤11|xprime1|gg 1 and |x2‚Ä≤|‚Čę1much-greater-thansubscriptsuperscriptūĚĎ•‚Ä≤21|xprime2|gg 1 respectively. One can check those linearly decaying terms in ūĚíěMS¬Į‚Äč(x1,x2,y1,y2,Pz,őľ)subscriptsuperscriptūĚíě1¬ĮMSsubscriptūĚĎ•1subscriptūĚĎ•2subscriptūĚĎ¶1subscriptūĚĎ¶2superscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßūĚúáCoverlinerm MS(x1,x2,y1,y2,Pz,mu) are explicitly cancelled by the linearly decaying terms in őī‚ÄčūĚíěH‚Äč(x1,x2,y1,y2,Pz,őľ)ūĚõŅsubscriptsuperscriptūĚíě1ūĚźĽsubscriptūĚĎ•1subscriptūĚĎ•2subscriptūĚĎ¶1subscriptūĚĎ¶2superscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßūĚúádeltaCH(x1,x2,y1,y2,Pz,mu), which is equivalent to the cancellation of the log terms ln‚Ā°(z12)superscriptsubscriptūĚĎß12ln(z12), ln‚Ā°(z22)superscriptsubscriptūĚĎß22ln(z22) and ln‚Ā°((z1‚ąíz2)2)superscriptsubscriptūĚĎß1subscriptūĚĎß22ln((z1-z2)2) through the ratio at short distance in coordinate space. For readers‚Äô convenience, a Mathematica notebook on the hybrid scheme matching kernel is attached to the source package on arXiv.

Before closing this section we give a few remarks in order. ‚ÄĘ In this work, we have focused on one of the Lorentz structures of DA of a light őõőõLambda baryon at the leading twist, but the renormalization strategy is also applicable to the other Lorentz structures at the leading twist at one loop accuracy. ‚ÄĘ This renormalization strategy is likely to be generalized to twist-4 LCDAs. Since a baryon is interpolated by three quark fields, there are nine LCDAs at twist-4citebib.bib52. For an explicit distribution amplitude, one needs to pick up the appropriate gamma matrices and insert them into the interpolating operator to project out the desired component. Furthermore, several interesting workscitebib.bib53, bib.bib54 have recently calculated the perturbative contributions to PDFs at twist-3 in LaMET, and pointed out that after properly taking into account the zero-mode contributions (or the so-called endpoint contributions), the twist-3 equal-time matrix elements can be used to extract the corresponding lightcone PDFs. Accordingly, future applications to twist-4 LCDAs of a light baryon should also investigate the zero-mode contributions. ‚ÄĘ The ratio scheme is used to remove the UV divergences in small separations which is not always optimal. In particular, if there is mixing between matrix elements, an RI-MOM scheme (e. g. , citebib.bib55, bib.bib56) is likely beneficial. When there is mixing between matrix elements, the RI/MOM scheme can use the quark matrix elements and project out the contributions. This has been used in the analysis of parton distribution functionscitebib.bib46, bib.bib59, bib.bib57, bib.bib58, bib.bib60, bib.bib61, bib.bib39, bib.bib62, bib.bib63, bib.bib64. However, the implementation of RI/MOM scheme in the baryon DA case is not straightforward. The interpolating current of a baryon is made of three quarks, which does not have any corresponding quark transition matrix elements that can be constructed on the lattice. In addition, the baryon DA is a two-dimensional distribution, and the use of discrete symmetries to constrain the mixing pattern is also more complicated than the one-dimensional PDF case.
content_sections.4 Summary: To summarize, this paper continues the work on a direct method to extract the shape distribution of LCDA of a light baryon through the simulation of equal-time correlation functions under the framework of large momentum effective theory. To clear the obstacles on the road to renormalize the quasi-DAs, we have developed a hybrid renormalization scheme. By combining the self-renormalization at large spatial separations and the ratio scheme at short spatial separations, the hybrid renormalization scheme removes the UV divergences without introducing extra nonperturbative effects. The corresponding equal-time correlation functions have been calculated in the coordinate space to the next-to-leading order, and the matching kernel between the quasi-DAs and LCDAs has been derived. In the calculation, the őõőõLambda baryon has been taken as an example to demonstrate the scheme, but such a scheme can be straightforwardly generalized to other octet and decuplet baryons. This approach offers a practical methodology for computing LCDA which can be carried out in lattice calculations, and a preliminary analysis using this approach is now being conducted by members of Larton Parton collaboration. Acknowledgements. We would like to thank Jun Zeng, Zhifu Deng, Minhuan Chu, Jun Hua, and Qi-An Zhang for their insightful comments and invaluable discussions. This work is supported in part by the Natural Science Foundation of China under Grants No.12125503, No. U2032102, No. 12061131006, and No. 12335003. Part of the computations in this paper was run on the Siyuan-1 cluster supported by the Center for High Performance Computing at Shanghai Jiao Tong University.

all_papers.title: Transverse-Momentum-Dependent Wave Functions of Pion from Lattice QCD
all_papers.abstract:
We present a first lattice QCD calculation of the
transverse-momentum-dependent wave functions (TMDWFs) of the pion using
large-momentum effective theory. Numerical simulations are based on one
ensemble with 2+1+1 flavors of highly improved staggered quarks action with
lattice spacing $a=0.121$~fm from the MILC Collaboration, and one with 2 +1
flavor clover fermions and tree-level Symanzik gauge action generated by the
CLS Collaboration with $a=0.098$~fm. As a key ingredient, the soft function is
first obtained by incorporating the one-loop perturbative contributions and a
proper normalization. Based on this and the equal-time quasi-TMDWFs simulated
on the lattice, we extract the light-cone TMDWFs. The results are comparable
between the two lattice ensembles and a comparison with phenomenological
parametrization is made. Our studies provide a first attempt of $ab$ $initio$
calculation of TMDWFs which will eventually lead to crucial theory inputs for
making predictions for exclusive processes under QCD factorization.

qualitative: Large Momentum Effective Theory (LaMET) is used to compute transverse-momentum-dependent wave functions (TMDWFs) of the pion from first principles in lattice QCD.

Quasi-TMDWFs are extracted from equal-time correlation functions on the lattice, which are then matched to light-cone TMDWFs via a matching procedure involving the soft function and renormalization factors.
The soft function is determined by incorporating one-loop perturbative contributions and proper normalization, serving as a key ingredient in the matching between quasi- and light-cone TMDWFs.

Renormalization of quasi-TMDWFs requires removal of linear divergences and pinch pole singularities using Wilson loop factors ZE(LE, b‚üā), with extrapolation techniques applied to handle statistical uncertainties at large LE.

A vertex renormalization factor ZO is introduced to cancel logarithmic divergences from Wilson line endpoints, determined via a short-distance matching window where discretization and higher-twist effects are negligible.

Analytical extrapolation in coordinate space is performed to remove unphysical oscillations in quasi-TMDWFs at large quasi-light-front distances őĽ = zPz, using a parametrization involving trigonometric functions and exponential damping.
The extrapolation form is validated by consistency with original lattice data and systematic uncertainty estimated by varying the extrapolation boundary őĽL = zL Pz.
The Collins-Soper kernel is computed on the CLS ensemble, with dominant systematic uncertainty at small b‚üā arising from the imaginary part of the matching kernel.
quantitative: Lattice spacing: a = 0.121 fm (MILC ensemble, 2+1+1 flavors, HISQ action), a = 0.098 fm (CLS ensemble, 2+1 flavors, clover fermions, tree-level Symanzik gauge action).
Extrapolation of Wilson loop: ZE(LE, b‚üā) = c0(b‚üā) e^{-E(b‚üā) LE} [1 + c1(b‚üā) e^{-őĒE(b‚üā) LE}]
Renormalization factor ZO: ZO = 0.917 (MILC), ZO = 0.903 (CLS), obtained as average of ZO(z0=0a, b‚üā0=2a) and ZO(z0=0a, b‚üā0=3a).
Extrapolation boundary: őĽL = zL Pz ‚Čą 10, used to distinguish original data (őĽ > őĽL) from extrapolated region.
Systematic uncertainty from extrapolation estimated by varying őĽL = (zL ¬Ī 1) Pz and taking average of differences.

Parametrization of quasi-TMDWFs: Re[ő®¬Ī(z, b‚üā, őľ, ő∂z)] = [k1(b‚üā) cos(ŌÄd/2) - k2(b‚üā) f(b‚üā) sin(ŌÄd/2)] e^{-őĽ/őĽ0} őĽ^d, Im[ő®¬Ī(z, b‚üā, őľ, ő∂z)] = [k1(b‚üā) f(b‚üā) cos(ŌÄd/2) + k2(b‚üā) sin(ŌÄd/2)] e^{-őĽ/őĽ0} őĽ^d.
Transverse momentum window: b‚üā0 = 2a and 3a used for ZO determination; b‚üā0 = 0a avoided due to discretization effects.

content_sections.Supplemental Materials .1 Fits for two p: In lattice simulations, quasi-TMDWFs can be extracted from the two-point correlation functions C2¬ĪsuperscriptsubscriptūĚź∂2plus-or-minusC2pm as shown in Eq. (LABEL:eq:c2fit) of the main text. C2¬ĪsuperscriptsubscriptūĚź∂2plus-or-minusC2pm consists of ground-state contributions and excited-state contaminations, thus one can adopt a two-state fit to separate the ground-state contribution to quasi-TMDWF. In addition, when tūĚĎ°t becomes large, the excited-state contamination decreases exponentially, so a one-state analysis also allows to extract the ground-state contribution. Fig. shows a comparison of one- and two-state fits for the renormalized C2R+superscriptsubscriptūĚź∂2limit-fromūĚĎÖC2R+ at L,z,b‚üā=6,3,4‚ÄčaūĚźŅūĚĎßsubscriptūĚĎŹperpendicular-to634ūĚĎéL,z,bperp=6,3,4a on MILC ensemble, in which they give consistent results. However, the stability of a two-state fit relies strongly on the size of the excited-state contribution, which requires very high precision lattice data. Therefore we employ a one-state fit in the range t‚Č•3‚ÄčaūĚĎ°3ūĚĎétgeq 3a to provide reasonable and stable uncertainties in the following analysis.
content_sections.Supplemental Materials .2 Renormalizatio: The linear divergence and pinch pole singularity in quasi-TMDWFs can be removed by the Wilson loop ZE‚Äč(LE,b‚üā)subscriptūĚĎćūĚźłsubscriptūĚźŅūĚźłsubscriptūĚĎŹperpendicular-toZEleft(LE,bperpright), in which LE‚Č°2‚ÄčL+|z|subscriptūĚźŅūĚźł2ūĚźŅūĚĎßLEequiv 2L+|z|. In lattice simulations, the statistical uncertainty of ZEsubscriptūĚĎćūĚźłZE for large LEsubscriptūĚźŅūĚźłLE and b‚üāsubscriptūĚĎŹperpendicular-tobperp is out of control as shown in Fig. . Fortunately, the self-energy corrections and gluon exchanging effect introduce linear divergence as exponential form proportional to LEsubscriptūĚźŅūĚźłLEcitebib.bib33, bib.bib34, bib.bib14. Therefore one can adopt an extrapolation for the Wilson loop by a two-state fit via the following equation: ZE‚Äč(LE,b‚üā)=c0‚Äč(b‚üā)‚Äče‚ąíE‚Äč(b‚üā)‚ÄčLE‚Äč[1+c1‚Äč(b‚üā)‚Äče‚ąíőĒ‚ÄčE‚Äč(b‚üā)‚ÄčLE]. subscriptūĚĎćūĚźłsubscriptūĚźŅūĚźłsubscriptūĚĎŹperpendicular-tosubscriptūĚĎź0subscriptūĚĎŹperpendicular-tosuperscriptūĚĎíūĚźłsubscriptūĚĎŹperpendicular-tosubscriptūĚźŅūĚźłdelimited-1subscriptūĚĎź1subscriptūĚĎŹperpendicular-tosuperscriptūĚĎíőĒūĚźłsubscriptūĚĎŹperpendicular-tosubscriptūĚźŅūĚźłdisplaystyle ZE(LE,bperp)=c0(bperp)e-E(bperp)LEleft[1+c1(bperp)e-Delta E(bperp)LEright]. As shown in Fig. , taking the result on MILC ensemble as an example, one can see that the extrapolated data is in line with the original data. Moreover, to cancel the logarithmic divergence arising from the endpoints of the Wilson lines, we need an additional quark Wilson line vertex renormalization factor ZOsubscriptūĚĎćūĚĎāZO. According to Ref. citebib.bib21, this factor ZOsubscriptūĚĎćūĚĎāZO can be computed from the quotient of renormalized quasi-TMDWF in the rest frame calculated on the lattice and perturbatively: ZO‚Äč(1/a,őľ)=ő®¬Ī,0(z0,b‚üā0,ő∂z=0,L)ZE‚Äč(2‚ÄčL+|z0|,b‚üā0,őľ)‚ÄčŌąMS¬Į‚Äč(z0,b‚üā0,őľ). displaystyle ZO(1/a,mu)=fractildePsipm,0left(z0,bperp 0,zetaz=0,Lright)sqrtZEleft(2L+|z0|,bperp 0,muright)tildepsioverlineMSleft(z0,bperp 0,muright). ő®¬Ī,0superscriptő®plus-or-minus0tildePsipm,0 denotes the bare quasi-TMDWF in coordinate space. The perturbative quasi-TMDWF ŌąMS¬ĮsuperscriptūĚúď¬ĮMStildepsioverlineMS in the MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlineMS scheme in the denominator has been recently calculated incitebib.bib21. In our analysis, we adopt a short distance region for z0subscriptūĚĎß0z0 and b‚üā0subscriptūĚĎŹperpendicular-toabsent0bperp 0 matching with perturbative calculation. We look for a window of b‚üā0subscriptūĚĎŹperpendicular-toabsent0bperp 0 where both discretization effects and higher twist contaminations are negligible. In such a window, ZOsubscriptūĚĎćūĚĎāZO should have only a mild dependence on b‚üā0subscriptūĚĎŹperpendicular-toabsent0bperp 0. These dependences are investigated in Fig. . As shown in the figure, ZO‚Äč(z0=0‚Äča,b‚üā0=2‚Äča)subscriptūĚĎćūĚĎāformulae-sequencesubscriptūĚĎß00ūĚĎésubscriptūĚĎŹperpendicular-toabsent02ūĚĎéZO(z0=0a,bperp 0=2a) and ZO‚Äč(z0=0‚Äča,b‚üā0=3‚Äča)subscriptūĚĎćūĚĎāformulae-sequencesubscriptūĚĎß00ūĚĎésubscriptūĚĎŹperpendicular-toabsent03ūĚĎéZO(z0=0a,bperp 0=3a) reach a b‚üā0subscriptūĚĎŹperpendicular-toabsent0bperp 0 window for both MILC and CLS ensembles. all_papers.title:
Lattice QCD Calculation of $x$-dependent Meson Distribution Amplitudes
  at Physical Pion Mass with Threshold Logarithm Resummation
all_papers.abstract:
We present a lattice quantum chromodynamics (QCD) calculation of the
$x$-dependent pion and kaon distribution amplitudes (DA) in the framework of
large momentum effective theory. This calculation is performed on a fine
lattice of $a=0.076$ fm at physical pion mass, with the pion boosted to $1.8$
GeV and kaon boosted to $2.3$ GeV. We renormalize the matrix elements in the
hybrid scheme and match to $\overline{\rm MS }$ with a subtraction of the
leading renormalon in the Wilson-line mass. The perturbative matching is
improved by resumming the large logarithms related to the small quark and gluon
momenta in the soft-gluon limit. After resummation, we demonstrate that we are
able to calculate a range of $x\in[x_0,1-x_0]$ with $x_0=0.25$ for pion and
$x_0=0.2$ for kaon with theoretical systematic errors under control. The kaon
DA is shown to be slighted skewed, and narrower than pion DA. Although the
$x$-dependence cannot be direct calculated beyond these ranges, we estimate
higher moments of the pion and kaon DAs by complementing our calculation with
short-distance factorization.

sections.1: Ji, Parton Physics on a Euclidean Lattice, Phys
sections.3: Hua et¬†al., Pion and Kaon Distribution Amplitudes from Lattice QCD, Phys
sections.19: Bazavov et¬†al., Meson screening masses in (2+1)-flavor QCD, Phys
sections.40: Pineda, Determination of the bottom quark mass from the Upsilon(1S) system, JHEP 06 (2001) 022, [hep-ph/0105008].
sections.43: Huo et¬†al., Self-renormalization of quasi-light-front correlators on the lattice, Nucl
sections.45: Regge, Introduction to complex orbital momenta, Nuovo Cim
sections.51: Altmannshofer et¬†al., The Belle II Physics Book, PTEP 2019 (2019), no

qualitative: Large Momentum Effective Theory (LaMET) provides a framework to calculate x-dependent meson distribution amplitudes (DAs) by relating quasi-distributions‚ÄĒdefined from finite-momentum hadrons on a Euclidean lattice‚ÄĒto light-cone DAs through momentum-space factorization.

LaMET enables direct access to the x-dependence of meson DAs in a moderate range of x, avoiding the need for model-dependent fits, by computing quasi-DA matrix elements at large hadron momentum and matching them to the light-cone DA via perturbative matching.

The power corrections in LaMET are suppressed by powers of xPz or (1‚ąíx)Pz, where Pz is the hadron momentum along the quantization axis, making the expansion valid only in the bulk region away from the endpoints x‚Üí0 or x‚Üí1.

Renormalization of quasi-DAs is performed in the hybrid scheme, which combines advantages of different renormalization schemes to reduce lattice artifacts and improve control over systematic uncertainties.
Leading renormalon resummation (LRR) is applied to remove the linear renormalon ambiguity of order őõQCD, improving the power accuracy of the matching to sub-leading order.

Threshold logarithm resummation is implemented to account for large logarithms arising from small quark and gluon momenta in the soft-gluon limit, enhancing the perturbative accuracy of the matching kernel.
The combination of LRR and threshold resummation at NNLL accuracy allows for reliable extraction of the light-cone DA in a controlled range of x, with theoretical systematics under control.

For regions beyond the LaMET range (near x=0 and x=1), short-distance factorization (SDF) is used in conjunction with the mid-x results to estimate higher moments of the DA, providing a global picture of the distribution.
quantitative: Lattice spacing: a = 0.076 fm
Physical pion mass used in the calculation
Pion boosted to momentum Pz = 1.8 GeV
Kaon boosted to momentum Pz = 2.3 GeV
Range of x for pion DA: x ‚ąą [0.25, 0.75]
Range of x for kaon DA: x ‚ąą [0.2, 0.8]
Perturbative matching performed at next-to-next-to-leading logarithmic (NNLL) accuracy with threshold resummation
Matching kernel includes next-to-leading order (NLO) corrections
Renormalization performed in the hybrid scheme with subtraction of the leading renormalon in the Wilson-line mass
Final matching to the MS¬Į scheme with LRR and threshold resummation

content_sections.1 Introduction: Pseudo-scalar meson distribution amplitudes (DAs) ŌēM‚Äč(x)subscriptitalic-ŌēūĚĎÄūĚĎ•phiM(x) of a meson MūĚĎÄM with valence quark q‚Äčq¬Į‚Ä≤ūĚĎěsuperscript¬ĮūĚĎě‚Ä≤qbarqprime describe the probability amplitude of finding the meson moving on the light-cone in its minimal Fock state, a quark-antiquark pair |q‚Äčq¬Į‚Ä≤‚ü©ketūĚĎěsuperscript¬ĮūĚĎě‚Ä≤|qbarqprime each carrying a momentum fraction of xūĚĎ•x and 1‚ąíx1ūĚĎ•1-x, respectively. They are important universal inputs to hard exclusive processes and form factorscitebib.bib1, bib.bib2, bib.bib3, bib.bib4 at large momentum transfer Q2‚ČęőõQCD2much-greater-thansuperscriptūĚĎĄ2superscriptsubscriptőõQCD2Q2ggLambdarm QCD2. Especially, the kaon DA gains particular interest because of its relevance to CP violating processes in heavy meson decays, such as B‚ÜíŌÄ‚ÄčK‚ÜíūĚźĶūĚúčūĚźĺBtopi K and D‚ÜíK‚ÄčK¬Į‚ÜíūĚź∑ūĚźĺ¬ĮūĚźĺDto KbarK citebib.bib5, bib.bib6, bib.bib7, which are important probes to new physics beyond Standard Model. So far, the xūĚĎ•x-dependence of meson DAs are only weakly constrained by experimental measurements of form factorscitebib.bib4, bib.bib8, bib.bib9, bib.bib10, thus many model-dependent theoretical calculations are suggested, providing very different shapes with different model assumptionscitebib.bib11, bib.bib12, bib.bib13. A direct xūĚĎ•x-dependence calculation from first principle methods, such as lattice QCD, could improve our understanding of the meson structures. The meson DAs can be defined as light-like correlations, i‚ÄčfM‚ÄčŌēM‚Äč(x,őľ)=‚ąęd‚Äčő∑‚ąí2‚ÄčŌÄ‚Äčei‚Äčx‚ÄčP+‚Äčő∑‚ąí‚Äč‚ü®0|q¬Į‚Ä≤‚Äč‚Äčő≥5‚Äčő≥+‚ÄčW‚Äč(0,ő∑‚ąí)‚Äčq‚Äč(ő∑‚ąí)|M‚Äč(P)‚ü©,ūĚĎĖsubscriptūĚĎďūĚĎÄsubscriptitalic-ŌēūĚĎÄūĚĎ•ūĚúáūĚĎĎsuperscriptūĚúā2ūĚúčsuperscriptūĚĎíūĚĎĖūĚĎ•superscriptūĚĎÉsuperscriptūĚúāquantum-operator-product0superscript¬ĮūĚĎě‚Ä≤0subscriptūĚõĺ5subscriptūĚõĺūĚĎä0superscriptūĚúāūĚĎěsuperscriptūĚúāūĚĎÄūĚĎÉdisplaystyleifMphiM(x,mu)=intfracdeta-2pieixP+eta- 0|overlineqprimegamma5gamma+W(0,eta-)q(eta-)|M(P), where fMsubscriptūĚĎďūĚĎÄfM is the meson‚Äôs decay constant, W‚Äč(0,ő∑‚ąí)=ūĚíę‚Äčexp‚Ā°[‚ąíi‚Äčg‚Äč‚ąę0ő∑‚ąíūĚĎĎs‚Äčnőľ‚ÄčAőľ‚Äč(n‚Äčs)]ūĚĎä0superscriptūĚúāūĚíęūĚĎĖūĚĎĒsubscriptsuperscriptsuperscriptūĚúā0differential-dūĚĎ†subscriptūĚĎõūĚúásuperscriptūĚźīūĚúáūĚĎõūĚĎ†W(0,eta-)=hatPexpleft[-iginteta-0!!ds,nmuAmu(ns)right] is the Wilson line between the two light-cone coordinates 00 and ő∑‚ąí=(ő∑0+ő∑3)/2superscriptūĚúāsuperscriptūĚúā0superscriptūĚúā32eta-=(eta0+eta3)/sqrt2 with ūĚíęūĚíęhatP denoting the path-ordering operator. The real-time dependence in the light-cone DA definition makes it difficult to simulate directly on a Euclidean lattice. Thus different approaches have been proposed to extract partial information of meson DAs with lattice calculations, including the calculation of their lowest moments ‚ü®(2‚Äčx‚ąí1)n‚ü©delimited-‚ü®‚ü©superscript2ūĚĎ•1ūĚĎõ(2x-1)n from local twist-2 operatorscitebib.bib14, bib.bib15, bib.bib16, bib.bib17, bib.bib18, bib.bib19 through the operator product expansion (OPE) of the pion correlators, the calculation of non-local correlations that can be related to the meson DAs through OPE or short distance factorization (SDF)citebib.bib20, bib.bib21, bib.bib22, bib.bib23, bib.bib24, bib.bib25, bib.bib26, and the large momentum effective theory (LaMET)citebib.bib27, bib.bib28, bib.bib29, bib.bib30, bib.bib31, bib.bib32, bib.bib33, bib.bib34, bib.bib35, bib.bib36 that directly calculates the xūĚĎ•x-dependence from a momentum-space factorization. The local twist-2 operator calculation and the SDF approach in principle allows us to obtain a few lowest-order meson DA moments, but the xūĚĎ•x-dependence could only be obtained by fitting with some model assumptions of the shape. On the other hand, LaMET relates a class of observables at finite hadron momentum, named quasi-distributions, to the light-cone distribution through a factorization in momentum space, with power corrections suppressed by the power of parton momentum x‚ÄčPzūĚĎ•subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßxPz or (1‚ąíx)‚ÄčPz1ūĚĎ•subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß(1-x)Pz, thus provides an approach to calculate the xūĚĎ•x-dependence in a moderate range of xūĚĎ•x with well-controlled systematics. So far, various lattice artifacts and theoretical systematics have been studied in the LaMET calculation of the meson DAs since the first attemptscitebib.bib30, bib.bib31. The theoretical efforts include the lattice renormalization, improvement of the power accuracy, and the inclusion of higher-order effects in perturbation theory. The renormalization of the linear divergence in the quasi-DA correlatorscitebib.bib37, bib.bib38, bib.bib39, bib.bib40 using regularization-independent momentum subtraction (RI/MOM) citebib.bib41, bib.bib42, bib.bib43, bib.bib44 scheme or the ratio citebib.bib45, bib.bib46 scheme in the early stage, which was later improved with the hybrid renormalization schemecitebib.bib47, bib.bib48, bib.bib49. The power corrections resulting from the linear renormalon ambiguity of order őõQCDsubscriptőõQCDLambdatextQCD in renormalization and perturbative matching is resolved with the leading-renormalon resummation (LRR) methodcitebib.bib35, bib.bib50, thus the power accuracy is improved to sub-leading order. The high-order effects are examined by including the calculation of next-to-leading order (NLO) matching kernel for quasi-DAcitebib.bib30, bib.bib31, bib.bib51, bib.bib52, bib.bib53, and the renormalization group resummation (RGR)citebib.bib54, bib.bib55 and threshold resummationcitebib.bib54, bib.bib56, bib.bib57, bib.bib36 that resum the logarithm to further include higher-order logarithm contributions. Since the power expansion parameter is related to the physical scale x‚ÄčPzūĚĎ•subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßxPz or (1‚ąíx)‚ÄčPz1ūĚĎ•subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß(1-x)Pz, the LaMET expansion cannot be applied to endpoint regions x‚Üí0,1‚ÜíūĚĎ•01xto0,1. Beyond the range that LaMET calculates, it has also been proposed to constrain the endpoint regions with the global information of DA, such as the lower moments or short distance correlations, extracted from SDFcitebib.bib58, bib.bib35. Numerically, the first continuum extrapolation of the DA was carried out in Ref. citebib.bib32 with the RI/MOM scheme at heavier-than-physical pion masses, succeeded by a continuum extrapolation at physical pion mass with NLO hybrid-scheme renormalization and matching, but without LRRcitebib.bib34. The LRR method was first implemented in Ref. citebib.bib35 with NLO matching and RGR. Meanwhile, the meson DA moments have also been calculated using the SDF approach at NLO accuracycitebib.bib22, bib.bib25. Recently, there is new progress to develop the threshold resummation for the LaMET calculation of pion-DA on a domain-wall ensemble that preserves chiral symmetrycitebib.bib36. In this work, we present the xūĚĎ•x-dependence calculation of pion and kaon DAs on a lattice ensemble with physical pion mass and lattice spacing a=0.076ūĚĎé0.076a=0.076 fm, by boosting the pion momentum up to 1.81.81.8GeV and the kaon momentum up to 2.32.32.3GeV. We renormalize the bare meson quasi-DA matrix elements with the hybrid scheme, and then match them to the continuum MS¬Į¬ĮMSoverlinerm MS scheme with LRR. After Fourier transforming to the xūĚĎ•x-space, we match the quasi-DA to the light-cone with next-to-next-to-leading logarithmic (NNLL) threshold resummation and NLO matching, which provides a reliable calculation in the range x‚ąą[0.25,0.75]ūĚĎ•0.250.75xin[0.25,0.75] for pion and x‚ąą[0.2,0.8]ūĚĎ•0.20.8xin[0.2,0.8] for kaon. Finally, we utilize the short distance correlations along with the already determined mid-xūĚĎ•x distribution, to better constrain the distribution in the endpoint region, thus provide us a rough estimate for higher moments of DA. This work is organized as follows. In Sec. , we present our lattice setup of the calculation, and show the extraction of the bare matrix elements. In Sec. , we renormalize the bare matrix elements in the hybrid scheme with LRR and obtain xūĚĎ•x-dependent quasi-DAs. In Sec. , we apply the threshold-resummed perturbative inverse matching to extract the light-cone DA in a range of x‚ąą[x0,1‚ąíx0]ūĚĎ•subscriptūĚĎ•01subscriptūĚĎ•0xin[x0,1-x0], and provide a rough estimate for the higher moments of pion and kaon DAs by modeling the endpoint regions using complemenarity with SDF. Finally, we conclude in Sec. .
content_sections.2 Numerical setup: We use two 2+1 flavor Highly Improved Staggered Quark (HISQ)citebib.bib59 gauge ensembles generated by the HotQCD collaborationcitebib.bib60 with lattice size Ls√óLt=643√ó64subscriptūĚźŅūĚĎ†subscriptūĚźŅūĚĎ°superscript64364Lstimes Lt=643times 64 and lattice spacing of a = 0.076 fm. The quark mass in the sea are both at the physical point. We use the Wilson-Clover action for the valence sector, and the clover coefficients are set to be cs‚ÄčwsubscriptūĚĎźūĚĎ†ūĚĎ§csw = 1.0372citebib.bib61 using the averaged plaquette after 1-step HYP smearingcitebib.bib62. The Wilson-Clover quark mass are tuned so that the pion and kaon mass are 140 MeV and 498 MeV, respectively. The calculations used QUDA multigrid algorithmcitebib.bib63, bib.bib64, bib.bib65, bib.bib66 for the Wilson-Dirac operator inversions to get the quark propagators. All Mode Averaging (AMA) techniquecitebib.bib67 is applied to increase the statistics. The pion correlators on this ensemble have already been generated and analyzed in a previous workcitebib.bib25, so we only present the analysis of kaon raw data in this section. In order to derive the bare matrix elements of ground state, we need to compute the two-point functions to extract the energy spectrum and get the overlap amplitudes, CKK‚Äč(ūĚźŹ,ts)=‚ü®[KS‚Äč(ūĚźŹ,ts)]‚Äč[KS‚Äč(ūĚźŹ,0)]‚Ä†‚ü©. subscriptūĚź∂KKūĚźŹsubscriptūĚĎ°ūĚĎ†delimited-‚ü®‚ü©delimited-subscriptūĚźĺūĚĎÜūĚźŹsubscriptūĚĎ°ūĚĎ†superscriptdelimited-subscriptūĚźĺūĚĎÜūĚźŹ0‚Ä†displaystyle Crm KK(P,ts)=left[KS(P,ts)][KS(P,0)]daggerright. where KSsubscriptūĚźĺūĚĎÜKS are the kaon interpolators, namely, KS‚Äč(ūĚźŹ,t)=‚ąĎūĚźĪs¬ĮS‚Äč(ūĚźĪ,t)‚Äčő≥5‚ÄčuS‚Äč(ūĚźĪ,t)‚Äče‚ąíi‚ÄčūĚźŹ‚čÖūĚźĪ,subscriptūĚźĺūĚĎÜūĚźŹūĚĎ°subscriptūĚźĪsubscript¬ĮūĚĎ†ūĚĎÜūĚźĪūĚĎ°subscriptūĚõĺ5subscriptūĚĎĘūĚĎÜūĚźĪūĚĎ°superscriptūĚĎí‚čÖūĚĎĖūĚźŹūĚźĪdisplaystylebeginsplitKS(P,t)=sumxbarsS(x,t)gamma5uS(x,t)e-iPcdotx, endsplit in which boosted smearingcitebib.bib68, bib.bib69 (denoted by SūĚĎÜS) is applied to have better overlap with ground states and improve the signal of high-momenta states. The Gaussian radius of the light and strange quarks used in this work are rlGsuperscriptsubscriptūĚĎüūĚĎôūĚźļrlG = 0.59 fm and rsGsuperscriptsubscriptūĚĎüūĚĎ†ūĚźļrsG = 0.83 fm. Note that the scale variation becomes large when zūĚĎßz increases, because the scale z‚ąí1/2‚ąľőõQCDsimilar-tosuperscriptūĚĎß12subscriptőõQCDz-1/sqrt2simLambdarm QCD becomes non-perturbative. The second moment of kaon DA is slightly lower than the moment of pion DA, indicating the kaon DA to be narrower, similar to what has been observed in previous xūĚĎ•x-dependent calculationscitebib.bib31, bib.bib32, bib.bib34. In principle, the odd moments can also be extracted from the imaginary part of the matrix elements. In our data, we found that the imaginary part are non-vanishing for smaller momenta data, indicating a non-zero first moment of kaon DA. However, for large momenta, the imaginary parts decreases with momentum, and for the largest momentum Im[H‚Äč(z,nz=9)]ūĚźĽūĚĎßsubscriptūĚĎõūĚĎß9imaginary[H(z,nz=9)] are consistent with zero at short distance. Thus in principle we cannot describe all data with a universal first moment. Since we focus on the large momentum expansion approach in this work, we show the fit results for the ratio of imaginary part at the largest momentum nz=9subscriptūĚĎõūĚĎß9nz=9 to the real part of nz=1subscriptūĚĎõūĚĎß1nz=1 in the range of z‚ąą[a,zmax]ūĚĎßūĚĎésubscriptūĚĎßmaxzin[a,zrm max] at different zmaxsubscriptūĚĎßmaxzrm max. This results in a first Mellin moment of kaon ‚ü®őĺ‚ü©K‚Äč(őľ)‚Čą0.0013‚Äčsubscriptdelimited-‚ü®‚ü©ūĚúČūĚźĺūĚúá0.001360xiK(mu)approx 0.0013, corresponding to the first Gegenbauer coefficient a1=0.002‚ÄčsubscriptūĚĎé10.00210a1=0.002, which is consistent with zero, as shown in Fig. . The suppression of the kaon skewness at large momentum may suggest that such skewness originating from the mass difference between light and strange quark is no longer important in the infinite momentum limit, where both partons act just as massless particles. However, considering that our subtracted imaginary part is still potentially contaminated by the PzsubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßPz dependence in zőľ‚Äčk‚Äč(z2,z‚čÖP)superscriptūĚĎßūĚúáūĚĎėsuperscriptūĚĎß2‚čÖūĚĎßūĚĎÉzmuk(z2,zcdot P) term in Eq. , and since previous lattice calculations of local twist-2 opeartors suggest a non-zero kaon first momentcitebib.bib16, bib.bib77, bib.bib17, bib.bib19, the suppressed imaginary part at large momentum in our data may just be a lattice artifact, and needs further investigation.

Taking the moments we fitted from the RG-invariant ratios and tune the value of őī‚ÄčmūĚõŅūĚĎödelta m, we find the matrix elements eőī‚Äčm‚Äč|z|‚ÄčHB‚Äč(z,Pz,O)superscriptūĚĎíūĚõŅūĚĎöūĚĎßsuperscriptūĚźĽūĚźĶūĚĎßsubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßūĚĎāedelta m|z|HB(z,Pz,O) agree well with OPE reconstruction Eq. when őī‚Äčm‚Čą0.6ūĚõŅūĚĎö0.6delta mapprox 0.6GeV for kaon and őī‚Äčm‚Čą0.57ūĚõŅūĚĎö0.57delta mapprox 0.57GeV for pion, as shown in Fig. . This justifies our choice of using C0‚Äč(z,őľ)subscriptūĚź∂0ūĚĎßūĚúáC0(z,mu) calculated from perturbation theory in Eq. to renormalize the short distance matrix elements. Then we apply it to the hybrid scheme with zs=3‚ÄčasubscriptūĚĎßūĚĎ†3ūĚĎézs=3a in Fig. . We find the largest three momenta converge to a universal shape, and the imaginary part for kaon is non-vanishing but close to zero, indicating the existence of small skewness.

The LaMET factorization is naturally defined in momentum space, which requires the xūĚĎ•x-dependence of quasi-DA to obtain the xūĚĎ•x-dependent light-cone DA. The extraction of xūĚĎ•x-dependence requires the Fourier transform of the above renormalized correlation functions to the momentum space. Thus in principle we need to know the distribution to arbitrarily large z‚ÄčPzūĚĎßsubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßzPz. On the other hand, the noise increases exponentially at large zūĚĎßz, limiting our calculations to a certain range of zūĚĎßz values. An exact Fourier transformation is thus impossible. However, the contribution of the unknown long-tail distribution turns out to be bounded by physical constraints. For example, the Euclidean correlation functions must have a finite correlation length őĽ0‚ąĚPzproportional-tosubscriptūĚúÜ0subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßlambda0propto Pz in the coordinate space, which results in an exponential decay exp‚Ā°([‚ąíőĽ/őĽ0])delimited-ūĚúÜsubscriptūĚúÜ0exp[-lambda/lambda0] of the correlation functions at large őĽ=z‚ÄčPzūĚúÜūĚĎßsubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßlambda=zPz. We are thus able to make an extrapolation of the long-tail distribution with these constraints to reduce uncertainty from it. One example of the long-tail modeling iscitebib.bib47, H‚Äč(őĽ)‚ÜíőĽ‚Üí‚ąě(c1‚Äče‚ąíi‚ÄčőĽ/2(‚ąíi‚ÄčőĽ)d1+c2‚Äčei‚ÄčőĽ/2(i‚ÄčőĽ)d2)‚Äče‚ąíőĽ/őĽ0,‚ÜíūĚúÜ‚ÜíūĚźĽūĚúÜsubscriptūĚĎź1superscriptūĚĎíūĚĎĖūĚúÜ2superscriptūĚĎĖūĚúÜsubscriptūĚĎĎ1subscriptūĚĎź2superscriptūĚĎíūĚĎĖūĚúÜ2superscriptūĚĎĖūĚúÜsubscriptūĚĎĎ2superscriptūĚĎíūĚúÜsubscriptūĚúÜ0displaystyle H(lambda)xrightarrowlambdatoinftyleft(fracc1e-ilambda/2(-ilambda)d1+fracc2eilambda/2(ilambda)d2right)e-lambda/lambda0, where the parameterization inside the round brackets is motivated from the Regge behaviorcitebib.bib80 of the light-cone distribution near the endpoint regions. For symmetric pion DA, c1=c2subscriptūĚĎź1subscriptūĚĎź2c1=c2 and d1=d2subscriptūĚĎĎ1subscriptūĚĎĎ2d1=d2. For kaon DA, since the imaginary part is too small to be extrapolated, we model only the real part, and directly discrete Fourier transform the imaginary part truncated at the point when it starts to be consistent with zero. The model dependence of the extrapolation is estimated from three different fits of the long tail, by including or excluding the exponential decay e‚ąíőĽ/őĽ0superscriptūĚĎíūĚúÜsubscriptūĚúÜ0e-lambda/lambda0 (labeled ‚Äúexponential‚ÄĚ and ‚Äúalgebraic‚ÄĚ) in Eq. or by extrapolating from a larger zūĚĎßz (labeled ‚Äúlarge őĽūĚúÜlambda‚ÄĚ). The results are shown in Fig. . The long-tail extrapolation will eventually introduce a small systematic uncertainties to the light-cone DA near x=0.5ūĚĎ•0.5x=0.5 in our case. Compared to the pion quasi-DA, the more precise long-range correlation in kaon quasi-DA helps reduce such a systematic uncertainty.

content_sections.4 Matching to the light-cone DA 4.2 High: With the LaMET method, we have calculated the pion DA for a range of x‚ąą[0.25,0.75]ūĚĎ•0.250.75xin[0.25,0.75] for pion and x‚ąą[0.2,0.8]ūĚĎ•0.20.8xin[0.2,0.8] for kaon. Beyond this range, the perturbation theory no longer converges, and the power corrections in Eq. are increasingly important. Both effects prevent us from extracting the distribution closer to the endpoint with current hadron momentum Pz‚ąľ2similar-tosubscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß2Pzsim 2GeV. Although the xūĚĎ•x-dependence is not directly calculable in this region, it has been proposed to constrain the endpoint regions with the global information of DA, such as the lower moments or short distance correlations, extracted from SDFcitebib.bib58, bib.bib35. In coordinate space, the perturbation theory still works at z‚Č™őõQCD‚ąí1much-less-thanūĚĎßsuperscriptsubscriptőõQCD1zllLambdarm QCD-1, and the power corrections ūĚí™‚Äč(zn‚ÄčőõQCDn)ūĚí™superscriptūĚĎßūĚĎõsubscriptsuperscriptőõūĚĎõQCDO(znLambdanrm QCD) are also suppressed at short range. Thus the short distance correlations, along with the already determined mid-xūĚĎ•x distribution, can be utilized to better constrain the distribution in the endpoint regions, thus allowing us to roughly estimate the higher moments of DA. The Mellin moments ‚ü®őĺi‚ü©delimited-‚ü®‚ü©superscriptūĚúČūĚĎĖxii and Gegenbauer moments aisubscriptūĚĎéūĚĎĖai are calculated by ‚ü®őĺi‚ü©=‚ąę01ūĚĎĎx‚ÄčŌē‚Äč(x)‚Äč(2‚Äčx‚ąí1)i,delimited-‚ü®‚ü©superscriptūĚúČūĚĎĖsuperscriptsubscript01differential-dūĚĎ•italic-ŌēūĚĎ•superscript2ūĚĎ•1ūĚĎĖdisplaystylexii=int01dxphi(x)(2x-1)i, ai=‚ąę01ūĚĎĎx‚ÄčŌē‚Äč(x)‚ÄčCi3/2‚Äč[2‚Äčx‚ąí1]‚Äč4‚Äč(i+3/2)3‚Äč(i+1)‚Äč(i+2),subscriptūĚĎéūĚĎĖsuperscriptsubscript01differential-dūĚĎ•italic-ŌēūĚĎ•subscriptsuperscriptūĚź∂32ūĚĎĖdelimited-2ūĚĎ•14ūĚĎĖ323ūĚĎĖ1ūĚĎĖ2displaystyle ai=int01dxphi(x)C3/2i[2x-1]frac4(i+3/2)3(i+1)(i+2), where Ci3/2‚Äč[2‚Äčx‚ąí1]subscriptsuperscriptūĚź∂32ūĚĎĖdelimited-2ūĚĎ•1C3/2i[2x-1] are the Gegenbauer Polynomials. The results are summarized in Table. The second moment of our kaon DA is consistent with the local twist-2 operators by RQCD collaborationcitebib.bib19, but the second moment of our pion DA is much larger than their calculation. In a previous work on the moment analysis of the same pion DA datacitebib.bib25 with NLO matching, it is suggested that the power-law parameter mūĚĎöm lies between 0.20.20.2 and 0.320.320.32 with different analysis choices, consistent with our estimate m=0.31‚ÄčūĚĎö0.316m=0.31 in this calculation. Our second moment and fourth moment are also in 2ŌÉūĚúésigma agreement with their analysis of ‚ü®őĺ2‚ü©=0.287‚Äč‚Äčdelimited-‚ü®‚ü©superscriptūĚúČ20.28766xi2=0.287 and ‚ü®őĺ4‚ü©=0.14‚Äč‚Äčdelimited-‚ü®‚ü©superscriptūĚúČ40.1433xi4=0.14. Note that the fitted second Mellin moments ‚ü®őĺ2‚ü©delimited-‚ü®‚ü©superscriptūĚúČ2xi2 for the pion and kaon data in this approach agree with those fitted from RG-invariant ratios in Fig. . Such consistency in the lowest moments provide us more confidence about our estimate of higher moments. From both moments and the xūĚĎ•x-dependence, the pion DA is flatter than kaon, showing the non-negligible quark mass dependence of the meson DAs. The kaon is only slightly skewed as mūĚĎöm and nūĚĎõn are very close, and the first Mellin moment is consistent with zero. Higher moments estimated from this approach can be used as inputs to the pion and kaon phenomenology. An example is to calculate the pion transition form factor FŌÄ‚Äčő≥‚Äčő≥‚ąó‚Äč(Q2)subscriptūĚźĻūĚúčūĚõĺsuperscriptūĚõĺsuperscriptūĚĎĄ2Fpigammagamma*(Q2) at large Q2superscriptūĚĎĄ2Q2, which can be factorized as, F‚Äč(Q2)=2‚ÄčfŌÄ6‚ÄčQ2‚Äč‚ąę01Ōē‚Äč(x,őľ)‚ÄčT‚Äč(x,őľ,Q2),ūĚźĻsuperscriptūĚĎĄ22subscriptūĚĎďūĚúč6superscriptūĚĎĄ2superscriptsubscript01italic-ŌēūĚĎ•ūĚúáūĚĎáūĚĎ•ūĚúásuperscriptūĚĎĄ2displaystyle F(Q2)=fracsqrt2fpi6Q2int01phi(x,mu)T(x,mu,Q2), where the hard kernel T‚Äč(x,őľ,Q2)ūĚĎáūĚĎ•ūĚúásuperscriptūĚĎĄ2T(x,mu,Q2) has been calculated up to 2-loop order citebib.bib83. The convolution has also been formulated in terms of Gagenbauer coefficients with the hard coefficients cnsubscriptūĚĎźūĚĎõcn provided up to 2-loopcitebib.bib83, Q2‚ÄčF‚Äč(Q2)=2‚ÄčfŌÄ‚Äč‚ąĎn=0,2,‚Ä¶an‚Äč(őľ)‚Äčcn‚Äč(őľ,Q). superscriptūĚĎĄ2ūĚźĻsuperscriptūĚĎĄ22subscriptūĚĎďūĚúčsubscriptūĚĎõ02‚Ä¶subscriptūĚĎéūĚĎõūĚúásubscriptūĚĎźūĚĎõūĚúáūĚĎĄdisplaystyle Q2F(Q2)=sqrt2fpisumn=0,2,dotsan(mu)cn(mu,Q). We truncate the above equation at n=6ūĚĎõ6n=6 and show the result from our estimate of moments in Fig. . The scale variation is examined by choosing őľ‚ąąQ/2,Q,2‚ÄčQūĚúáūĚĎĄ2ūĚĎĄ2ūĚĎĄmuinQ/2,Q,2Q, with the corresponding an‚Äč(őľ)subscriptūĚĎéūĚĎõūĚúáan(mu) evolved from 222GeV to őľūĚúámu using their anomalous dimensions up to 3 loopscitebib.bib76. Our band is in good agreement with data from Bellecitebib.bib10 from Q2>4‚ÄčGeV2superscriptūĚĎĄ24superscriptGeV2Q2>4rmGeV2. Note that our estimation of higher moments are model-dependent. For our pion momentum Pz‚Čą1.8subscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎß1.8Pzapprox 1.8GeV, LaMET only provides reliable prediction in a range of x‚ąą[0.25,0.75]ūĚĎ•0.250.75xin[0.25,0.75]. In principle, this range is not reaching close enough to the endpoint for us to model the DA as a simple power-law function. Moreover, since the lattice spacing is not fine enough, we only have three data points of spatial correlation function at z‚Č§3‚ÄčaūĚĎß3ūĚĎézleq 3a that stays within the perturbative region, thus a complicated model could hardly be determined by this approach. To provide a more reliable estimate for the overall shape and higher moments of DA, we should include more calculations on finer lattice spacings and with higher hadron momenta.
content_sections.5 Conclusion: In this work, we present a lattice QCD calculation of the xūĚĎ•x-dependent pion and kaon DAs in the framework of LaMET. This calculation is performed on a fine lattice of a=0.076ūĚĎé0.076a=0.076fm at physical pion mass, and has used the state-of-the-art analysis methods, including the recently developed resummation methods of small-momentum logarithms. With the pion boosted to 1.81.81.8GeV and kaon boosted to 2.32.32.3GeV, we are able to calculate a range of x‚ąą[x0,1‚ąíx0]ūĚĎ•subscriptūĚĎ•01subscriptūĚĎ•0xin[x0,1-x0] with x0=0.25subscriptūĚĎ•00.25x0=0.25 for pion and x0=0.2subscriptūĚĎ•00.2x0=0.2 for kaon with systematics under control. Beyond this range, the perturbation theory and the power-expansion of the factorization theorem are no longer reliable, thus the DA cannot be directly accessed. Our results suggest the pion DA is flatter than the kaon DA, and the asymmetry in the kaon DA is small. Using complementarity, we estimate higher moments of the pion and kaon DAs by combining our calculation with short-distance factorization. The second moments are consistent with the OPE fits to the RG-invariant ratios of the matrix elements.

all_papers.title: Factorization connecting continuum and lattice TMDs
all_papers.abstract:
Transverse-momentum-dependent parton distribution functions (TMDs) can be
studied from first principles by a perturbative matching onto
lattice-calculable quantities: so-called lattice TMDs, which are a class of
equal-time correlators that includes quasi-TMDs and TMDs in the
Lorentz-invariant approach. We introduce a general correlator that includes as
special cases these two Lattice TMDs and continuum TMDs, like the Collins
scheme. Then, to facilitate the derivation of a factorization relation between
lattice and continuum TMDs, we construct a new scheme, the Large Rapidity (LR)
scheme, intermediate between the Collins and quasi-TMDs. The LR and Collins
schemes differ only by an order of limits, and can be matched onto one another
by a multiplicative kernel. We show that this same matching also holds between
quasi and Collins TMDs, which enables us to prove a factorization relation
between these quantities to all orders in $\alpha_s$. Our results imply that
there is no mixing between various quark flavors or gluons when matching
Collins and quasi TMDs, making the lattice calculation of individual flavors
and gluon TMDs easier than anticipated. We cross-check these results explicitly
at one loop and discuss implications for other physical-to-lattice scheme
factorizations.

sections.44: Ji, Parton Physics on a Euclidean Lattice, Phys

qualitative: Large Momentum Effective Theory (LaMET) provides a framework for defining quasi-observables, such as quasi-TMDs, which are equal-time correlators calculable on the lattice by using boosted hadron states with large momentum.

Quasi-TMDs are defined using spacelike Wilson line paths and are related to physical continuum TMDs through a perturbative factorization formula, enabling first-principles lattice calculations of transverse-momentum-dependent parton distributions.

The LaMET approach allows extraction of the nonperturbative Collins-Soper evolution kernel from ratios of quasi-TMDs at different hadron momenta, providing a way to access the full TMD evolution without direct light-cone calculations.

Lattice TMDs, including quasi-TMDs and those in the Lorentz-invariant MHENS scheme, are connected to physical continuum TMDs (e.g., in the Collins scheme) via factorization relations that involve perturbative matching kernels.

The paper introduces the Large Rapidity (LR) scheme as an intermediate framework between the Collins and quasi-TMDs, differing only by the order of limits, which facilitates the derivation of all-order factorization relations.
There is no flavor or gluon mixing in the matching between Collins and quasi-TMDs, simplifying the lattice extraction of individual quark flavor and gluon TMDs.
The factorization between lattice and continuum TMDs holds to all orders in őĪs, and includes full treatment of lattice renormalization, soft function subtractions, and finite Wilson line lengths.
quantitative: The factorization relation between quasi-TMDs and Collins TMDs is proven to all orders in őĪs, with a multiplicative matching kernel connecting the LR and Collins schemes.
The matching between quasi-TMDs and Collins TMDs is given by a multiplicative kernel that is independent of flavor and gluon mixing, implying no cross-flavor or gluon contributions in the matching.
The paper explicitly verifies the factorization at one-loop order, confirming the all-order structure.
The Wilson line in the quasi-TMD is finite in length, and the factorization accounts for this finite length, which is crucial for lattice applications.
The soft function subtraction and lattice renormalization are explicitly included in the factorization framework, ensuring consistency with lattice QCD calculations.

authors: Markus A. Ebert, Stella T. Schindler, Iain W. Stewart and Yong Zhao

content_sections.1 Introduction: Nuclear and particle physicists have striven to ascertain the full three-dimensional structure of hadrons for decades, through a combination of experimental measurements and their theoretical description via parton distribution functions (PDFs). The study of PDFs in the longitudinal momentum direction has reached a high level of maturity within many frameworks, such as global fitting effortscitebib.bib1, bib.bib2, bib.bib3, bib.bib4, bib.bib5, bib.bib6, bib.bib7, bib.bib8, lattice QCD calculations using moments citebib.bib9, bib.bib10, bib.bib11, quasi-distributions in large-momentum effective theory (LaMET)citebib.bib12, bib.bib13, bib.bib14, and various other techniques citebib.bib15, bib.bib16, bib.bib17, bib.bib18, bib.bib19, bib.bib20, bib.bib21, bib.bib22. A major thrust of current research is to extend this progress to transverse-momentum-dependent PDFs (TMDs). In principle, TMDs can be extracted from experiments using global fits citebib.bib23, bib.bib24, but due to limited data these extractions have not yet yielded the same precision as PDFs. However, ongoing and planned experiments such as the Electron-Ion Collidercitebib.bib25, bib.bib26 will have enhanced capabilities to probe TMDs, and it is important to complement them with theoretical insight. TMDs are intrinsically nonperturbative at small transverse momenta and so lattice QCD provides the only practical method for their first-principles calculation. Unfortunately, TMDs are defined along nonlocal lightlike or close-to-lightlike Wilson line paths, which depend explicitly on a real-valued time variable and thus lie outside the reach of current lattice techniques due to a sign problem, the speculated non-deterministic non-polynomial hard issue of numerically averaging over complex weights in a Euclidean path integral. To circumvent this obstacle, one can construct equal-time correlators that are calculable on the lattice, which we collectively dub ‚ÄúLattice TMDs‚ÄĚ. We can then extract information from lattice calculations by relating a lattice TMD to a physical continuum TMD (that appear in cross sections) through a so-called factorization formula, which forms the focus of this paper. There exist several approaches for defining continuum and lattice TMDs. The first lattice TMD studies used the Lorentz-invariant approach of refs. citebib.bib27, bib.bib28, bib.bib29, bib.bib30, bib.bib31, bib.bib32, which we refer to as the Musch-H√§gler-Engelhardt-Negele-Sch√§fer (MHENS) scheme. This approach defines TMDs which are calculated with lattice QCD using spacelike Wilson line paths, and then uses Lorentz invariance to relate them to the path considered for the continuum TMD definitions, which is spacelike but close to the light-cone. This method has so far primarily been used to calculate ratios of physical TMD moments. Later on, LaMET motivated the study of quasi-TMDscitebib.bib33, bib.bib34, bib.bib35, bib.bib36, bib.bib37, bib.bib38, bib.bib39, bib.bib40, bib.bib41, bib.bib42, which are Euclidean distributions defined using boosted hadron matrix elements of somewhat different spacelike Wilson line paths. Since the quasi-TMD obeys the Collins-Soper evolutioncitebib.bib33, one can extract the nonperturbative Collins-Soper (CS) evolution of TMDs using ratios of quasi-TMDs at different hadron momentacitebib.bib35, and first lattice results were obtained in refs. citebib.bib43, bib.bib44, bib.bib45, bib.bib46, bib.bib47, bib.bib48. Methods to calculate the so-called soft function have also been proposed citebib.bib38 and implemented on the lattice citebib.bib46, bib.bib47. To relate lattice TMDs and physical continuum TMDs one should derive a factorization formula that demonstrates that these TMDs agree in the infrared, and perhaps differ in the ultraviolet by perturbative matching coefficientscitebib.bib33, bib.bib34, bib.bib36, bib.bib39, bib.bib41, bib.bib49. In this work, we set up a unified notational framework for lattice and continuum TMDs and derive the factorization formula between the physical Collins TMD and quasi-TMD to all orders in őĪssubscriptūĚõľūĚĎ†alphas, for both quark and gluon TMDs. Up until now, no lattice-to-continuum factorization formlula has been proven, but a matching between the quasi-TMD and Collins-TMD for the non-singlet quark case has been verified at one-loop order.1 In ref. citebib.bib39 arguments for the factorization based on an analysis of leading regions were suggested. The presentation of such an analysis will be an important complement to the proof given here. Ref. citebib.bib49 analyzes factorization for lattice-friendly correlators. The transverse distribution that they study there is not the same as the quasi-TMD in refs. citebib.bib33, bib.bib34, bib.bib36, bib.bib39, bib.bib41 and this work. In our analysis here we also fully account for lattice renormalization, soft function subtractions, and finite Wilson line lengths; items that are important to account for in analyses for lattice-QCD-friendly TMDs.
content_sections.1 Introduction 1.1 Statement of factoriz: Factorization formulae that relate the non-singlet quark quasi-TMD and physical TMD have been proposed in refs. citebib.bib35, bib.bib36, bib.bib38, bib.bib39. (1.1) Here ns=u‚ąídnsūĚĎĘūĚĎĎrm ns=u-d for non-singlet flavor, and CnssubscriptūĚź∂nsCrm ns is a perturbative matching coefficient which does not depend on spincitebib.bib40, bib.bib49. The quasi-TMD depends on the hadron momentum PzsuperscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßtildePz, while the TMD depends on the CS scale ő∂ūĚúĀzeta; also, ő≥ő∂qsuperscriptsubscriptūĚõĺūĚúĀūĚĎěgammazetaq is the anomalous dimension for ő∂ūĚúĀzeta-evolution, which is often referred to as the CS kernelcitebib.bib50, bib.bib51, bib.bib52. Since we are interested in nonperturbative bT‚ąľőõQCD‚ąí1similar-tosubscriptūĚĎŹūĚĎásuperscriptsubscriptőõQCD1bTsimLambdaQCD-1, for the proper quasi-TMD fnssubscriptūĚĎďnstildefrm ns we must have gSq=1superscriptsubscriptūĚĎĒūĚĎÜūĚĎě1gSq=1, while for the naive quasi-TMD fnsnaivesuperscriptsubscriptūĚĎďnsnaivefrm nsrm naive the function gSq‚Äč(bT,őľ)superscriptsubscriptūĚĎĒūĚĎÜūĚĎěsubscriptūĚĎŹūĚĎáūĚúágSq(bT,mu) is nonperturbative and arises from using a naive quasi-soft function SnaiveRsubscriptsuperscriptūĚĎÜūĚĎÖnaivetildeSRrm naive, but can be calculated from the reduced soft function with the methods proposed inref. citebib.bib39. The definition of the proper quasi-TMD we use here is not the same as earlier literature, see for more details. This factorization is valid at large but finite PzsuperscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßtildePz, with power corrections suppressed by the parton momentum x‚ÄčPzūĚĎ•superscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßxtildePz as shown in eq. . In this work, we prove eq. at all orders in őĪssubscriptūĚõľūĚĎ†alphas and generalize it to the quasi-TMDs of all partons iūĚĎĖi, including light quark flavors (u,d,s)ūĚĎĘūĚĎĎūĚĎ†(u,d,s) and gluons. Two key results of this work are that eq. holds for both quark and gluon quasi-TMDs, and there is no mixing between the quark and gluon channels or quarks of different flavors. This differs from the case of the factorizations of quark and gluon longitudinal quasi-PDFscitebib.bib54. The matching coefficient CisubscriptūĚź∂ūĚĎĖCi takes different values for the two different cases i=q,gūĚĎĖūĚĎěūĚĎĒi=q,,g, but is independent of quark flavor and spin.
content_sections.1 Introduction 1.2 Strategy for proving: We begin by developing a unified notational framework that is applicable to both lattice and continuum TMDs. This framework brings to the forefront the common underlying structural features of different TMD schemes, allowing one to more easily construct factorization formulae relating them to one another. We can write every lattice and continuum TMD as the product of a proton matrix element (beam function), vacuum matrix element (soft factor), and appropriate renormalization factors. In the cases studied in the literature, we show that the beam function can be expressed as the Fourier transform of a generic, common correlator ő©ő©Omega. Each scheme manifests as a special case of the correlator, encoded by a choice of ő©ő©Omega‚Äôs arguments and of the order in which we take parameter limits needed for proper TMD regularization and renormalization. More details are provided later, and we summarize the choices needed for various schemes in Tables and . Having a unified notational framework is useful for constructing relationships between continuum TMD schemes that can be connected to physical observables, with schemes that can be computed on the lattice. From the structure of the correlator ő©ő©Omega, we observe that the Collins and quasi-TMDs are closely related. To relate these schemes we begin by constructing a new TMD scheme that is intermediate between the quasi and Collins TMDs: the Large-Rapidity (LR) scheme. The LR scheme uses the same ingredients as the Collins scheme, but performs UV renormalization at large but finite Wilson line rapidities. Using Lorentz invariance, we show that the quasi- and LR scheme TMDs are equivalent, up to terms suppressed at large proton rapidity. We then show that we can relate the LR and Collins TMDs simply by a perturbative matching kernel, which is flavor-diagonal and spin-independent for both quarks and gluons. Combining the quasi-to-LR and LR-to-Collins relations leads to eq. . We summarize relationships between a number of common TMD schemes in . This proof is a beautiful application of the fundamental principle underlying LaMETcitebib.bib12, bib.bib13, bib.bib14: partons encode the internal degrees of freedom of a highly energetic hadron, with the hadron momentum limit Pz‚Üí‚ąě‚ÜísuperscriptūĚĎÉūĚĎßPztoinfty taken prior to UV renormalization. In contrast, on the lattice one must carry out UV renormalization prior to the large-momentum limit. This different order of limits in an asymptotically free theory like QCD induces a nontrivial matching kernel between a parton observable and its corresponding lattice construction. Peculiarly, in TMDs this noncommutativity of orders of limits appears naturally, since at intermediate steps of the calculation one must regulate so-called rapidity divergencescitebib.bib53, bib.bib50, bib.bib55, bib.bib56, bib.bib57, bib.bib58, bib.bib59, bib.bib60, bib.bib61, bib.bib62, bib.bib63, bib.bib64, usually by choosing to deviate from lightcone kinematics or by introducing an additional regulator on the lightcone. These divergences cancel when combining the hadronic and soft matrix elements, allowing one to take the lightcone limit (or infinite rapidity-regulator limit), but also forcing one to decide whether to take this limit before or after UV renormalization. Many schemes for constructing TMDs take the lightcone limit first, e. g. the Collins schemecitebib.bib53; others, such as the Ji-Ma-Yuan (JMY) scheme, do the oppositecitebib.bib56. Once again, exchanging these orders of limits is a UV effect, and thus induces a nontrivial matching relation between Collins and JMY TMDs. This paper is structured as follows: introduces our new notational framework for defining TMDs. We then provide an overview of the definitions of common physical and lattice TMD schemes. presents the proof of the factorization statement in eq. . We then discuss the physical implications of this proof, in particular the lack of flavor mixing in the matching and utility for calculating ratios of TMDs. We also confirm the momentum evolution RGE equation for the hard matching coefficient, and give a complete solution for it with next-to-leading-logarithmic resummation. From our proof, we also gain intuition for factorization relations between other lattice and continuum TMD schemes, in particular for the MHENS and Collins schemes which differ due to the presence of Wilson line cusp angles in the former that are not in the latter, and by the need for different soft factors in these two TMDs. We make concluding remarks and outline future directions in . In the appendices, we verify our quasi-to-Collins factorization results analytically at one-loop order, present a one-loop comparison of the continuum JMY and Collins TMDs, and also discuss Wilson line self-energies.

(2.2) Here, ŌÉ0subscriptūĚúé0sigma0 is the Born cross-section; the sum runs over all parton flavors i,jūĚĎĖūĚĎói,j contributing to the Born process i‚Äčj‚ÜíL‚ÜíūĚĎĖūĚĎóūĚźŅijto L; Hi‚ÄčjsubscriptūĚźĽūĚĎĖūĚĎóHij is the hard function, which encodes virtual corrections to the Born process; and fi/hsubscriptūĚĎďūĚĎĖ‚Ąéfi/h are the TMDs, functions which describe the dynamics of partonic quarks and gluons inside the parent hadron h‚Ąéh. A struck hadron carries a fraction x1,2=Q‚Äče¬ĪY/EcmsubscriptūĚĎ•12ūĚĎĄsuperscriptūĚĎíplus-or-minusūĚĎĆsubscriptūĚźłcmx1,2=Qepm Y/Erm cm of its parent hadron‚Äôs longitudinal momentum, with Ecm=(P1+P2)2subscriptūĚźłcmsuperscriptsubscriptūĚĎÉ1subscriptūĚĎÉ22Erm cm=sqrt(P1+P2)2 the center-of-mass energy of the incoming hadrons. The ellipses in eq. denote additional parameters related to UV and rapidity renormalization, whose precise forms are scheme dependent. Note that we suppress indices related to spin-dependent processes and contributions. The literature is rife with schemes for defining TMDs, each of which has different strengths for different types of calculations. This section reviews schemes relevant for lattice studies; in particular, we only discuss schemes based on off-lightcone Wilson lines. Schemes with intrinsically lightlike Wilson linescitebib.bib65, bib.bib58, bib.bib62, bib.bib60, bib.bib63, bib.bib64 are not accessible on a Euclidean lattice, but many are equivalent to the Collins scheme once limits needed to obtain TMD PDFs are taken; see refs. citebib.bib36, bib.bib66 for an overview. Because each scheme in the literature employs its own conventions and notation, in we begin by introducing new unified TMD Lorentz-invariant correlators for which all schemes follow as special cases. Then, we provide definitions of physical and lattice schemes in sections and , respectively.

These matrix elements involve open and closed staple-shaped Wilson lines, for which we develop a generic notation. In the quark correlator, őďőďGamma denotes a generic Dirac structure, while for the gluon correlator őľ,őĹ,ŌĀ,ūĚúáūĚúąūĚúĆmu,,nu,,rho, and ŌÉūĚúésigma are Lorentz indices. (2.11) None of the TMD schemes we study in this paper contains a vector őīőľsuperscriptūĚõŅūĚúádeltamu that is linearly independent of bőľ,Pőľ,superscriptūĚĎŹūĚúásuperscriptūĚĎÉūĚúábmu,Pmu, and ő∑‚ÄčvőľūĚúāsuperscriptūĚĎ£ūĚúáeta vmu; thus, these schemes have six independent Lorentz invariants. However, the quasi and MHENS TMDs do not follow from the same correlator defined with six invariants, since they fix őīőľsuperscriptūĚõŅūĚúádeltamu in different ways, as we will see below. Hence, even if the first six invariants in eq. are fixed to be the same, the two approaches have different values for the last four invariants, and thus the quasi- and MHENS TMDs belong to distinct schemes.

JMY gluon TMDs are not explicitly defined in the literature; nonetheless, one can define them in an analogous manner to the quark TMDs through eq. .Finally, we introduce a new continuum TMD scheme, the LR scheme. The LR scheme uses the same beam and soft functions as the Collins scheme, but a different order of carrying out UV renormalization and approaching the lightcone. Specifically, fi/hLR‚Äč(x,b‚ÜíT,őľ,ő∂,yP‚ąíyB)=lim‚ąíyB‚Čę1limŌĶ‚Üí0ZuvLR‚Äč(ŌĶ,őľ,yn‚ąíyB)‚ÄčBi/hC‚Äč(x,b‚ÜíT,ŌĶ,yP‚ąíyB)SCR‚Äč(bT,ŌĶ,2‚Äčyn,2‚ÄčyB),subscriptsuperscriptūĚĎďLRūĚĎĖ‚ĄéūĚĎ•subscript‚ÜíūĚĎŹūĚĎáūĚúáūĚúĀsubscriptūĚĎ¶ūĚĎÉsubscriptūĚĎ¶ūĚźĶsubscriptmuch-greater-thansubscriptūĚĎ¶ūĚźĶ1subscript‚Üíitalic-ŌĶ0superscriptsubscriptūĚĎćuvLRitalic-ŌĶūĚúásubscriptūĚĎ¶ūĚĎõsubscriptūĚĎ¶ūĚźĶsubscriptsuperscriptūĚźĶūĚź∂ūĚĎĖ‚ĄéūĚĎ•subscript‚ÜíūĚĎŹūĚĎáitalic-ŌĶsubscriptūĚĎ¶ūĚĎÉsubscriptūĚĎ¶ūĚźĶsuperscriptsubscriptūĚĎÜūĚź∂ūĚĎÖsubscriptūĚĎŹūĚĎáitalic-ŌĶ2subscriptūĚĎ¶ūĚĎõ2subscriptūĚĎ¶ūĚźĶdisplaystyle fLRi/h(x,vecbT,mu,zeta,yP-yB)=limbeginsubarrayc-yBgg 1endsubarray,limepsilonto 0,ZuvLR(epsilon,mu,yn-yB)fracBCi/h(x,vecbT,epsilon,yP-yB)sqrtSCR(bT,epsilon,2yn,2yB),, (2.26) where ŌĶ‚Üí0‚Üíitalic-ŌĶ0epsilonto 0 implements UV renormalization. The UV counterterm ZuvLRsuperscriptsubscriptūĚĎćuvLRZrm uvLR does not depend on the rapidities in the beam function because the beam function does not involve nontrivial cusp angles, and the renormalization of its staple-shaped Wilson line and quark operators are rapidity-independent. It does however explicitly depend on the Wilson line rapidity difference yn‚ąíyBsubscriptūĚĎ¶ūĚĎõsubscriptūĚĎ¶ūĚźĶyn-yB through the renormalization of the soft function. In particular, the UV renormalization constant ZuvLRsuperscriptsubscriptūĚĎćuvLRZrm uvLR is the product of those of the bare beam function and soft factor. As will be shown in , the bare beam function at large, but finite, (‚ąíyB)subscriptūĚĎ¶ūĚźĶ(-yB) is equal to the quasi-beam function in a hadron state with momentum given by yP‚ąíyBsubscriptūĚĎ¶ūĚĎÉsubscriptūĚĎ¶ūĚźĶyP-yB. Using the auxiliary field formalism of the Wilson lines, one can showcitebib.bib71, bib.bib72, bib.bib37, bib.bib73 that renormalization of the quasi-beam function is multiplicative in coordinate space and independent of the external hadron momentum. Therefore, ZuvLRsuperscriptsubscriptūĚĎćuvLRZrm uvLR is independent of yP‚ąíyBsubscriptūĚĎ¶ūĚĎÉsubscriptūĚĎ¶ūĚźĶyP-yB, and only depends on yn‚ąíyBsubscriptūĚĎ¶ūĚĎõsubscriptūĚĎ¶ūĚźĶyn-yB due to the renormalization of the soft factor. This should be contrasted with the Collins scheme, where ZuvCsuperscriptsubscriptūĚĎćuvCZrm uvrm C depends on ő∂‚ąĚe2‚Äč(yP‚ąíyn)proportional-toūĚúĀsuperscriptūĚĎí2subscriptūĚĎ¶ūĚĎÉsubscriptūĚĎ¶ūĚĎõzetapropto e2(yP-yn), cf. eq. . We note that the renormalized LR scheme TMD depends on yP‚ąíyBsubscriptūĚĎ¶ūĚĎÉsubscriptūĚĎ¶ūĚźĶyP-yB; hence, the limit ‚ąíyB‚Čę1much-greater-thansubscriptūĚĎ¶ūĚźĶ1-yBgg 1 is to be understood as taking (‚ąíyB)subscriptūĚĎ¶ūĚźĶ(-yB) large but finite instead of taking the limit yB‚Üí‚ąí‚ąě‚ÜísubscriptūĚĎ¶ūĚźĶyBto-infty. The LR and Collins TMDs use the same ő∂=(2‚Äčx‚Äčmh‚ÄčeyP‚ąíyn)2ūĚúĀsuperscript2ūĚĎ•subscriptūĚĎö‚ĄésuperscriptūĚĎísubscriptūĚĎ¶ūĚĎÉsubscriptūĚĎ¶ūĚĎõ2zeta=(2xmheyP-yn)2 to encode ynsubscriptūĚĎ¶ūĚĎõyn dependence. We can also view the LR scheme as the JMY scheme defined with spacelike instead of timelike Wilson lines. See for further elaboration on and derivation of LR scheme properties.
content_sections.2 Definition of TMDs 2.3 Lattice TMD mat: Next, we provide a brief overview of TMD functions that are amenable to calculation using lattice QCD. Unlike the continuum schemes in , lattice TMDs are defined using finite-length Wilson lines. We can obtain their matrix elements from paths involving equal-time spacelike Wilson lines.Quasi-TMDs are objects that share the same infrared physics as TMDs, but have finite-length spacelike Wilson lines and are computable on the lattice citebib.bib34, bib.bib36. Therefore, the cusp angles in the quasi-beam function are always trivially ŌÄ/2ūĚúč2pi/2, so that the UV renormalization factor is independent of bzsuperscriptūĚĎŹūĚĎßtildebz and can be pulled out of the Fourier integral in eq. citebib.bib37. This is a key difference to the MHENS scheme discussed in . The construction of the quasi-beam function in eq. is guided by the observation that by boosting the operator and taking |ő∑|‚Üí‚ąě‚ÜíūĚúā|tildeeta|toinfty, one recovers eq. . In contrast, the soft function depends on two almost-lightlike directions and thus cannot be obtained by boosting an equal-time operatorcitebib.bib36; several potential (quasi-)soft functions have been proposedcitebib.bib33, bib.bib34, bib.bib36 despite the fact that these proposals cannot recover eq. under any Lorentz boost.
